title: docs-site-content-ownership-rotations-and-support-escalation-2027
preview:
OVERVIEW

Purpose:
This runbook defines the operating model for the Developer Docs site (doc.redwood.internal), the canary content publishing flow, content-owner rotation, and the canonical support-escalation patterns for the top SDK/API issues we see in production. The goal is to reduce mean time to resolution for doc-driven developer problems, keep quickstarts accurate, and create deterministic rollback and remediation paths for site incidents.

Audience:
- Docs authors and maintainers (DX writers, SDK engineers)
- Oncall docs rotation owners
- Developer Support and Solutions Engineering
- Platform and SRE engineers responsible for docs hosting and CDN

GOALS & SUCCESS METRICS
- Reduce doc-related support tickets by 40% for SDK onboarding flows in 90 days.
- Canary publish failure rate < 1% per release.
- Time-to-first-fix for canonical SDK bugs documented in quickstarts: <= 4 hours for P0, <= 24 hours for P1.

OWNERSHIP, ROTATION, AND ESCALATION
- Primary owner: eng-developer-experience/docs-site (rotation based).
- Rotation window: 2 weeks per owner. Primary rota owners are two engineers/writers who share handoff responsibilities.
- Escalation path: owner -> docs-tech lead -> eng-platform -> eng-sre -> oncall infra lead.

OWNERSHIP TABLE (text table)
| Role                     | Team                     | Responsibilities                                  | Oncall Rota |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|------------|
| Docs Primary Owner       | eng-developer-experience | Merge content, validate snippets, run canary      | 2-week     |
| Canary Gatekeeper        | eng-platform             | Approve canary infra changes, validate builds     | oncall     |
| Support Escalation Lead  | customer-success         | Triage doc-driven tickets, coordinate fixes       | n/a        |

CANARY PUBLISH WORKFLOW (high level)
1) Author creates a content branch and runs local lint + snippet validation (docs-lint).
2) Create a pull request with the label "docs/canary" and add the canary checklist template.
3) CI runs: lint, static link-check, snippet execution (local runtime mocks), minimal smoke test against canary CDN staging.
4) Canary Gatekeeper performs manual validation of 2 representative quickstarts (SDK JavaScript and Python) using the staging site.
5) If approved, merge to canary branch. Canary is exposed to 2% of internal traffic for 24 hours.
6) Telemetry signals evaluated (error rate, snippet execution failures, spike in support hits). If green, promote to main release.

CANARY CHECKLIST (must be present in PR to proceed)
- [ ] docs-lint: pass
- [ ] snippet-exec: local run success (python/js snippets)
- [ ] link-check: no 4xxs
- [ ] author verified steps on staging
- [ ] owner added to PR

DOCS-SITE RELEASE GATE (pre-merge required items)
- smoke: average page render < 250ms on staging (p50)
- snippet success rate = 100% on the two validated quickstarts
- telemetry: no new 5xx errors in staging APIs

TOP SUPPORT ISSUES AND CANONICAL FIXES
The following list reflects recurring support categories for SDK/Docs integration and the standardized remediation steps to be used by Support and Docs owners. Each issue includes rapid diagnosis steps and a canonical patch pattern.

1) SDK authentication errors for new users ("401: invalid api key")
- Symptoms: new customers fail first API call from quickstart.
- Diagnosis: check snippet uses REDWOOD_API_KEY vs REDWOOD_KEY; confirm header format: Authorization: Bearer <key>.
- Canonical fix: update quickstart snippets to use environment variable REDWOOD_API_KEY and add a short note: "Ensure you have copied the key from Console > API keys and pin to repo secrets."
- Patch example: replace header code block in SDK quickstart with guarded snippet:

  // Example (JavaScript)
  const key = process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY || process.env.REDWOOD_KEY;
  if (!key) throw new Error('missing REDWOOD_API_KEY');

2) Streaming responses hang in local sandbox (SDK stream resumes but never completes)
- Symptoms: SDK returns partial tokens then stops.
- Diagnosis: check client timeout settings, CDN chunking. Use curl to verify server stream at staging URL.
- Canonical fix: add explicit client-side stream resume instructions and set default timeout to 90s in quickstarts.

3) Mismatch between SDK-generated request shape and runtime ("bad request: invalid field 'tools'")
- Symptoms: API rejects requests that worked previously.
- Diagnosis: run sample payload against local mirror endpoint (mirror: /v1/debug/mirror). Compare JSON schema.
- Canonical fix: update API examples to include the runtime-compatible wrapper, and include both compact and full examples.

4) Embeddings dimensionality confusion (users expect 1536, get 1024)
- Symptoms: downstream similarity search yields poor scores.
- Diagnosis: verify model specified in quickstart and confirm embedding vector length in returned payload.
- Canonical fix: add explicit subheading "Model/Embedding matrix" in quickstarts that lists popular model names and expected dimensions.

5) Rate-limited quickstarts in CI (flaky integration tests)
- Symptoms: flaky CI due to api-rate-limits during test runs.
- Diagnosis: check CI logs for 429 responses; verify whether tests use shared API keys.
- Canonical fix: provide a CI-friendly mock harness and sample -- use ENV:CI=true to enable mock responses in quickstarts.

6) Local credential rotation instructions outdated
- Symptoms: users cannot rotate keys using documented CLI.
- Diagnosis: verify CLI version and compare to Console UI.
- Canonical fix: replace CLI sequence with Console steps and include a single CLI command that uses the new --kms flag.

7) Incorrect timeout/backoff guidance for batch embedding jobs
- Symptoms: jobs time out systematically at 30s for larger batches.
- Diagnosis: check recommended batch_size and per-request timeout in quickstart.
- Canonical fix: update recommendations: batch_size <= 128, per-request timeout = 120s, and provide pseudocode for exponential backoff:

  backoff = min(60, 2**attempt + jitter)

8) Broken snippet due to dependency drift (npm package changed API)
- Symptoms: snippet fails to import or method names changed.
- Diagnosis: run install locally; reproduce error; check pinned versions.
- Canonical fix: pin minimal reproducible versions in quickstarts (package.json example) and create a short "dependency stability" note.

QUICKSTART TEMPLATE (recommended minimal contents)
- Title and one-line purpose
- Prereqs (SDK version, Node/Python, minimum OS)
- Setup (API key, env var, CLI install)
- Minimal code example (request and expected response)
- Troubleshooting section (top 3 local failures)
- Telemetry & metrics to check after running

EXAMPLE QUICKSTART SNIPPET (Python)
```python
# setup: pip install redwood-sdk==1.4.2
import os
from redwood import RedwoodClient
key = os.environ.get('REDWOOD_API_KEY')
client = RedwoodClient(api_key=key, timeout=90)
resp = client.generate("Write a 2-line haiku about latency")
print(resp.text)
```

API EXAMPLE (curl)
curl -X POST https://api.redwood.example/v1/generate 
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" 
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
  -d '{"model":"rwd-compact-1","input":"Summarize the rubric"}'

DOCS SITE OPERATIONS: CHECKLIST FOR RELEASES
- Validate internal links with link-checker
- Validate snippet-exec on macOS and Linux runners
- Confirm telemetry coverage for new pages (page_view, snippet_fail, page_error)
- Announce to #docs-release with link to canary and callout of areas that changed

ROLLBACK PROCEDURE (fast path)
1) If snippet_exec failures > 0.5% or support tickets spike > 30% in 1 hour, initiate rollback.
2) Owner triggers a CDN rollback using the tag from previous release:
   sh infra/cdn-rollback.sh --tag v<previous-tag>
3) Reopen PR, add hotfix for failing snippets, and re-run canary on staging.
4) Postmortem owner: the docs rotation owner for that window. Run blameless postmortem within 72 hours.

MONITORING & DASHBOARD (what to watch)
- Docs site page_view p50/p95 render time
- snippet_exec success rate (per-language)
- support: doc-related ticket counts and mean time to close
- search: top 10 0-result searches weekly

TEMPLATES & ARTIFACTS (links)
- Canary checklist template: /templates/docs/canary-checklist.md
- Quickstart template: /templates/docs/quickstart-minimal.md
- Snippet harness repo: git@repo:redwood/docs-snippet-harness.git

APPENDIX: EXAMPLE SNIPPET PINNED package.json (for node quickstart)
{
  "name": "redwood-quickstart-sample",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "dependencies": {
    "@redwood/sdk": "1.4.2"
  }
}

CONTACTS (primary on-call and backup)
- Primary: Maya Chen (mchen@redwood.internal)
- Backup: Ethan Park (epark@redwood.internal)

CHANGE LOG
- 2027-02-10: Initial publication (Maya Chen).
- 2027-03-01: Updated rollback thresholds and examples (Ethan Park).

END OF DOCUMENT

title: Docs site change propagation & developer onramp playbook
preview:
Summary:

This playbook defines how content changes (snippets, SDK quickstarts, API examples, runbooks) move from authoring to published docs on the public developer docs site and how the developer onramp is kept in sync with canonical fixes and top support issue updates. It codifies roles, automation, verification checks, rollback criteria, and telemetry to ensure we ship accurate, timely developer-facing content.

Scope & audience:
- Scope: content owned by eng-developer-experience (quickstarts, API examples, docs-site pages, canonical fixes). Does NOT replace product PRDs or private customer runbooks living under eng-private-deployments.
- Audience: docs authors, SDK owners, DX PMs, Developer Support, and Docs site SRE.

Definitions:
- Snippet: small code or config fragment included in multiple pages (examples, quickstarts).
- Golden trace: authoritative set of inputs and outputs used to validate an API example or sample app.
- Canonical fix: a vetted correction applied to docs after a support pattern is identified.

Roles & ownership (high level):
- Author: writes content and tests locally. Primary responsibility for correctness.
- Snippet owner: maintains a snippet library file; responds to breakages within 72 hours.
- Docs CI owner (team: eng-developer-experience/site): maintains docs pipeline and runbooks.
- Support liaison: triages incoming issue, recommends canonical fix, and opens PRs when urgent.

Ownership quick table (described):
- Service: snippet registry | Owner: SDK team | SLA: 72h for critical bugs
- Service: docs-pipeline CI | Owner: site SRE | SLA: 24h for CI outages
- Service: published docs | Owner: docs product manager | SLA: 5 business days for content updates in non-critical path

Publishing flow (end-to-end):
1. Authoring:
   - Create or update page in local markdown/snippet file. Use snippet IDs that match the registry (format: sdk-{lang}-{id}, e.g. sdk-py-auth-02).
   - Pre-commit hook runs: lint-md, snippet-validate, golden-trace-check.
2. Preview & review:
   - Push branch and open PR against docs-repo. CI generates a preview build and a preview URL. Command: `docs-pipeline preview --branch ${BRANCH}`
   - Add reviewers: at least one snippet owner and one domain SDK owner. Use the PR template which includes checklist (see Verification checklist).
3. Merge gating:
   - Passing CI and 2 approvals required. If change touches snippets used in published quickstarts, add `@site-sre` review.
4. Publish:
   - Merge triggers `docs-pipeline apply`. By default changes land in staging and are promoted to prod on a scheduled window (see Release windows).
   - For hotfixes (security, API-breaking), use `docs-pipeline apply --env=prod --hotfix --owner=${TEAM}`. Hotfixes bypass the scheduled promotion but require post-merge audit.

Snippet lifecycle & best practices:
- Single source-of-truth: store snippets in `docs/snippets/` in canonical format (header with metadata).
- Metadata required per snippet (describe in text): snippet_id, owner, languages, last_tested, golden_trace_id.
- Naming convention: `{component}-{lang}-{short}` (lowercase, dashes).
- Test coverage: snippets must be covered by at least one unit/integration golden trace or CI smoke test.

Example snippet header (YAML in file top; shown here as plain):
```yaml
# snippet: sdk-py-auth-02
# owner: sdk-python
# languages: python
# last_tested: 2027-02-01
# golden_trace: gt-auth-2027-01
```

Docs CI / automation (commands & policies):
- Preview: `docs-pipeline preview --branch ${BRANCH}` generates a unique preview URL and lazy-evaluates large snippets.
- Validate: `docs-pipeline validate --path docs/snippets --schema snippet-schema.yaml` runs snippet schema checks.
- Apply (staging): `docs-pipeline apply --env=staging --owner=${TEAM}`
- Promote (staging -> prod): automatic nightly promotion at 02:00 UTC unless hold label is present. For emergency promotion use `--manual` flag.

Sample CI job snippet (described):
- Job: snippet-validate
  - Runs: python scripts/snippet_validator.py --root docs/snippets
  - Produces: artifact list of changed snippet_ids and failing golden traces.

Golden trace & canonical fixes workflow:
1. Detect: Support files an issue or automated telemetry detects mismatch (e.g., example output drift).
2. Triage: Support liaison opens tracking issue and marks `impact:developer-experience`.
3. Reproduce: Author or on-call reproduces locally against golden-trace inputs.
4. Fix PR: Create PR that updates snippet/golden trace and includes test updates. Mark PR with `canonical-fix`.
5. Verification and merge: run CI, get approvals from snippet owner and Support liaison. For fixes that affect multiple quickstarts run `docs-pipeline preview --smoke` and validate sample matrix.
6. Postmortem: If the issue impacted >10 customers or was live >72 hours, create a short postmortem and link to PR.

Top support patterns and canonical fixes (examples):
- Pattern: Expired auth token in SDK quickstart causing 401s
  - Canonical fix: update example to show short-lived token exchange + note about token TTL. Add golden trace to assert new behavior.
- Pattern: Streaming API sample uses legacy param `stream_mode`
  - Canonical fix: update snippet to the current streaming API and add deprecation note with migration steps.
- Pattern: Embeddings dimensionality mismatch in example datasets
  - Canonical fix: add explicit schema validation in quickstart and provide a compatibility adapter snippet.

Rollback & hotpatch playbook (numbered):
1. If a production page introduces a functional error and a hotfix PR is not ready, immediately revert the merge via `docs-pipeline revert --commit ${SHA}`.
2. Notify: post in #docs-pipeline Slack channel with incident tag and link to revert.
3. Create emergency PR that includes corrected snippet + tests. Mark as `emergency-hotpatch` and request expedited approvals.
4. After merge, run post-deploy checks (see Verification checklist) and close incident with owner notes.

Verification checklist (before merging):
- [ ] Preview build URL accessible and renders without JS console errors.
- [ ] All snippet IDs referenced exist in `docs/snippets/` and validate against schema.
- [ ] Golden trace tests pass locally and in CI.
- [ ] If change touches quickstarts: run smoke integration (`docs-pipeline preview --smoke`) and confirm example app starts within 60s.
- [ ] PR includes update to changelog or release notes when removing/deprecating examples.

Release windows & SLAs (short):
- Normal content: automated nightly promotion to prod (02:00 UTC).
- Low-risk updates: minor content updates may be scheduled weekly.
- Hotfix SLA: docs-pipeline CI owner will respond within 24h to CI outages; snippet owners have 72h for critical fixes.

Telemetry & monitoring (what to track):
- Preview build success rate (target > 98% monthly).
- `snippet-validate` failure rate and top offending snippet IDs.
- Time-to-merge for canonical fixes (median target <= 4 business days).
- Support ticket count tagged `docs-issue` (reduce by 30% YoY).

Support escalation paths (how Support interacts with docs):
1. Support opens an internal issue with labels `impact:developer-experience` + `severity`.
2. Support liaison assigns to snippet owner and adds a suggested canonical fix draft (if known).
3. If not resolved in SLA, escalate to Docs Product Manager and schedule a triage sync.

FAQ (short):
Q: Who can mark a change as a hotfix?
A: The snippet owner or Docs Product Manager. Hotfixes require `site-sre` notification post-deploy.

Q: What counts as a canonical fix that needs postmortem?
A: Any change that: a) affected >10 paying customers, or b) caused a documented outage of a sample app for >72 hours.

Related links:
- Docs pipeline README: `/confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/docs-pipeline-readme`
- Snippet schema: `/confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/snippet-schema`
- Support triage runbook: `/confluence/eng-developer-experience/support-top-issues/support-triage-runbook`

Appendix: Quick commands reference (one-liners):
- Preview a branch: `docs-pipeline preview --branch feature/x-example`
- Validate snippets: `docs-pipeline validate --path docs/snippets`
- Apply staging: `docs-pipeline apply --env=staging --owner=sdk-python`
- Emergency revert: `docs-pipeline revert --commit ${SHA}`

Contact & ownership:
- Primary author: Marina Patel (marina@redwood.example.com)
- Docs CI owner: site-sre@redwood.example.com
- Support liaison: dev-support-leads@redwood.example.com

Document versioning: major edits should increment the header version and add a short summary line in the top of the page.

End of playbook.

```yaml
# snippet: sdk-py-auth-02
# owner: sdk-python
# languages: python
# last_tested: 2027-02-01
# golden_trace: gt-auth-2027-01
```

Docs CI / automation (commands & policies):
- Preview: `docs-pipeline preview --branch ${BRANCH}` generates a unique preview URL and lazy-evaluates large snippets.
- Validate: `docs-pipeline validate --path docs/snippets --schema snippet-schema.yaml` runs snippet schema checks.
- Apply (staging): `docs-pipeline apply --env=staging --owner=${TEAM}`
- Promote (staging -> prod): automatic nightly promotion at 02:00 UTC unless hold label is present. For emergency promotion use `--manual` flag.

Sample CI job snippet (described):
- Job: snippet-validate
  - Runs: python scripts/snippet_validator.py --root docs/snippets
  - Produces: artifact list of changed snippet_ids and failing golden traces.

Golden trace & canonical fixes workflow:
1. Detect: Support files an issue or automated telemetry detects mismatch (e.g., example output drift).
2. Triage: Support liaison opens tracking issue and marks `impact:developer-experience`.
3. Reproduce: Author or on-call reproduces locally against golden-trace inputs.
4. Fix PR: Create PR that updates snippet/golden trace and includes test updates. Mark PR with `canonical-fix`.
5. Verification and merge: run CI, get approvals from snippet owner and Support liaison. For fixes that affect multiple quickstarts run `docs-pipeline preview --smoke` and validate sample matrix.
6. Postmortem: If the issue impacted >10 customers or was live >72 hours, create a short postmortem and link to PR.

Top support patterns and canonical fixes (examples):
- Pattern: Expired auth token in SDK quickstart causing 401s
  - Canonical fix: update example to show short-lived token exchange + note about token TTL. Add golden trace to assert new behavior.
- Pattern: Streaming API sample uses legacy param `stream_mode`
  - Canonical fix: update snippet to the current streaming API and add deprecation note with migration steps.
- Pattern: Embeddings dimensionality mismatch in example datasets
  - Canonical fix: add explicit schema validation in quickstart and provide a compatibility adapter snippet.

Rollback & hotpatch playbook (numbered):
1. If a production page introduces a functional error and a hotfix PR is not ready, immediately revert the merge via `docs-pipeline revert --commit ${SHA}`.
2. Notify: post in #docs-pipeline Slack channel with incident tag and link to revert.
3. Create emergency PR that includes corrected snippet + tests. Mark as `emergency-hotpatch` and request expedited approvals.
4. After merge, run post-deploy checks (see Verification checklist) and close incident with owner notes.

Verification checklist (before merging):
- [ ] Preview build URL accessible and renders without JS console errors.
- [ ] All snippet IDs referenced exist in `docs/snippets/` and validate against schema.
- [ ] Golden trace tests pass locally and in CI.
- [ ] If change touches quickstarts: run smoke integration (`docs-pipeline preview --smoke`) and confirm example app starts within 60s.
- [ ] PR includes update to changelog or release notes when removing/deprecating examples.

Release windows & SLAs (short):
- Normal content: automated nightly promotion to prod (02:00 UTC).
- Low-risk updates: minor content updates may be scheduled weekly.
- Hotfix SLA: docs-pipeline CI owner will respond within 24h to CI outages; snippet owners have 72h for critical fixes.

Telemetry & monitoring (what to track):
- Preview build success rate (target > 98% monthly).
- `snippet-validate` failure rate and top offending snippet IDs.
- Time-to-merge for canonical fixes (median target <= 4 business days).
- Support ticket count tagged `docs-issue` (reduce by 30% YoY).

Support escalation paths (how Support interacts with docs):
1. Support opens an internal issue with labels `impact:developer-experience` + `severity`.
2. Support liaison assigns to snippet owner and adds a suggested canonical fix draft (if known).
3. If not resolved in SLA, escalate to Docs Product Manager and schedule a triage sync.

FAQ (short):
Q: Who can mark a change as a hotfix?
A: The snippet owner or Docs Product Manager. Hotfixes require `site-sre` notification post-deploy.

Q: What counts as a canonical fix that needs postmortem?
A: Any change that: a) affected >10 paying customers, or b) caused a documented outage of a sample app for >72 hours.

Related links:
- Docs pipeline README: `/confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/docs-pipeline-readme`
- Snippet schema: `/confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/snippet-schema`
- Support triage runbook: `/confluence/eng-developer-experience/support-top-issues/support-triage-runbook`

Appendix: Quick commands reference (one-liners):
- Preview a branch: `docs-pipeline preview --branch feature/x-example`
- Validate snippets: `docs-pipeline validate --path docs/snippets`
- Apply staging: `docs-pipeline apply --env=staging --owner=sdk-python`
- Emergency revert: `docs-pipeline revert --commit ${SHA}`

Contact & ownership:
- Primary author: Marina Patel (marina@redwood.example.com)
- Docs CI owner: site-sre@redwood.example.com
- Support liaison: dev-support-leads@redwood.example.com

Document versioning: major edits should increment the header version and add a short summary line in the top of the page.

End of playbook.

title: Polyglot adapter patterns, handshake, and telemetry mapping
preview:
Summary:

This page documents an opinionated, production-oriented pattern for building polyglot adapters and SDK shims that integrate client code (Node/Python/Go) with the Redwood inference runtime. The guidance focuses on three areas: reliable handshake and capability negotiation, consistent telemetry mapping for downstream observability, and a set of canonical fixes for the top support issues we see in adapter integrations.

Intended audience:
- SDK engineers building official Redwood client libraries
- Integration engineers implementing third-party or customer-side adapters
- Developer experience (DX) and support engineers who triage adapter-related incidents

Why this pattern exists:
- We routinely see fragmentation across language adapters (timeouts, retry semantics, telemetry labels) that increases incident MTTR and makes cross-language support expensive.
- A small common contract (handshake + telemetry schema + canonical retry rules) reduces ambiguity and enables unified dashboards, test harnesses, and golden-trace comparisons.

Design overview:
1) Lightweight handshake and capability negotiation (required)
- Purpose: ensure client, adapter, and runtime agree on supported features (streaming, function-calls, token-encoding, kv-cache).
- Contract: an idempotent HTTP POST to /v1/adapter-handshake with a small JSON payload. Example payload (pseudo):

  {
    "client_id": "my-product/1.3.0",
    "language": "python",
    "capabilities": ["streaming", "function-calls", "prefix-cache"]
  }

- Expected runtime response: 200 with fields: runtime_id, accepted_capabilities, recommended_timeouts_ms, telemetry_endpoint.
- Fallback: If handshake fails (5xx or timeout), adapter must fall back to the conservative compatibility mode (disable streaming, use sync fallback) and surface a WARN-level telemetry event.

2) Telemetry and event mapping (required)
- Goal: consistent labels across languages for traces, metrics, and logs so that the Redwood Console can correlate token-cost, latency, and error budgets.
- Minimal required telemetry fields to emit on every request:
  - trace.service = "adapter.<language>"
  - trace.client_id = client_id from handshake (if available)
  - metric.request_type = generation | embeddings | rerank
  - metric.tokens_input, metric.tokens_output (integers)
  - tag.model_variant = model-id:revision
  - tag.rollout_id = optional rollout label (can be empty)

- Telemetry schema table (copyable):

  | logical_field | event_type | example_value | notes |
  |---------------|------------|---------------|-------|
  | trace.service | trace | adapter.node | must be static per adapter binary |
  | trace.client_id | trace | my-app/2.0.1 | prefer semantic versioning |
  | metric.latency_ms | metric | 12 | p99 and p50 recorded at the adapter and runtime |
  | metric.tokens_input | metric | 24 | required for cost reconciliation |
  | error.kind | log | tokenization_error | map runtime codes to canonical names |
- Implementation notes: use OpenTelemetry conventions where possible; always include a short adapter.version semantic tag.

3) Retry and timeout recipe (recommended default)
- Default per-request policy:
  1. client_timeout = handshake.recommended_timeouts_ms.client_request || 30000ms
  2. adapter_socket_timeout = min(client_timeout, 60000ms)
  3. Retries: idempotent-only endpoints (health, handshake, embeddings) allow 2 retries with exponential backoff jitter (base 200ms). Generation requests: do not automatically retry on 4xx; on 5xx follow single retry if request_body_size < 64KB.

Procedural runbook: adapter integration quick test (3–5 minutes)
1) Start local runtime (dev mode): REDWOOD_RUNTIME=local ./start-runtime --dev
2) Send handshake: curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/v1/adapter-handshake -d '{"client_id":"acme-svc/0.1.0","language":"go","capabilities":["streaming"]}'
3) Confirm response contains "accepted_capabilities" and a telemetry_endpoint
4) Exercise a minimal generation call with streaming (or fallback) and assert telemetry events are received (see curl + otlp collector example below)

JS snippet (adapter-side, simplified):
```
// Js pseudo-code
const handshake = await fetch(`${runtime}/v1/adapter-handshake`, {method:'POST', body: JSON.stringify({client_id:'acme/1.0.0', language:'node', capabilities:['streaming']})});
const handshakeJson = await handshake.json();
const telemetryEndpoint = handshakeJson.telemetry_endpoint;
// On each request emit open-telemetry span with attributes from handshake
```

Python snippet (adapter-side, simplified):
```
# Python pseudo-code
resp = session.post(f"{runtime}/v1/adapter-handshake", json={"client_id":"acme/py/0.9.2","language":"python","capabilities":["function-calls"]})
meta = resp.json()
# set recommended_timeout = meta.get('recommended_timeouts_ms', {}).get('client_request', 30000)
```

Top support issues and canonical fixes (observed patterns)
- Symptom: Missing tokens_input/ tokens_output metrics in traces
  - Fix: ensure tokenizers are run inside the adapter boundary and emit metric.tokens_input before request is forwarded. Add unit test: verify metrics emitted for a sample prompt.
- Symptom: Streaming stalls at the adapter and runtime shows partial frames
  - Fix: validate that adapter streaming buffer is flushed after each chunk and that chunk size obeys handshake.recommended_timeouts_ms.chunk_window; add network keepalive (TCP) and backpressure signals.
- Symptom: Inconsistent error labels across languages (e.g., "400-tty" vs "bad_request")
  - Fix: map runtime error codes to the canonical error.kind names table below before emitting logs/telemetry.

Canonical error.kind mapping (table):
  | runtime_code | canonical_name | retryable |
  |--------------|----------------|-----------|
  | 400          | bad_request    | false     |
  | 413          | request_too_large | false |
  | 429          | rate_limited   | true      |
  | 500          | internal_error | true      |

Acceptance tests and CI checklist (required for SDK PRs)
- Automated handshake test that runs against a small emulated runtime and asserts accepted_capabilities and recommended_timeouts are reasonable.
- Telemetry smoke check: CI must assert that a sample request emits the required telemetry fields (trace.service, metric.tokens_input, metric.tokens_output).
- Golden-trace diff: run a quick generation with the CI runtime and assert no more than 3% deviation in token counts and that latency p50 < 200ms for unit-size prompts.

Operational ownership and escalation
- Owning teams: SDK authors own adapter code; eng-platform owns runtime handshake endpoints and telemetry schema.
- Pager: if handshake endpoint shows >1% 5xx for 15 minutes, page eng-platform oncall.

Appendix: Example OTLP collector config (minimal)
```
receivers:
  otlp:
    protocols:
      http: {}
exporters:
  logging:
service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      exporters: [logging]
```

References and follow-ups:
- Implement a small test harness in the SDK repo that spins up the emulated runtime and runs the handshake + telemetry smoke tests.
- Consider adding a server-side enforcement that rejects adapter requests without the required telemetry tags after a 6-month grace period.

Document history:
- 2026-11-12: Draft created by Asha Patel
- 2026-12-10: Added telemetry table and retry recipe (Ben Carter review)
- 2027-02-03: Finalized canonical error mappings and CI checklist (Lina Zhou review)

title: Runtime Adapter Playground: Authoring Kit, Telemetry Stubs, and Canary Templates
preview:
Overview

This document describes the Runtime Adapter Playground Authoring Kit — a lightweight, reproducible developer experience for building, testing, and canarying SDK adapters and runtime integrations against Redwood’s local sandbox. The kit bundles:

- opinionated repo layout and templates for new adapters (HTTP, gRPC, and local shim).
- telemetry stub server and example event schemas so adapters emit stable contract telemetry.
- canary deployment templates and a short runbook for gradual rollout with automated rollback.
- troubleshooting checklist of top support issues and canonical fixes for adapter authors.

Goal and audience

- Goal: Reduce time-to-first-success for adapter authors (internal teams and third-party integrators) by providing a single, documented playground and set of templates that cover the most common failure modes.
- Audience: SDK engineers, integrator partners, and platform engineers validating runtime shims.

When to use this kit

- Authoring a new adapter to translate third-party model runtime APIs to Redwood’s runtime API.
- Adding telemetry to an existing adapter and validating it against the telemetry contract.
- Performing a canary rollout of an adapter bundle to Dedicated/Private deployments.

Contents of the kit

1) Repo layout (recommended)

- /adapter/ — adapter source (language-specific).
- /stubs/telemetry/ — telemetry stub server (node/python).
- /templates/ — canary manifests, CI examples, and example feature flags.
- /examples/local-run/ — docker-compose for local sandbox + adapter.
- /docs/ — quickstart and runbook snippets.

2) Telemetry contract (summary)

- Required events: adapter.start, adapter.request, adapter.response, adapter.error, adapter.metrics.snapshot
- Core fields (all events): request_id, timestamp_iso, adapter_id, adapter_version, env, host_id
- P95 latency metric is expected on adapter.response: latency_ms (number)

Example JSON event (adapter.response):

{
  "event_type": "adapter.response",
  "request_id": "req-1a2b3c",
  "timestamp_iso": "2027-01-10T15:32:12Z",
  "adapter_id": "llm-http-adapter-v1",
  "adapter_version": "0.3.0",
  "env": "local",
  "host_id": "vm-023",
  "status": "ok",
  "latency_ms": 42,
  "tokens_in": 32,
  "tokens_out": 96
}

3) Telemetry stub server (quickstart)

- Purpose: receive adapter events on a local endpoint and validate schema, sample rate, and required fields.
- Start (node example):

$ cd stubs/telemetry && npm ci && node server.js

- Default listen port: 9911 (configurable via TELEMETRY_PORT). The stub returns 200 for valid events, 400 for schema violations, and logs to stdout.

4) Local sandbox run (docker-compose)

Steps to run the playground locally:

1. Clone kit: git clone git@internal:redwood/runtime-adapter-playground.git
2. Start the telemetry stub and local sandbox:

$ docker-compose -f examples/local-run/docker-compose.yml up --build

3. Build adapter image (example for Go adapter):

$ docker build -t rwd/adapter-go:dev ./adapter/go

4. Attach adapter to sandbox (local manifest):

$ kubectl apply -f templates/local/adapter-binding.yaml --context=kind-local

5. Exercise the adapter with the included curl script: templates/local/requests.sh

5) Canary rollout template and steps

We provide a Kubernetes manifest and a short CI snippet for a staged canary. The template uses traffic-splitting via ingress weights and automated monitoring checks that gate promotion.

Canary SLOs (suggested thresholds):

- Latency: p95 < 250ms (adapter.response.latency_ms)
- Error rate: < 0.5% (adapter.error events / total requests)
- Observability: telemetry ingestion success > 99% (stub acceptance rate)

Canary promotion sequence (numbered):

1. Push adapter image and tag: rwd/adapter-<name>:canary-<sha>
2. Create canary Deployment with 10% traffic weight.
3. Monitor for 30 minutes with automated checks: p95, error rate, and telemetry ingestion.
4. If checks pass, increase to 50% for 15 minutes.
5. Final promotion to 100% or rollback if any check is violated.

Automated gate example (Jenkins/GitHub Actions snippet):

- run: ./scripts/check-canary.sh --endpoint https://canary.local --window 30m --p95 250 --error-rate 0.005

check-canary.sh performs:

- queries telemetry stub for adapter.response/p95 and adapter.error counts
- computes failure conditions and returns non-zero exit to fail CI

6) Common adapter authoring patterns and templates

- Idempotent request handling: adapters must include request_id handling and be safe for retries. Template provides a small Redis-backed dedupe helper.
- Graceful partial failures: adapters should emit adapter.error with error_code and fallback_reason and return a graceful 200 with a structured error payload to the caller when downstream model fails.
- KV cache hints: include recommended cache TTLs in adapter metadata to allow the runtime to leverage prefix caching.

7) Troubleshooting and canonical fixes (top support issues)

A. Missing API key or 401 from Redwood host
- Symptom: adapter.request receives 401 from runtime or requests fail immediately.
- Canonical fix: Validate environment variable REDWOOD_API_KEY is present in adapter pod and the adapter signs requests using the standard header Authorization: Bearer <key>. Use the included diagnostic script: templates/local/check-credentials.sh

B. Telemetry sample rate lower than expected
- Symptom: production monitoring shows ingestion < 90% of events.
- Canonical fix: Ensure telemetry client is not dropping events on full buffers. Increase telemetry queue size (TELEMETRY_QSIZE env var) and switch to async flush on shutdown. See stubs/telemetry/README.md for recommended values (qsize=500, flush_interval_ms=250).

C. Streaming truncation or mis-ordered chunks
- Symptom: Client observes partial streaming responses or chunks arrive out of order.
- Canonical fix: Confirm adapter is using connection: keep-alive and sets chunked transfer semantics per the streaming guideline (templates/streaming/README). Add sequence_id to adapter.response events and validate order in the telemetry stub.

D. Elevated p95 latency after canary promotion
- Symptom: p95 jumps beyond threshold after migration to new adapter.
- Canonical fix: Rollback using canary manifest, capture pprof/trace from the adapter instance, and validate kernel scheduling and batch sizing. The troubleshooting checklist includes commands to capture CPU, GPU, and network stats: 

$ kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh -c "curl -s localhost:6060/debug/pprof/profile?seconds=30 > /tmp/profile.pprof"

8) Example adapter-binding manifest (local)

| Field | Example | Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| adapter_id | llm-http-adapter-v1 | unique id used in telemetry.adapter_id |
| health_check_path | /healthz | runtime uses for liveness probing |
| max_concurrency | 8 | cap per instance, used by autoscaler |
| telemetry_endpoint | http://host.docker.internal:9911/events | local stub address |

9) Access and ownership

- Kit owner: eng-developer-experience (doc); runtime-adapter-team (code).
- Escalation: open an issue in the runtime-adapter-playground repo and tag @runtime-adapter-team and @devx-oncall. For production incidents contact eng-sre via PagerDuty.

10) Appendices

A. Quick CLI snippets

- Build and push canary image:
$ docker build -t rwd/adapter-example:canary-$(git rev-parse --short HEAD) .
$ docker push rwd/adapter-example:canary-$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)

- Apply canary manifest (k8s):
$ kubectl apply -f templates/canary/adapter-canary.yaml

B. YAML example for canary scaling (templates/canary/adapter-canary.yaml):

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: adapter-example-canary
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: adapter-example
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: adapter-example
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: adapter
        image: rwd/adapter-example:canary-abc123
        env:
        - name: REDWOOD_API_KEY
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: redwood-credentials
              key: api_key

C. Contacts and runbook links

- Runtime Adapter team Slack: #rwd-runtime-adapter
- DevX docs: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/sdk-local-proxy-sandbox-strategies-2026.json
- Incident runbook: /confluence/eng-oncall-and-incident-response/runbooks/adapter-incident-runbook

If you are contributing templates or fixes, open a PR against the runtime-adapter-playground repo and add one of the reviewers listed at the top. The templates are designed to be minimal but extensible; include a short integration test in /examples/local-run/tests that exercises both telemetry emission and the runtime request path.

- troubleshooting checklist of top support issues and canonical fixes for adapter authors.

Goal and audience

- Goal: Reduce time-to-first-success for adapter authors (internal teams and third-party integrators) by providing a single, documented playground and set of templates that cover the most common failure modes.
- Audience: SDK engineers, integrator partners, and platform engineers validating runtime shims.

When to use this kit

- Authoring a new adapter to translate third-party model runtime APIs to Redwood’s runtime API.
- Adding telemetry to an existing adapter and validating it against the telemetry contract.
- Performing a canary rollout of an adapter bundle to Dedicated/Private deployments.

Contents of the kit

1) Repo layout (recommended)

- /adapter/ — adapter source (language-specific).
- /stubs/telemetry/ — telemetry stub server (node/python).
- /templates/ — canary manifests, CI examples, and example feature flags.
- /examples/local-run/ — docker-compose for local sandbox + adapter.
- /docs/ — quickstart and runbook snippets.

2) Telemetry contract (summary)

- Required events: adapter.start, adapter.request, adapter.response, adapter.error, adapter.metrics.snapshot
- Core fields (all events): request_id, timestamp_iso, adapter_id, adapter_version, env, host_id
- P95 latency metric is expected on adapter.response: latency_ms (number)

Example JSON event (adapter.response):

{
  "event_type": "adapter.response",
  "request_id": "req-1a2b3c",
  "timestamp_iso": "2027-01-10T15:32:12Z",
  "adapter_id": "llm-http-adapter-v1",
  "adapter_version": "0.3.0",
  "env": "local",
  "host_id": "vm-023",
  "status": "ok",
  "latency_ms": 42,
  "tokens_in": 32,
  "tokens_out": 96
}

3) Telemetry stub server (quickstart)

- Purpose: receive adapter events on a local endpoint and validate schema, sample rate, and required fields.
- Start (node example):

$ cd stubs/telemetry && npm ci && node server.js

- Default listen port: 9911 (configurable via TELEMETRY_PORT). The stub returns 200 for valid events, 400 for schema violations, and logs to stdout.

4) Local sandbox run (docker-compose)

Steps to run the playground locally:

1. Clone kit: git clone git@internal:redwood/runtime-adapter-playground.git
2. Start the telemetry stub and local sandbox:

$ docker-compose -f examples/local-run/docker-compose.yml up --build

3. Build adapter image (example for Go adapter):

$ docker build -t rwd/adapter-go:dev ./adapter/go

4. Attach adapter to sandbox (local manifest):

$ kubectl apply -f templates/local/adapter-binding.yaml --context=kind-local

5. Exercise the adapter with the included curl script: templates/local/requests.sh

5) Canary rollout template and steps

We provide a Kubernetes manifest and a short CI snippet for a staged canary. The template uses traffic-splitting via ingress weights and automated monitoring checks that gate promotion.

Canary SLOs (suggested thresholds):

- Latency: p95 < 250ms (adapter.response.latency_ms)
- Error rate: < 0.5% (adapter.error events / total requests)
- Observability: telemetry ingestion success > 99% (stub acceptance rate)

Canary promotion sequence (numbered):

1. Push adapter image and tag: rwd/adapter-<name>:canary-<sha>
2. Create canary Deployment with 10% traffic weight.
3. Monitor for 30 minutes with automated checks: p95, error rate, and telemetry ingestion.
4. If checks pass, increase to 50% for 15 minutes.
5. Final promotion to 100% or rollback if any check is violated.

Automated gate example (Jenkins/GitHub Actions snippet):

- run: ./scripts/check-canary.sh --endpoint https://canary.local --window 30m --p95 250 --error-rate 0.005

check-canary.sh performs:

- queries telemetry stub for adapter.response/p95 and adapter.error counts
- computes failure conditions and returns non-zero exit to fail CI

6) Common adapter authoring patterns and templates

- Idempotent request handling: adapters must include request_id handling and be safe for retries. Template provides a small Redis-backed dedupe helper.
- Graceful partial failures: adapters should emit adapter.error with error_code and fallback_reason and return a graceful 200 with a structured error payload to the caller when downstream model fails.
- KV cache hints: include recommended cache TTLs in adapter metadata to allow the runtime to leverage prefix caching.

7) Troubleshooting and canonical fixes (top support issues)

A. Missing API key or 401 from Redwood host
- Symptom: adapter.request receives 401 from runtime or requests fail immediately.
- Canonical fix: Validate environment variable REDWOOD_API_KEY is present in adapter pod and the adapter signs requests using the standard header Authorization: Bearer <key>. Use the included diagnostic script: templates/local/check-credentials.sh

B. Telemetry sample rate lower than expected
- Symptom: production monitoring shows ingestion < 90% of events.
- Canonical fix: Ensure telemetry client is not dropping events on full buffers. Increase telemetry queue size (TELEMETRY_QSIZE env var) and switch to async flush on shutdown. See stubs/telemetry/README.md for recommended values (qsize=500, flush_interval_ms=250).

C. Streaming truncation or mis-ordered chunks
- Symptom: Client observes partial streaming responses or chunks arrive out of order.
- Canonical fix: Confirm adapter is using connection: keep-alive and sets chunked transfer semantics per the streaming guideline (templates/streaming/README). Add sequence_id to adapter.response events and validate order in the telemetry stub.

D. Elevated p95 latency after canary promotion
- Symptom: p95 jumps beyond threshold after migration to new adapter.
- Canonical fix: Rollback using canary manifest, capture pprof/trace from the adapter instance, and validate kernel scheduling and batch sizing. The troubleshooting checklist includes commands to capture CPU, GPU, and network stats: 

$ kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh -c "curl -s localhost:6060/debug/pprof/profile?seconds=30 > /tmp/profile.pprof"

8) Example adapter-binding manifest (local)

| Field | Example | Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| adapter_id | llm-http-adapter-v1 | unique id used in telemetry.adapter_id |
| health_check_path | /healthz | runtime uses for liveness probing |
| max_concurrency | 8 | cap per instance, used by autoscaler |
| telemetry_endpoint | http://host.docker.internal:9911/events | local stub address |

9) Access and ownership

- Kit owner: eng-developer-experience (doc); runtime-adapter-team (code).
- Escalation: open an issue in the runtime-adapter-playground repo and tag @runtime-adapter-team and @devx-oncall. For production incidents contact eng-sre via PagerDuty.

10) Appendices

A. Quick CLI snippets

- Build and push canary image:
$ docker build -t rwd/adapter-example:canary-$(git rev-parse --short HEAD) .
$ docker push rwd/adapter-example:canary-$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)

- Apply canary manifest (k8s):
$ kubectl apply -f templates/canary/adapter-canary.yaml

B. YAML example for canary scaling (templates/canary/adapter-canary.yaml):

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: adapter-example-canary
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: adapter-example
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: adapter-example
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: adapter
        image: rwd/adapter-example:canary-abc123
        env:
        - name: REDWOOD_API_KEY
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: redwood-credentials
              key: api_key

C. Contacts and runbook links

- Runtime Adapter team Slack: #rwd-runtime-adapter
- DevX docs: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/sdk-local-proxy-sandbox-strategies-2026.json
- Incident runbook: /confluence/eng-oncall-and-incident-response/runbooks/adapter-incident-runbook

If you are contributing templates or fixes, open a PR against the runtime-adapter-playground repo and add one of the reviewers listed at the top. The templates are designed to be minimal but extensible; include a short integration test in /examples/local-run/tests that exercises both telemetry emission and the runtime request path.

title: Embedded Feature Simulator: SDK Onboarding Examples & Telemetry Codelets
preview:
Overview

This page describes a lightweight, reproducible simulator pattern for embedding feature flows used in SDK onboarding, local integration tests, and support triage. The simulator ("embedded feature simulator") is a small, language-agnostic harness that mimics common client-side behavior: batching, framing, streaming, and local telemetry emission. It is intended to help SDK authors, support engineers, and sample-app integrators validate behavior against Redwood API expectations before full integration.

Why this exists

- Speed: launch a local stub that reproduces class of SDK runtime problems in under a minute.
- Determinism: control tokenization and response timing to exercise batching and timeout logic.
- Observability alignment: validate telemetry event formats and correlation IDs before customers escalate.
- Canonical fixes: include reproducible codelets that match support playbooks.

Scope and limitations

- The simulator is not a replacement for full e2e tests against the Redwood hosted runtime.
- It focuses on API-level interactions (HTTP/HTTP2/WebSocket-style streaming) and SDK telemetry hooks, not GPU performance.
- Intended for developer machines, CI smoke lanes, and SDK support sandboxes.

Quickstart: run the Python simulator locally

1) Prerequisites
   - Python 3.10+
   - pip install -r requirements.txt (requirements include fastapi, uvicorn, httpx)

2) Start simulator (one command)

   $ python simulator_app.py --port 8085 --latency-ms 40 --stream-chunk-size 16

3) Run sample SDK call (node/python) against http://localhost:8085/v1/generate

   - Expected: immediate 200 with streaming chunks every 40ms and telemetry events logged to /v1/telemetry endpoint.

Simulator features and flags

- --latency-ms: mean response chunk delay to simulate pacing.
- --stream-chunk-size: tokens emitted per chunk in streaming mode.
- --failure-mode: none | intermittent-429 | partial-kv-loss | stuck-kv
- --telemetry-mode: local-log | post-to-endpoint

Design: core components

1) Request ingress matcher
   - Matches incoming HTTP request to an example flow (embedding, generation, stream) by header and body shape.
   - Allows flow overrides for testing features such as model-variant fallback and routing policies.

2) Response generator (deterministic)
   - Template-driven text generation with token boundary awareness so SDK token counters behave the same as real runs.
   - Support for a small set of templates: concise, verbose, hallucination-trigger, truncated.

3) Telemetry emitter
   - Emits canonical telemetry events that SDKs are expected to produce. By default it POSTs to /v1/telemetry on the same simulator.
   - Events follow the telemetry schema in the "Telemetry contract" section below.

4) Failure injector
   - Deterministic toggles for 429 spikes, stream truncation, and retry-after semantics.

Telemetry contract (canonical schema)

- event_time: ISO-8601 timestamp string
- sdk_client_id: string (UUID)
- request_id: string (alphanumeric)
- flow: one of ["generation", "embedding", "rerank"]
- model: string (example: "redwood-1-small")
- tokens_sent: integer (estimated)
- tokens_received: integer (estimated)
- latency_ms: integer
- status: one of ["ok", "partial", "error", "rate_limited"]
- error_code: optional string (e.g., "429_RATE_LIMIT")

Example telemetry event (JSON)

{
  "event_time": "2026-11-07T14:03:22.511Z",
  "sdk_client_id": "b4b1d5fe-7a9a-4e7e-b2c6-9f2d8e5c3f11",
  "request_id": "req_8x9z1a",
  "flow": "generation",
  "model": "redwood-1-small",
  "tokens_sent": 24,
  "tokens_received": 128,
  "latency_ms": 198,
  "status": "ok"
}

Codelets: minimal examples for common scenarios

A) Node.js sample: simulate slow streaming handling

// pseudo-code
const res = await fetch('http://localhost:8085/v1/generate', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify({ prompt }) });
for await (const chunk of res.body) {
  // assert that SDK resumes progress and appends tokens in-place
}

B) Python sample: verify retry-on-429 with jitter

import httpx

async def call():
  r = await client.post('http://localhost:8085/v1/generate', json=payload)
  if r.status_code == 429:
    # follow SDK backoff policy and assert eventual success or graceful downgrade

C) Telemetry validator (curl + jq)

$ curl -s http://localhost:8085/v1/telemetry | jq '.[] | select(.status != "ok")'

Test matrix and recommended smoke lanes

- Local fast feedback: latency-ms=10, stream-chunk-size=32, failure-mode=none (run on dev machine).
- CI smoke: latency-ms=50, stream-chunk-size=8, failure-mode=intermittent-429 (assert SDK backoff and error counters).
- Support repro: latency-ms=200, failure-mode=partial-kv-loss (reproduce cache/KV-miss patterns).

Operational runbook: triage steps for telemetry mismatches

1) Reproduce with simulator: get SDK to call the local simulator with the same headers and body observed in customer logs.
2) Validate telemetry contract: curl /v1/telemetry and compare fields with customer payloads.
3) If tokens_sent mismatch > 10%: check tokenizer version mismatch in SDK sample. Action: pin tokenizers in SDK dependency and document in release notes. Owner: devx-team.
4) If streaming order incorrect: check framing logic (is-end / seq ids) in SDK. Action: apply canonical stream-merge helper from examples folder. Owner: sdk-runtime team.
5) If rate-limited behavior diverges: compare backoff implementation against "SDK backoff reference" (link in related pages). If needed, ship a hotfix. Owner: platform reliability.

Canonical fixes and example patches

- Fix: streaming reassembly bug in Node adapter
  - Symptom: partial tokens swallowed when multiple concurrent streams share buffer.
  - Patch: use per-request stream buffer and flush-on-boundary. Example patch included in /examples/node/stream-fix.js.

- Fix: telemetry missing sdk_client_id for certain async flows
  - Symptom: telemetry events from nested async tasks lacked the correlation id.
  - Patch: ensure request-scoped context propagation; we include a small codelet for async-local-storage with clear license.

Repository layout (what you get when you clone the examples repo)

- /simulator/simulator_app.py           # Python FastAPI harness
- /examples/node/stream-fix.js           # Node minimal adapter and fixes
- /examples/python/retry_on_429.py      # Python sample with injectable jitter/backoff
- /examples/telemetry/validator.sh       # curl + jq validator for telemetry events
- /docs/README.md                        # Quickstart and troubleshooting tips

Best practices for SDK authors

- Emit request_id early and propagate it to all async boundaries.
- Always record tokens_sent and an estimated tokens_received to help downstream billing and SLO calculations.
- Use local simulator in CI for a lightweight smoke gate: fail builds only on protocol regressions, not textual quality diffs.
- Include a telemetry validator job that compares event shapes to the canonical schema.

Maintenance and ownership

- Ownership: devx-team maintains simulator codelets and examples.
- SLA: update simulator templates for each major SDK release and new streaming feature within two weeks of the release.
- Review cadence: quarterly review with platform-reliability and sdk-runtime teams.

Appendix: troubleshooting tips

- If simulator emits constant 429s: ensure failure-mode was not left on (run with --failure-mode none).
- If tokens mismatch with production samples: verify tokenizer version and shared encoding table.
- If telemetry endpoint returns 5xx from simulator: check local port conflicts and increase logging with --telemetry-mode local-log.

Contacts and escalation

- Primary owner: Priya Natarajan (devx-team) — priya@redwood.ai
- Secondary: Daniel Kim (sdk-runtime) — daniel@redwood.ai
- For urgent customer-facing regressions open the "SDK Support" PagerDuty channel and tag devx on Slack (#devx-oncall).

Related material

- SDK backoff reference and canonical retry windows (linked in related_pages).
- Tokenizer compatibility matrix (refer to product-docs/tokenizer-matrix).

Change log

- 2026-06-01: created initial draft with simulator design and quickstart.
- 2026-07-20: added failure injection modes and telemetry contract.
- 2026-11-07: updated examples to include Node stream-fix codelet and CI smoke lane recommendations.

title: SDK telemetry mismatch — canonical detection & fixes
preview:
Summary
=======

This document describes the common telemetry-mismatch patterns we see between Redwood client SDKs and the Redwood serving/control plane, concrete detection heuristics, triage steps, canonical fixes (SDK and server-side), and recommended tests to prevent regressions. The audience is SDK engineers, developer-experience support, and oncall SREs.

Why this matters
------------------

Telemetry fidelity is required for accurate cost attribution, latency breakdowns, and automatic routing decisions. Mismatches lead to:
- Incorrect token counts in billing dashboards
- Misattributed latency or error spikes that obscure root cause
- Invalid sampling for analytics or A/B rollouts
- Failed dependability checks in canary deployments

Common symptom categories
-------------------------

1) Token count delta — SDK reports fewer/more tokens than server: typically caused by encoding differences, retry double-counting or missing streaming ack points.
2) Event ordering drift — SDK emits events out of order (e.g., start->complete swapped) due to async middleware.
3) Missing error codes — SDK surface layer catches and transforms internal codes to generic errors, stripping server codes used by routing.
4) Sampling mismatch — SDK sampling config (telemetry.sample_rate) different from platform defaults causing uneven analytics coverage.

Detection heuristics (quick checks)
----------------------------------

- Compare per-route token_count_sdk vs token_count_platform aggregated by 5m windows. Flag routes with absolute delta > 5% and > 1k tokens in window.
- Alert when SDK-side success rate diverges from platform success rate by >2 percentage points for 10m.
- Monitor event latency histogram skew: if client-observed p95 differs from server p95 by >50ms and correlation with 'retry' tag is >10%, inspect retry handling.

Automated diagnostic queries (examples)
---------------------------------------

1) Token delta (SQL-ish pseudocode)

SELECT route, window_start, SUM(token_count_sdk) AS sdk_tokens, SUM(token_count_server) AS server_tokens,
  (SUM(token_count_server)-SUM(token_count_sdk))/NULLIF(SUM(token_count_server),0) AS relative_delta
FROM telemetry_ingest.tokens
WHERE window_start >= now() - interval '1 hour'
GROUP BY route, window_start
HAVING SUM(server_tokens) > 1000 AND ABS(relative_delta) > 0.05;

2) Event ordering mismatches (log query)

index=client-telemetry OR index=platform-telemetry |
  transaction_id | sort timestamp | dedup transaction_id | where first.event != last.event

Canonical root causes and fixes
--------------------------------

A. Encoding/Tokenizer mismatch
- Root cause: SDK and server use different tokenizer versions or normalization rules (e.g., unicode normalization, pre-tokenization truncation).
- Fix: Pin tokenizer version in SDK packages and ensure platform tokenizer metadata is available in model catalog.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Add tokenizer-version metadata to every request header: X-Redwood-Tokenizer: "tkn-v2025-11"
  2. Expose a compatibility check API: redwood.sdk.compatibility.checkTokenizer(model, tokenizerVersion)
  3. Add unit tests asserting token counts for a canonical prompt set (see tests/telemetry/token-compat-sets).
- Server-side mitigation: When mismatch exceeds threshold, annotate request with token_estimate_source="client-est" and route to an investigational queue.

B. Retry double-counting
- Root cause: Retries increment telemetry counters both on the original request and on the retry path; duplicate transaction_id is not deduped.
- Fix: Use idempotency key + platform-level dedup TTL.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Generate idempotency_key per logical request and attach header: Idempotency-Key: <uuid4>
  2. Ensure retries reuse the same key; backoff middleware must not regenerate it.
  3. Document the behavior: retries are counted once on platform if idempotency_key present and dedupe TTL=30s.
- Server-side mitigation: Implement duplicate transaction detection using (idempotency_key, route, model) keys and de-duplicate metrics increments.

C. Streaming vs. Final-event mismatch
- Root cause: SDK emits a "response_complete" event when stream is closed locally, while platform waits for final server ack; missing streaming-ack messages cause underreporting.
- Fix: Align client and server streaming lifecycle and add explicit ack messages.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Implement websocket-level "stream_ack" message after final chunk is processed.
  2. Emit events in order: request.start -> chunk.* -> stream_ack -> request.complete.
  3. Add tests that simulate partial disconnects and ensure stream_ack is retried.
- Server-side mitigation: Accept client-side stream_ack as authoritative for final token count if signed by SDK key.

D. Telemetry sanitization middleware swallowing server codes
- Root cause: Middleware transforms platform error 503 -> SDKError("transient") and drops structured_code. Downstream systems rely on structured_code for routing.
- Fix: Preserve structured_error_code and include a mapping in SDK error objects.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Extend Error class: StructuredError { message, structured_code, server_timestamp }
  2. Surface structured_code in telemetry events as metadata.
  3. Do not convert structured_code to free text.
- Server-side mitigation: Add assertion tests in the CI that call each SDK and verify structured_code arrives unmodified.

Operational runbook (high-level)
--------------------------------

When an alert fires for telemetry mismatch:
1) Pager oncall receives alert and checks pre-baked diagnostic queries (Token delta, Event ordering).
2) If delta localized to a specific SDK version -> open a bug in SDK repo and tag "telemetry-high"; escalate to SDK release lead.
3) If delta global across SDK versions -> trace by model/region and check for server deployment or tokenizer change; open incident if >5% customers affected.
4) Apply temporary mitigations: enable server-side token_estimate_source tagging, pause affected routing automations, and reduce sampling of analytics to avoid noise.
5) After root cause fix, run golden prompt set and deploy compatibility test before closing the ticket.

Detailed triage checklist (step-by-step)
-----------------------------------------

1. Collect IDs: transaction_id, request_id, sdk_version, model, route, region.
2. Run token delta query for the transaction_id.
3. Search logs for idempotency_key collisions.
4. Reproduce locally with the same SDK version and tokenizer bundle.
5. If streaming: run controlled disconnect test to see if stream_ack missing.
6. If error codes missing: replay requests with debug-telemetry enabled and capture raw server response.
7. Draft PR with minimal SDK change and smoke tests, request expedited review from reviewer list.

Preventing regressions (tests and release gates)
------------------------------------------------
- Add a required pre-merge check in SDK repositories: "telemetry-integration-check" that runs the token-compat fixture (10 canonical prompts) and fails on >1% token count variance.
- Platform CI must run an SDK compatibility matrix (current SDK stable + previous two versions) on every tokenizer or tokenization-related commit.
- Add e2e canary that compares client-observed vs server-observed telemetry for a synthetic traffic profile every hour and opens a ticket on drift >2%.

Snippets & examples
-------------------

1) Example: attaching headers in Python SDK

```python
from redwood import Client
client = Client(api_key="REDACTED")
resp = client.generate(
  model="redwood-alpha-1",
  prompt="Summarize the Q1 goals.",
  headers={
    "X-Redwood-Tokenizer": "tkn-v2025-11",
    "Idempotency-Key": "6f5f0f6a-1b5c-4caa-b2aa-bb1f0e2d3f4a",
  }
)
```

2) Example server-side de-dup pseudocode (Go)

```go
func recordTelemetry(req TelemetryReq) {
  key := fmt.Sprintf("%s:%s:%s", req.IdempotencyKey, req.Route, req.Model)
  if cache.Exists(key) {
    return // already counted
  }
  cache.Set(key, true, 30*time.Second)
  metrics.Inc("tokens", req.TokenCount)
}
```

Configuration table (recommended defaults)
-----------------------------------------

| Setting | SDK default | Platform default | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| telemetry.sample_rate | 0.05 | 0.05 | Ensure SDK default matches platform to avoid sampling mismatch |
| telemetry.tokenizer_version_header | "X-Redwood-Tokenizer" | required | Set and assert at request time |
| telemetry.idempotency_ttl_seconds | 30 | 30 | Deduplication window for token counting |
| telemetry.stream_ack_enabled | true | true | Required for websocket/stream transports |

Ownership & escalation
-----------------------

- Primary owner: Developer Experience (SDK team) — triage and release SDK patches.
- Secondary owner: Eng Platform — server-side dedup and compatibility checks.
- If customer-visible billing impact > $5k in 24h -> page finance-legal and account managers; start incident response.

Communications templates
------------------------

Short customer notification (billing-impacting):
"We detected a telemetry reporting discrepancy affecting X requests between SDK version Y and the platform during YYYY-MM-DD. We have deployed a mitigation and are rolling an SDK fix. We will provide a follow-up with corrected billing if required."

Internal slack template to #dx-oncall:
"ALERT: telemetry-drift route={route} sdk={sdk_version} delta={percent}% action=triage assigned={owner}"

Appendix: canonical prompt set (for token compatibility tests)
-----------------------------------------------------------
- 10 prompts covering: JSON generation, long legal text, unicode normalization edge cases, heavily tokenized code, multi-lingual inputs (zh, es, ar), and streaming-chunked responses.
- Stored at repo: redwood/telemetry/canonical-prompts/compat-2026.json

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-02-10: Document created by Ava Chen.
- 2026-02-20: Added server de-dup pseudocode and configuration table (reviewer: Diego Ramirez).
- 2026-03-01: Added streaming ack guidance and updated triage thresholds (reviewer: Priya Nandakumar).
text:
Summary
=======

This document describes the common telemetry-mismatch patterns we see between Redwood client SDKs and the Redwood serving/control plane, concrete detection heuristics, triage steps, canonical fixes (SDK and server-side), and recommended tests to prevent regressions. The audience is SDK engineers, developer-experience support, and oncall SREs.

Why this matters
------------------

Telemetry fidelity is required for accurate cost attribution, latency breakdowns, and automatic routing decisions. Mismatches lead to:
- Incorrect token counts in billing dashboards
- Misattributed latency or error spikes that obscure root cause
- Invalid sampling for analytics or A/B rollouts
- Failed dependability checks in canary deployments

Common symptom categories
-------------------------

1) Token count delta — SDK reports fewer/more tokens than server: typically caused by encoding differences, retry double-counting or missing streaming ack points.
2) Event ordering drift — SDK emits events out of order (e.g., start->complete swapped) due to async middleware.
3) Missing error codes — SDK surface layer catches and transforms internal codes to generic errors, stripping server codes used by routing.
4) Sampling mismatch — SDK sampling config (telemetry.sample_rate) different from platform defaults causing uneven analytics coverage.

Detection heuristics (quick checks)
----------------------------------

- Compare per-route token_count_sdk vs token_count_platform aggregated by 5m windows. Flag routes with absolute delta > 5% and > 1k tokens in window.
- Alert when SDK-side success rate diverges from platform success rate by >2 percentage points for 10m.
- Monitor event latency histogram skew: if client-observed p95 differs from server p95 by >50ms and correlation with 'retry' tag is >10%, inspect retry handling.

Automated diagnostic queries (examples)
---------------------------------------

1) Token delta (SQL-ish pseudocode)

SELECT route, window_start, SUM(token_count_sdk) AS sdk_tokens, SUM(token_count_server) AS server_tokens,
  (SUM(token_count_server)-SUM(token_count_sdk))/NULLIF(SUM(token_count_server),0) AS relative_delta
FROM telemetry_ingest.tokens
WHERE window_start >= now() - interval '1 hour'
GROUP BY route, window_start
HAVING SUM(server_tokens) > 1000 AND ABS(relative_delta) > 0.05;

2) Event ordering mismatches (log query)

index=client-telemetry OR index=platform-telemetry |
  transaction_id | sort timestamp | dedup transaction_id | where first.event != last.event

Canonical root causes and fixes
--------------------------------

A. Encoding/Tokenizer mismatch
- Root cause: SDK and server use different tokenizer versions or normalization rules (e.g., unicode normalization, pre-tokenization truncation).
- Fix: Pin tokenizer version in SDK packages and ensure platform tokenizer metadata is available in model catalog.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Add tokenizer-version metadata to every request header: X-Redwood-Tokenizer: "tkn-v2025-11"
  2. Expose a compatibility check API: redwood.sdk.compatibility.checkTokenizer(model, tokenizerVersion)
  3. Add unit tests asserting token counts for a canonical prompt set (see tests/telemetry/token-compat-sets).
- Server-side mitigation: When mismatch exceeds threshold, annotate request with token_estimate_source="client-est" and route to an investigational queue.

B. Retry double-counting
- Root cause: Retries increment telemetry counters both on the original request and on the retry path; duplicate transaction_id is not deduped.
- Fix: Use idempotency key + platform-level dedup TTL.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Generate idempotency_key per logical request and attach header: Idempotency-Key: <uuid4>
  2. Ensure retries reuse the same key; backoff middleware must not regenerate it.
  3. Document the behavior: retries are counted once on platform if idempotency_key present and dedupe TTL=30s.
- Server-side mitigation: Implement duplicate transaction detection using (idempotency_key, route, model) keys and de-duplicate metrics increments.

C. Streaming vs. Final-event mismatch
- Root cause: SDK emits a "response_complete" event when stream is closed locally, while platform waits for final server ack; missing streaming-ack messages cause underreporting.
- Fix: Align client and server streaming lifecycle and add explicit ack messages.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Implement websocket-level "stream_ack" message after final chunk is processed.
  2. Emit events in order: request.start -> chunk.* -> stream_ack -> request.complete.
  3. Add tests that simulate partial disconnects and ensure stream_ack is retried.
- Server-side mitigation: Accept client-side stream_ack as authoritative for final token count if signed by SDK key.

D. Telemetry sanitization middleware swallowing server codes
- Root cause: Middleware transforms platform error 503 -> SDKError("transient") and drops structured_code. Downstream systems rely on structured_code for routing.
- Fix: Preserve structured_error_code and include a mapping in SDK error objects.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Extend Error class: StructuredError { message, structured_code, server_timestamp }
  2. Surface structured_code in telemetry events as metadata.
  3. Do not convert structured_code to free text.
- Server-side mitigation: Add assertion tests in the CI that call each SDK and verify structured_code arrives unmodified.

Operational runbook (high-level)
--------------------------------

When an alert fires for telemetry mismatch:
1) Pager oncall receives alert and checks pre-baked diagnostic queries (Token delta, Event ordering).
2) If delta localized to a specific SDK version -> open a bug in SDK repo and tag "telemetry-high"; escalate to SDK release lead.
3) If delta global across SDK versions -> trace by model/region and check for server deployment or tokenizer change; open incident if >5% customers affected.
4) Apply temporary mitigations: enable server-side token_estimate_source tagging, pause affected routing automations, and reduce sampling of analytics to avoid noise.
5) After root cause fix, run golden prompt set and deploy compatibility test before closing the ticket.

Detailed triage checklist (step-by-step)
-----------------------------------------

1. Collect IDs: transaction_id, request_id, sdk_version, model, route, region.
2. Run token delta query for the transaction_id.
3. Search logs for idempotency_key collisions.
4. Reproduce locally with the same SDK version and tokenizer bundle.
5. If streaming: run controlled disconnect test to see if stream_ack missing.
6. If error codes missing: replay requests with debug-telemetry enabled and capture raw server response.
7. Draft PR with minimal SDK change and smoke tests, request expedited review from reviewer list.

Preventing regressions (tests and release gates)
------------------------------------------------
- Add a required pre-merge check in SDK repositories: "telemetry-integration-check" that runs the token-compat fixture (10 canonical prompts) and fails on >1% token count variance.
- Platform CI must run an SDK compatibility matrix (current SDK stable + previous two versions) on every tokenizer or tokenization-related commit.
- Add e2e canary that compares client-observed vs server-observed telemetry for a synthetic traffic profile every hour and opens a ticket on drift >2%.

Snippets & examples
-------------------

1) Example: attaching headers in Python SDK

```python
from redwood import Client
client = Client(api_key="REDACTED")
resp = client.generate(
  model="redwood-alpha-1",
  prompt="Summarize the Q1 goals.",
  headers={
    "X-Redwood-Tokenizer": "tkn-v2025-11",
    "Idempotency-Key": "6f5f0f6a-1b5c-4caa-b2aa-bb1f0e2d3f4a",
  }
)
```

2) Example server-side de-dup pseudocode (Go)

```go
func recordTelemetry(req TelemetryReq) {
  key := fmt.Sprintf("%s:%s:%s", req.IdempotencyKey, req.Route, req.Model)
  if cache.Exists(key) {
    return // already counted
  }
  cache.Set(key, true, 30*time.Second)
  metrics.Inc("tokens", req.TokenCount)
}
```

Configuration table (recommended defaults)
-----------------------------------------

| Setting | SDK default | Platform default | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| telemetry.sample_rate | 0.05 | 0.05 | Ensure SDK default matches platform to avoid sampling mismatch |
| telemetry.tokenizer_version_header | "X-Redwood-Tokenizer" | required | Set and assert at request time |
| telemetry.idempotency_ttl_seconds | 30 | 30 | Deduplication window for token counting |
| telemetry.stream_ack_enabled | true | true | Required for websocket/stream transports |

Ownership & escalation
-----------------------

- Primary owner: Developer Experience (SDK team) — triage and release SDK patches.
- Secondary owner: Eng Platform — server-side dedup and compatibility checks.
- If customer-visible billing impact > $5k in 24h -> page finance-legal and account managers; start incident response.

Communications templates
------------------------

Short customer notification (billing-impacting):
"We detected a telemetry reporting discrepancy affecting X requests between SDK version Y and the platform during YYYY-MM-DD. We have deployed a mitigation and are rolling an SDK fix. We will provide a follow-up with corrected billing if required."

Internal slack template to #dx-oncall:
"ALERT: telemetry-drift route={route} sdk={sdk_version} delta={percent}% action=triage assigned={owner}"

Appendix: canonical prompt set (for token compatibility tests)
-----------------------------------------------------------
- 10 prompts covering: JSON generation, long legal text, unicode normalization edge cases, heavily tokenized code, multi-lingual inputs (zh, es, ar), and streaming-chunked responses.
- Stored at repo: redwood/telemetry/canonical-prompts/compat-2026.json

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-02-10: Document created by Ava Chen.
- 2026-02-20: Added server de-dup pseudocode and configuration table (reviewer: Diego Ramirez).
- 2026-03-01: Added streaming ack guidance and updated triage thresholds (reviewer: Priya Nandakumar).
