title: SDK: Batching & Streaming Playbook — Patterns, Quickstarts, and Canonical Fixes
preview:
Summary:
This playbook consolidates recommended SDK patterns for streaming + batching usage with Redwood Inference APIs, a short set of quickstart templates for client teams, and canonical fixes for the top support issues we observe in the field. The goal is to reduce token cost without compromising latency SLOs and to provide reproducible steps for common incidents.

Scope:
- SDKs covered: python, node, and go (patterns are portable).
- Traffic modes: low-latency single-request streaming, throughput-oriented batched synchronous requests, hybrid streaming-with-batch setups.
- Not covered: model tuning, model architecture changes.

Intended audience:
- Application engineers integrating Redwood APIs.
- SDK maintainers and support engineers.

1) Quick primer: when to stream vs batch
- Stream when per-request latency (time-to-first-token) is the priority (chat, UI feedback, interactive assistants). Target: TTF token < 200 ms for UI-grade experience.
- Batch when throughput and cost-per-token are the priority (offline generation, high-concurrency inference pipelines). Target: cost efficiency at the expense of slightly higher tail latency.
- Hybrid: use streaming for first N tokens to satisfy TTF SLO and then switch to batched generation for long outputs.

2) Recommended defaults (opinionated)
- Per-tenant latency SLO: 95th percentile TTF < 250ms (hosted tier). Track via console traces and token-level metrics.
- Default batch size by workload (recommendation table):
| Workload Type | Preferred batch size | Max acceptable batch | Notes |
| --- | ---: | ---: | --- |
| Short chat turns (<= 128 tokens) | 8 | 16 | Keep small to preserve TTF |
| Document embedding (dense) | 64 | 256 | Throughput-oriented; use asynchronous workers |
| Long form generation (>512 tokens) | 4 | 8 | Use streaming + chunking for UX |
| Reranking (many short sequences) | 32 | 128 | Use vectorized scoring endpoints when available |

3) SDK quickstart templates
- Python streaming quickstart (minimal):
```python
from redwood import RedwoodClient
client = RedwoodClient(api_key="$REDWOOD_KEY")
# lightweight stream handler
with client.chat.stream(model="redwood-2x", conversation_id="ui-123") as stream:
    stream.send({"role":"user","content":"Summarize latest sales deck"})
    for chunk in stream.receive():
        # update UI chunk-by-chunk
        print(chunk.text, end="", flush=True)
```

- Node batch generator (worker):
```js
const { RedwoodClient } = require('@redwood/inference');
const client = new RedwoodClient({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_KEY });
async function processBatch(inputs){
  // inputs: [{prompt, meta}, ...]
  const res = await client.generate.batch({model: 'open-rlhf-1', inputs, batchSize: 32, timeoutMs: 30000});
  return res.outputs;
}
```

- CLI to reproduce throughput locally (load test):
$ redwood-cli loadtest --model redwood-2x --concurrency 32 --rps 200 --prompt-file ./samples/prompts.txt --batch-size 16

4) Streaming implementation notes (SDK authors)
- Implement backpressure: when network or downstream UI cannot process chunks fast enough, provide a pause/resume API to the consumer.
- Idempotency: attach a client-generated request_id to streaming sessions so reconnecting clients can resume from the last acknowledged token.
- Heartbeats: emit heartbeat frames every 10s of inactivity to keep connections alive behind proxies/load balancers.
- Chunk size: prefer many small chunks (< 2 KB) for interactive UX; combine tokens only when network overhead is high.

5) Batching implementation notes (SDK + service rules)
- Continuous batching window: 8–20ms for interactive workloads; 50–200ms for higher throughput jobs. Tunable via SDK config.
- Max batch composition rules: do not mix model families with incompatible KV cache sizes in the same batch. SDK must group by model variant, seq_len, and quantization profile.
- Backoff policy: when the service returns 429 or capacity fallback, SDK should implement a jittered exponential backoff with max backoff 10s and initial backoff 50–150ms.

6) Top observed issues and canonical fixes (support playbook)
Issue A: Very high TTF for interactive chat after SDK upgrade
- Symptom: 95p TTF jumps from 120ms to 800ms, users report UI lag.
- Root causes to check (in order):
  1. SDK default batch window increased (check config: batchWindowMs).
  2. Client-side streaming disabled (client using blocking generate call).
  3. New kernel selection caused model to run with cold KV cache due to mixing quantized and FP variants in batch.
- Canonical fixes:
  - Confirm SDK config: set batchWindowMs=12 for chat-tier clients and enable streaming=true.
  - Add batching grouping key: model_name + seq_len_bucket.
  - Rollback the SDK library to previous minor if fix in SDK is needed; escalate to runtime if kernel changes are implicated.

Issue B: Spikes of 429s when autoscaling events happen
- Symptom: Short periods of 429s followed by recovery; usually at scale events during deployments.
- Root causes to check:
  1. Global autoscaler lag on Dedicated pools.
  2. Burst window misconfiguration in client's reserved capacity settings.
  3. Clients not honoring Retry-After header.
- Canonical fixes:
  - Respect Retry-After and implement exponential backoff.
  - For Dedicated customers, validate burstCapacity=true settings and confirm SLA tier.
  - If rollout to new model variant caused capacity fragmentation, use canary rollout (see rollout section).

Issue C: Token-count mismatches and double-chunking in transcripts
- Symptom: Clients display repeated tokens when reconnecting to streams or after chunk stitching.
- Causes & fixes:
  - Ensure each chunk contains absolute token offsets. Use the provided stream_offset metadata to stitch deterministically.
  - Provide client-side deduplication based on token offset and request_id.

7) Troubleshooting checklist (for on-call & support)
1. Reproduce locally with the same SDK version and a captured request/response trace.
2. Check SDK config: batch_size, batchWindowMs, streaming flag, timeoutMs.
3. Inspect service logs for kernel fallbacks, 429 rates, and queue depth (trace tags: request_id, tenant_id).
4. If related to Dedicated: verify pool utilization and scaling events in eng-sre dashboard.
5. If regression after deployment: bisect via canary rollback (rollout id in trace).

8) Rollout and rollback guidance for SDK changes
- Canary strategy: start at 1% of traffic for 24 hours, increase to 10% for 6–12 hours, then 50% for 3–6 hours before full rollout. Monitor: 95p TTF, 99p error rate, 429 rate, and cost-per-1k tokens.
- Emergency rollback steps:
  1. Create rollback release in SDK package manager (npm/pypi/go proxy).
  2. Patch server-side routing policy to pin traffic to previous SDK-compatible model variant for flagged tenants.
  3. Notify affected customers and enable temporary Dedicated burst capacity if needed.

9) Observability and dashboards to add (minimum set)
- Per-SDK-version: 95p TTF, 95p request latency to first token, 99p error rate, and average batch size.
- Tracing tags: sdk_version, batch_id, streaming_session_id, request_id, model_variant.
- Alerts (recommended thresholds):
  - 95p TTF > 350ms for 5m -> P1 oncall notified.
  - 429 rate > 1% of requests for 10m -> P2.
  - Avg batch size < 50% of configured target for 5m (indicates fragmentation) -> investigate routing/grouping.

10) Example configuration snippets (YAML)
# client-config.yaml
sdk:
  version: v2
  streaming: true
  batchWindowMs: 12
  targetBatchSize: 16
  backoff:
    initialMs: 100
    factor: 2.0
    maxMs: 10000
  resume_on_disconnect: true

11) Ownership and escalation
- SDK patterns & templates: eng-developer-experience (primary).
- Runtime fallback and kernel issues: eng-serving-runtime (secondary).
- Scaling & Dedicated pool issues: eng-sre (ops).
- Oncall escalation path: support -> eng-developer-experience -> eng-serving-runtime -> eng-sre.

12) How to add a new canonical fix (process)
1. Create a PR in the docs repo with a reproducible test case and a short patch (code + test).
2. Add the issue to the ‘SDK canonical fixes’ project board and assign reviewers.
3. After merge, mark the doc updated and notify the support channel with reproducer.

Appendix A — Compact checklist card (copy/paste for Slack)
Title: SDK streaming/batching incident triage\- Step 1: Capture request_id and SDK version\- Step 2: Run local repro via redwood-cli with same flags\- Step 3: Check batchWindowMs and streaming flag in client config\- Step 4: Inspect service traces for kernel fallback or 429s\- Step 5: Apply canonical fix if matched; escalate if not

Appendix B — Sample request/response excerpt (JSON)
{ "request_id": "req-9a3b2f", "model": "redwood-2x", "batch_id": "b-123", "chunks": [ { "offset": 0, "text": "Hello ", "seq": 1 }, { "offset": 6, "text": "world!", "seq": 2 } ] }

Notes & history:
- 2024-11-03: first internal draft created during cross-team postmortem for chat regressions.
- 2025-06-10: added hybrid streaming recommendation to improve UX on long-form outputs.
- 2026-02-18: public release of playbook with canonical fixes and default config recommendations.

Contact:
- eng-developer-experience channel on Slack (#eng-devx) and doc owner Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.ai).

Related artifacts:
- redwood-cli (internal): repo link in platform wiki.
- SDK test harness: /infra/ci/loadtests/sdk-loadtest-suite (internal link).
text:
Summary:
This playbook consolidates recommended SDK patterns for streaming + batching usage with Redwood Inference APIs, a short set of quickstart templates for client teams, and canonical fixes for the top support issues we observe in the field. The goal is to reduce token cost without compromising latency SLOs and to provide reproducible steps for common incidents.

Scope:
- SDKs covered: python, node, and go (patterns are portable).
- Traffic modes: low-latency single-request streaming, throughput-oriented batched synchronous requests, hybrid streaming-with-batch setups.
- Not covered: model tuning, model architecture changes.

Intended audience:
- Application engineers integrating Redwood APIs.
- SDK maintainers and support engineers.

1) Quick primer: when to stream vs batch
- Stream when per-request latency (time-to-first-token) is the priority (chat, UI feedback, interactive assistants). Target: TTF token < 200 ms for UI-grade experience.
- Batch when throughput and cost-per-token are the priority (offline generation, high-concurrency inference pipelines). Target: cost efficiency at the expense of slightly higher tail latency.
- Hybrid: use streaming for first N tokens to satisfy TTF SLO and then switch to batched generation for long outputs.

2) Recommended defaults (opinionated)
- Per-tenant latency SLO: 95th percentile TTF < 250ms (hosted tier). Track via console traces and token-level metrics.
- Default batch size by workload (recommendation table):
| Workload Type | Preferred batch size | Max acceptable batch | Notes |
| --- | ---: | ---: | --- |
| Short chat turns (<= 128 tokens) | 8 | 16 | Keep small to preserve TTF |
| Document embedding (dense) | 64 | 256 | Throughput-oriented; use asynchronous workers |
| Long form generation (>512 tokens) | 4 | 8 | Use streaming + chunking for UX |
| Reranking (many short sequences) | 32 | 128 | Use vectorized scoring endpoints when available |

3) SDK quickstart templates
- Python streaming quickstart (minimal):
```python
from redwood import RedwoodClient
client = RedwoodClient(api_key="$REDWOOD_KEY")
# lightweight stream handler
with client.chat.stream(model="redwood-2x", conversation_id="ui-123") as stream:
    stream.send({"role":"user","content":"Summarize latest sales deck"})
    for chunk in stream.receive():
        # update UI chunk-by-chunk
        print(chunk.text, end="", flush=True)
```

- Node batch generator (worker):
```js
const { RedwoodClient } = require('@redwood/inference');
const client = new RedwoodClient({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_KEY });
async function processBatch(inputs){
  // inputs: [{prompt, meta}, ...]
  const res = await client.generate.batch({model: 'open-rlhf-1', inputs, batchSize: 32, timeoutMs: 30000});
  return res.outputs;
}
```

- CLI to reproduce throughput locally (load test):
$ redwood-cli loadtest --model redwood-2x --concurrency 32 --rps 200 --prompt-file ./samples/prompts.txt --batch-size 16

4) Streaming implementation notes (SDK authors)
- Implement backpressure: when network or downstream UI cannot process chunks fast enough, provide a pause/resume API to the consumer.
- Idempotency: attach a client-generated request_id to streaming sessions so reconnecting clients can resume from the last acknowledged token.
- Heartbeats: emit heartbeat frames every 10s of inactivity to keep connections alive behind proxies/load balancers.
- Chunk size: prefer many small chunks (< 2 KB) for interactive UX; combine tokens only when network overhead is high.

5) Batching implementation notes (SDK + service rules)
- Continuous batching window: 8–20ms for interactive workloads; 50–200ms for higher throughput jobs. Tunable via SDK config.
- Max batch composition rules: do not mix model families with incompatible KV cache sizes in the same batch. SDK must group by model variant, seq_len, and quantization profile.
- Backoff policy: when the service returns 429 or capacity fallback, SDK should implement a jittered exponential backoff with max backoff 10s and initial backoff 50–150ms.

6) Top observed issues and canonical fixes (support playbook)
Issue A: Very high TTF for interactive chat after SDK upgrade
- Symptom: 95p TTF jumps from 120ms to 800ms, users report UI lag.
- Root causes to check (in order):
  1. SDK default batch window increased (check config: batchWindowMs).
  2. Client-side streaming disabled (client using blocking generate call).
  3. New kernel selection caused model to run with cold KV cache due to mixing quantized and FP variants in batch.
- Canonical fixes:
  - Confirm SDK config: set batchWindowMs=12 for chat-tier clients and enable streaming=true.
  - Add batching grouping key: model_name + seq_len_bucket.
  - Rollback the SDK library to previous minor if fix in SDK is needed; escalate to runtime if kernel changes are implicated.

Issue B: Spikes of 429s when autoscaling events happen
- Symptom: Short periods of 429s followed by recovery; usually at scale events during deployments.
- Root causes to check:
  1. Global autoscaler lag on Dedicated pools.
  2. Burst window misconfiguration in client's reserved capacity settings.
  3. Clients not honoring Retry-After header.
- Canonical fixes:
  - Respect Retry-After and implement exponential backoff.
  - For Dedicated customers, validate burstCapacity=true settings and confirm SLA tier.
  - If rollout to new model variant caused capacity fragmentation, use canary rollout (see rollout section).

Issue C: Token-count mismatches and double-chunking in transcripts
- Symptom: Clients display repeated tokens when reconnecting to streams or after chunk stitching.
- Causes & fixes:
  - Ensure each chunk contains absolute token offsets. Use the provided stream_offset metadata to stitch deterministically.
  - Provide client-side deduplication based on token offset and request_id.

7) Troubleshooting checklist (for on-call & support)
1. Reproduce locally with the same SDK version and a captured request/response trace.
2. Check SDK config: batch_size, batchWindowMs, streaming flag, timeoutMs.
3. Inspect service logs for kernel fallbacks, 429 rates, and queue depth (trace tags: request_id, tenant_id).
4. If related to Dedicated: verify pool utilization and scaling events in eng-sre dashboard.
5. If regression after deployment: bisect via canary rollback (rollout id in trace).

8) Rollout and rollback guidance for SDK changes
- Canary strategy: start at 1% of traffic for 24 hours, increase to 10% for 6–12 hours, then 50% for 3–6 hours before full rollout. Monitor: 95p TTF, 99p error rate, 429 rate, and cost-per-1k tokens.
- Emergency rollback steps:
  1. Create rollback release in SDK package manager (npm/pypi/go proxy).
  2. Patch server-side routing policy to pin traffic to previous SDK-compatible model variant for flagged tenants.
  3. Notify affected customers and enable temporary Dedicated burst capacity if needed.

9) Observability and dashboards to add (minimum set)
- Per-SDK-version: 95p TTF, 95p request latency to first token, 99p error rate, and average batch size.
- Tracing tags: sdk_version, batch_id, streaming_session_id, request_id, model_variant.
- Alerts (recommended thresholds):
  - 95p TTF > 350ms for 5m -> P1 oncall notified.
  - 429 rate > 1% of requests for 10m -> P2.
  - Avg batch size < 50% of configured target for 5m (indicates fragmentation) -> investigate routing/grouping.

10) Example configuration snippets (YAML)
# client-config.yaml
sdk:
  version: v2
  streaming: true
  batchWindowMs: 12
  targetBatchSize: 16
  backoff:
    initialMs: 100
    factor: 2.0
    maxMs: 10000
  resume_on_disconnect: true

11) Ownership and escalation
- SDK patterns & templates: eng-developer-experience (primary).
- Runtime fallback and kernel issues: eng-serving-runtime (secondary).
- Scaling & Dedicated pool issues: eng-sre (ops).
- Oncall escalation path: support -> eng-developer-experience -> eng-serving-runtime -> eng-sre.

12) How to add a new canonical fix (process)
1. Create a PR in the docs repo with a reproducible test case and a short patch (code + test).
2. Add the issue to the ‘SDK canonical fixes’ project board and assign reviewers.
3. After merge, mark the doc updated and notify the support channel with reproducer.

Appendix A — Compact checklist card (copy/paste for Slack)
Title: SDK streaming/batching incident triage\- Step 1: Capture request_id and SDK version\- Step 2: Run local repro via redwood-cli with same flags\- Step 3: Check batchWindowMs and streaming flag in client config\- Step 4: Inspect service traces for kernel fallback or 429s\- Step 5: Apply canonical fix if matched; escalate if not

Appendix B — Sample request/response excerpt (JSON)
{ "request_id": "req-9a3b2f", "model": "redwood-2x", "batch_id": "b-123", "chunks": [ { "offset": 0, "text": "Hello ", "seq": 1 }, { "offset": 6, "text": "world!", "seq": 2 } ] }

Notes & history:
- 2024-11-03: first internal draft created during cross-team postmortem for chat regressions.
- 2025-06-10: added hybrid streaming recommendation to improve UX on long-form outputs.
- 2026-02-18: public release of playbook with canonical fixes and default config recommendations.

Contact:
- eng-developer-experience channel on Slack (#eng-devx) and doc owner Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.ai).

Related artifacts:
- redwood-cli (internal): repo link in platform wiki.
- SDK test harness: /infra/ci/loadtests/sdk-loadtest-suite (internal link).

title: Developer runtime snippet kits and publish playbook
preview:
Summary

This page is a compact, production-oriented kit for Developer Experience (DX) engineers and SDK maintainers: a set of vetted runtime snippets, small quickstart templates, documentation-publishing checks, and a canonical troubleshooting list for the most frequent developer-reported inference integration problems. The goal is a single place to grab copy-paste-ready code and followable publish/run/playback steps that keep examples consistent across languages and the docs site.

Who should use this

- SDK owners and maintainers updating examples or adding new SDK surfaces.
- Documentation authors publishing live examples to the docs site.
- Support engineers triaging reproducible developer bugs.
- Solutions engineers building customer PoCs who need reliable starter templates.

When to pick a kit vs full quickstart

1. Use a snippet kit when you need short, 15–60 line, runnable examples embedded inline in docs.
2. Use a quickstart when the developer needs a multi-file project (local dev harness, docker-compose, CI checks).
3. Use the publish playbook when merging changes that include live examples, redirects, or new code sandboxes.

The kit (what's included)

- Language snippets: Python, TypeScript, and Curl for all common flows (init, streaming, embeddings, function/tool calling).
- Small templates: local-dev harness, retry/idempotency wrapper, small serverless handler (FastAPI / Next.js API route).
- Docs site checklist: static validation commands, snippet test harness commands, and the merge/publish sequence.
- Canonical fixes: curated fixes for the top 8 reproducible support issues tied to examples.

Usage examples (copy/paste)

1) Minimal Python initialization + streaming example

```python
# file: examples/python/streaming_init.py
import redwood  # placeholder package name used in SDK examples

client = redwood.Client(api_key="${REDWOOD_API_KEY}")

with client.stream_completions(model="redwood-1.1-large") as stream:
    stream.send_prompt("Summarize the following text:
- Keep it under 50 words.")
    for chunk in stream.iter_text():
        print(chunk, end="")

# Notes: run with: REDWOOD_API_KEY=sk_... python streaming_init.py
```

2) TypeScript: retry wrapper + small inference helper (Node 18+)

```ts
// file: examples/ts/retry-helper.ts
import { RedwoodClient } from '@redwood-inference/sdk';

const client = new RedwoodClient({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY });

export async function callWithRetry(payload: object, retries = 2) {
  for (let attempt = 0; attempt <= retries; attempt++) {
    try {
      return await client.completions.create(payload);
    } catch (err) {
      if (attempt === retries) throw err;
      // exponential backoff (simple)
      await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 200 * Math.pow(2, attempt)));
    }
  }
}
```

3) Curl example to reproduce a bad tokenization/encoding issue

```bash
curl -X POST 
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" 
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
  https://api.redwood-inference.internal/v1/completions 
  -d '{"model":"redwood-1.1-large","input":"Emoji test: 😊 — make sure it returns UTF-8 safe output"}'
```

Docs site operational notes (publish playbook)

Follow these steps when merging documentation changes that include runnable code snippets or new quickstarts: 

1) Local validation (author)
- Run snippet lint: tools/snippet-lint --path examples/ --format markdown
- Execute snippet smoke tests: ./tools/snippet-test-runner --languages python,ts --files examples/
- Confirm all sandboxed calls use the TEST_API_KEY (not personal prod keys).

2) CI gates (automated)
- CI runs: snippet-test-runner (containerized); failures block merges.
- Create a sandbox recording: tools/create-sandbox-recording --flow create_completions --seed 42. Store recording id in PR description.

3) Review (manual)
- Code/Doc owners must verify: language parity (each highlighted feature appears in at least two languages), and performance notes (e.g., expected latency for the snippet on shared sandbox).

4) Staging publish
- Push to docs-staging: ./scripts/publish-docs --staging --include-examples. The staging site will be reachable at https://staging.docs.redwood.local.
- Run the staged smoke tests: tools/staged-smoke --recording <id>. This verifies snippet output shape and no flaky network calls.

5) Production rollout
- After approvals, merge to main; CI will run full publish and invalidate cache.
- Monitor Docs Health dashboard for 24 hours for snippet-related errors tagged docs-snippet.*.

Snippet testing harness (how it works)

- Each snippet directory includes metadata.yaml with: language, run-command, run-timeout, mocked-responses (optional). Example:
runtime: python
run-command: REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY python streaming_init.py
run-timeout: 30s

- The harness runs snippets inside ephemeral containers and captures stdout/stderr and exit code. If a snippet uses network, it must either be recorded or explicitly marked as "allow-network: true" (rare).

Top reported issues (canonical fixes)

These are the eight most frequent problems encountered when developers follow examples. Each entry has a reproducible symptom, root cause, and canonical fix they can perform themselves.

1) Authentication 401 in example runs
- Symptom: Curl or SDK sample returns 401 with message "Invalid API key".
- Root cause: Example used a placeholder value or an expired test key; env var not exported in the shell.
- Fix: export REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY; ensure CI uses masked secrets. Update snippet header to show run command.

2) Streaming snippets hang indefinitely
- Symptom: Example process does not exit when stream completes.
- Root cause: Missing graceful-close/await on the streaming iterator; some SDKs require stream.close().
- Fix: Add explicit close/await in the snippet and update inline comment. Add a 60s timeout in snippet metadata.

3) Encoding/emoji corruption in curl examples
- Symptom: Emojis or special unicode render as question marks.
- Root cause: Incorrect Content-Type or curl not using --data-binary.
- Fix: Use -H 'Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8' and --data-binary instead of -d. Confirm container locale is UTF-8.

4) Mismatched parameter names across SDKs (model vs model_name)
- Symptom: Python example uses "model" while TypeScript example used "model_name" causing confusion.
- Root cause: SDK surface drift during a refactor.
- Fix: Align to canonical parameter names: use "model" everywhere. Add a schema lint that flags parameter-name differences.

5) CI snippet tests intermittently fail due to rate limits
- Symptom: Tests fail in CI with 429 errors.
- Root cause: Shared sandbox exhausted ephemeral throughput.
- Fix: Add retries in snippet harness and set TEST_QPS cap via env var. For CI, allocate parallel sandbox tokens.

6) Local quickstart fails with Docker overlay error
- Symptom: docker-compose up fails on Mac with overlayfs or permission denied.
- Root cause: Host filesystem permissions or conflicting local docker versions.
- Fix: Document known Mac workarounds in README (chmod the project dir, use docker-desktop > 4.0). Provide a non-root compose variant in templates.

7) Examples leak real keys into recorded artifacts
- Symptom: Sandbox recordings contain non-masked API keys.
- Root cause: Author ran snippet locally with a prod key when creating recording.
- Fix: Reject PRs that include recordings with unmasked secrets. Add a pre-commit hook to scrub keys (tools/scrub-recordings).

8) Examples produce different text across runs (flaky outputs)
- Symptom: Assertion on example output shape fails due to slight variation.
- Root cause: Using deterministic seeds not set or relying on non-deterministic logit sampling.
- Fix: Use deterministic parameters in CI (temperature=0, top_k/top_p fixed) for recorded assertions; in inline docs, call out that outputs are stochastic.

Ownership and escalation

- Example owners: 
  - Python snippets: @priya (owner)
  - TypeScript snippets: @ethan (owner)
  - Docs publishing: @maria (owner)
- If CI snippet test failures are systemic, open an incident in eng-developer-experience and tag "docs-snippet-failure"; SRE will investigate infrastructure quotas or container flakiness.

Template catalog (short descriptions)

- local-dev-harness/ : docker-compose, seeded sandbox, env examples. 
- retry-wrapper/ : language-specific retry logic + best-practice comments.
- serverless-handler/ : minimal server-side snippet for hosting a model call (FastAPI and Next.js).
- embedding-starter/ : sample for embeddings + simple vector store (sqlite) + example search.

Appendix: common commands and test vectors

- Run all snippet tests locally: ./tools/snippet-test-runner --all --languages python,ts
- Create a test sandbox: tools/create-sandbox --ttl 2h --seed 12345
- Quick smoke (python): REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY python examples/python/streaming_init.py
- Quick smoke (ts): REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY node examples/ts/retry-helper.ts
- Validate docs build: yarn workspace docs build --check-examples

Frequently asked questions

Q: What is the recommended snippet length?
A: Keep inline snippets under 60 lines (ideally 15–40). For longer flows, link to the quickstart project.

Q: Are recorded sandbox outputs allowed in PRs?
A: Yes — recordings are encouraged for CI stability. Always scrub keys and mark recordings with the sandbox id.

Q: How do we ensure language parity?
A: For any new API surface or feature, publish at least two language examples (Python + JS preferred). Add a docs-lint rule that checks presence.

Related resources

- tools/snippet-test-runner README (internal)
- docs authoring guide: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/docs-site-content-lifecycle-and-local-preview-2026
- API contract: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/api-standards/

Change log

- 2025-10-12: Draft created (initial set of snippets and smoke-run notes).
- 2025-11-30: Added retry-wrapper and staging publish steps.
- 2026-02-18: Added canonical fixes list and tightened CI expectations.

If you have a snippet you want added or a recurring support pattern to include, file a PR against this directory and ping the reviewer group listed above.

- tools/snippet-test-runner README (internal)
- docs authoring guide: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/docs-site-content-lifecycle-and-local-preview-2026
- API contract: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/api-standards/

Change log

- 2025-10-12: Draft created (initial set of snippets and smoke-run notes).
- 2025-11-30: Added retry-wrapper and staging publish steps.

If you have a snippet you want added or a recurring support pattern to include, file a PR against this directory and ping the reviewer group listed above.

title: Top Support Issues (Q1 2026): Themes, Root Causes, and Known-Issues Sprint Shortlist
preview:
## Purpose
This page summarizes the highest-volume and highest-severity Support themes observed in Q1 2026 (to date) and recommends documentation/UX changes to reduce time-to-triage and repeat tickets. It also records the shortlist selected for the Feb 2026 “known-issues sprint” and the rationale for prioritization.

This document is written for DevEx/Docs, Support Ops, and engineering partners (SRE/Platform/Runtime). It is intentionally customer-safe (no customer names, no contract terms beyond tier-level policy, no sensitive incident details).

---

## Time window + data sources
**Time window analyzed:** 2026-01-01 through 2026-02-12 (rolling Q1 view; refreshed during sprint review on 2026-02-14).

**Data sources:**
- Jira Support project (SUP tickets) with Priority (P0–P4), Tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private/Enterprise), Region, Model, and “Repeat issue?” flags.
- Slack bridges (#support, #incidents) for qualitative signal on where escalation context is missing.
- DevEx/docs feedback from Support macro refresh (snippets) and SDK troubleshooting requests.
- Engineering follow-ups in Linear for issues with clear product fixes.

**Method notes / limitations:**
- Ticket counts below represent *unique issues* (deduped by symptom + root cause when known). For example, multiple customer tickets about streaming disconnects with different client stacks are grouped when symptoms match.
- Severity is based on Support Priority and whether an incident was opened or SRE was paged.
- We intentionally separate “needs product fix” vs “can be mitigated with guidance” to drive the known-issues sprint.

---

## Executive summary
Across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private, the same pattern repeats: tickets spike when customers encounter a symptom that (a) has an immediate workaround, but (b) is not discoverable, copy/paste-ready, or consistent across SDKs and Support macros.

**Top themes (by combined volume + severity):**
1) **Streaming disconnects/timeouts/reconnect behavior** (Hosted + Enterprise): high ticket volume, high back-and-forth due to missing request IDs and unclear retry guidance.
2) **429 quota/rate-limit confusion after SDK changes** (Hosted): medium-high volume, drives repeated follow-ups and distrust in “headers vs limits.”
3) **Dedicated autoscaling instability (oscillation / churn)** (Dedicated): lower volume but disproportionately high severity and escalation frequency.
4) **Private installer upgrades stuck at migrations / rollback safety** (Private): low volume but critical when it occurs; requires standardized diagnostics bundle.
5) **Audit log delivery latency expectations** (Enterprise/Private): medium volume; the gap is “what’s normal vs abnormal” plus missing first-class lag signal.

**Key recommendation:** publish a curated, tagged “Known Issues & Workarounds” hub, then back-link it from:
- Support intake checklist + macros
- SDK troubleshooting sections
- Console tooltips/error messages where applicable

This is the fastest path to reducing repeat tickets while longer-term engineering fixes land.

---

## Top issues snapshot (ranked)
The table below ranks issues by a weighted score: **Score = (Ticket volume × 1) + (P0/P1 count × 4) + (Escalations/incident bridges × 6)**.

| Rank | Theme (short) | Primary tier(s) | Est. tickets (Q1-to-date) | P0/P1 count | Common trigger | Current state | Primary lever |
|---:|---|---|---:|---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Streaming disconnects / truncated streams | Hosted, Enterprise | 28 | 7 | Proxy/LB idle timeouts, client timeout defaults, retry behavior | Workaround available; docs inconsistent across SDKs | Docs + SDK examples + better error messaging |
| 2 | 429 quota mismatch / confusing rate-limit headers | Hosted | 21 | 3 | SDK upgrade, burst traffic, mixed endpoints | Engineering fix in progress; workaround needed | Product fix + KB + Support macro |
| 3 | Dedicated autoscaling oscillation → latency spikes | Dedicated | 9 | 4 | Scale-down too aggressive, cooldown misconfig, burst patterns | Workaround/config guidance available; guardrails WIP | KB + console/dashboard guidance + guardrails |
| 4 | Private upgrade stuck at migrations | Private | 6 | 3 | Installer version mismatch, schema migration lock, cluster resource pressure | Needs standardized diagnostics + rollback steps | Product diagnostics + KB |
| 5 | Audit log delivery latency (30+ min) | Enterprise, Private | 11 | 2 | Pipeline backlog or downstream export contention | Need expectations + lag metric/alert + KB | Observability + KB |
| 6 | Model version pin “ignored” during fallback | Dedicated, Enterprise | 7 | 2 | Capacity constraint triggers fallback; misunderstanding of pin policy | Needs clearer routing semantics and support-facing signals | Metrics + KB + console copy |
| 7 | Embeddings timeouts in eu-west | Hosted | 8 | 2 | Regional degradation, client timeout defaults | Mostly incident-driven; limited general KB today | Runbook links + escalation triggers |
| 8 | SAML SSO login loop (Console) | Enterprise | 5 | 2 | IdP config drift, metadata mismatch | Troubleshooting exists but fragmented | Consolidated doc + UI hinting |
| 9 | Rerank 500s on large batches | Hosted | 6 | 1 | Payload size, batch sizing | Quick workaround exists; not discoverable | KB + SDK sample |
| 10 | Structured output validation errors confusing | Hosted | 6 | 0 | Schema mismatch, strict mode | Needs clearer docs/error messages | Docs + product error improvements |

---

## Issue deep-dives (what Support is seeing + what we should change)

### 1) Streaming disconnects, timeouts, and reconnect semantics
**What customers report (common language):**
- “Stream resets mid-generation”
- “Truncated output; connection closes without a clear error”
- “Retries create duplicate side effects / double charges”

**What Support needs to resolve quickly (currently missing in too many tickets):**
- Request IDs (server-generated) and client correlation IDs
- Region, model, SDK version, HTTP library/proxy details
- Whether HTTP/2 is enabled; idle timeout settings; keepalive settings

**Why it repeats:** guidance is split across Slack tribal knowledge, SDK examples, and ad-hoc macros; customers don’t have a single authoritative “do this first” checklist.

**Recommended changes:**
- Publish a dedicated KB article with:
  - Symptom checklist
  - Safe retry + backoff guidance (including idempotency notes)
  - Client timeout defaults and recommended overrides
  - Temporary mitigations (e.g., adjust keepalive; if applicable, disable HTTP/2 where intermediaries are buggy)
  - “When to escalate” criteria mapped to Support P0/P1 triggers
- Add SDK troubleshooting sections that match the KB language verbatim (Python/TS/Go).
- Improve server-side error surfaces to include actionable hints + request ID propagation.

**Related work:**
- KB: workaround-streaming-timeouts-and-reconnect
- Linear: ENG-4822
- GitHub: redwood-docs/pr-2843, redwood/pr-19321

---

### 2) 429s, quota enforcement edge cases, and rate-limit header interpretation
**What customers report:**
- “We’re under our monthly commitment but getting 429”
- “Headers show remaining tokens but next call is rate-limited”
- “After upgrading the SDK, 429 rate increased”

**Common root causes (clustered):**
- Confusion between quota (account-level) vs rate limits (burst/concurrency)
- Client-side concurrency changes after SDK upgrade
- Header inconsistency in specific edge cases (known engineering bug)

**Recommended changes:**
- Publish a KB article explaining:
  - Difference between quota vs rate limiting
  - How to interpret rate-limit headers (and what’s authoritative)
  - Common SDK misconfigurations (retries amplifying load, concurrency defaults)
  - Support validation steps (what telemetry to check)
- Ensure Support macros include a short “copy/paste” explanation and a request for minimal diagnostics.
- Land engineering fix + regression tests, then update KB to mark workaround as “no longer needed” for patched versions.

**Related work:**
- KB: workaround-429-quota-mismatch-and-rate-limit-headers
- Linear: ENG-4825
- GitHub: redwood/pr-19322

---

### 3) Dedicated autoscaling oscillation causing latency spikes
**What customers report:**
- “Latency spikes every few minutes”
- “Autoscaler keeps adding/removing replicas; throughput unstable”

**Why it escalates:** when Dedicated is unstable, customers experience business-critical impact and expect faster, more prescriptive guidance than “we’re investigating.”

**Recommended changes:**
- Publish a Dedicated-specific KB article with:
  - How to identify oscillation in dashboards
  - Config knobs: min replicas, scale-down cooldown, target utilization
  - When to involve SRE/capacity planning (escalation triggers)
- Provide Support-safe canonical dashboard links (“quick view”).
- Land guardrails to prevent harmful configurations.

**Related work:**
- KB: workaround-dedicated-autoscaling-oscillation
- Linear: ENG-4823, ENG-4829

---

### 4) Private installer upgrade failures (stuck migrations) and safe rollback
**What customers report:**
- “Upgrade stuck at migrations for hours”
- “Installer errors are vague; unclear what to collect”

**Recommended changes:**
- Publish a Private upgrade KB article that is:
  - Explicit about safe rollback and “stop now” conditions
  - Explicit about artifacts to collect (installer logs, cluster events)
  - Explicit “when to escalate” and correct owning team
- Land an automated diagnostics bundle to reduce back-and-forth.

**Related work:**
- KB: workaround-private-installer-upgrade-stuck-migrations
- Linear: ENG-4828
- GitHub: redwood-private-installer/pr-566

---

### 5) Audit log delivery latency: expectations, mitigation, and confirming backlog
**What customers report:**
- “Audit logs are delayed 30–60 minutes”
- “Is this within SLO?”
- “We need proof it’s being worked on / monitored”

**Key gap:** customers (and Support) lack a crisp definition of “expected delivery window” vs “degradation,” and we lack a first-class lag metric visible to on-call and Support.

**Recommended changes:**
- Publish KB article that includes:
  - Expected delivery SLOs (tier-aware, contract-safe language)
  - How customers should implement retries/polling
  - How Support confirms pipeline backlog (without exposing sensitive infra detail)
- Add audit log lag metric + alert thresholds aligned with enterprise expectations.

**Related work:**
- KB: workaround-audit-log-delivery-latency
- Linear: ENG-4824
- GitHub: observability-pack/pr-711

---

## Common cross-cutting problems (and how we fix them once)

### A) Missing “minimum diagnostics” on intake
**Observed:** many P1/P2 tickets spend 3–5 messages just gathering region/model/SDK version/request IDs.

**Fix:** enforce required fields + prompt via Jira automation and macros.
- Add required fields on SUP creation/triage (region, model, SDK version, request IDs if available).
- Auto-suggest known issues index based on tags (streaming/429/audit-log/etc.).

### B) Inconsistent language across Support, KB, and SDK docs
**Observed:** different teams describe the same thing using different terms (“quota” vs “limit”, “disconnect” vs “reset”, “fallback” vs “routing”).

**Fix:** treat the KB articles as the source of truth and copy the same phrasing into:
- Support macros/snippets
- SDK troubleshooting
- Console help text / error messages where feasible

### C) “When to escalate” guidance is absent or too vague
**Observed:** Support either escalates too early (noise) or too late (customer impact) because escalation triggers are not embedded where people work.

**Fix:** every known-issue article must have a clearly labeled “When to escalate” section tied to the severity matrix.

---

## Known-issues sprint shortlist (Feb 2026) + rationale
We selected the following as sprint targets because they meet all three criteria:
1) **High impact** (P0/P1 frequency and/or escalation likelihood)
2) **Actionable workaround available** (validated by SRE/Eng)
3) **Repeatable** (customers can self-serve if guidance is clear)

| Shortlist item | Why it made the cut | Deliverable | Owner (Docs) | Reviewers |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Streaming timeouts/disconnects & reconnect | Highest volume; high friction; clear mitigations | KB + SDK troubleshooting + improved errors | Wei Chen | Dev Patel, Sean Gallagher |
| 429 quota mismatch & headers | Repeat confusion; product fix in flight; need interim clarity | KB + macro + post-fix update plan | Wei Chen | Ethan Park, Priya Shah |
| Dedicated autoscaling oscillation | High-sev for Dedicated; clear config guidance | KB + dashboard pointers | Kira Thompson | Sean Gallagher, Allison Grant |
| Private upgrade stuck migrations | Critical for Private; needs safe rollback steps | KB + diagnostics bundle pointer | Kira Thompson | Priya Natarajan, Naomi Feldman |
| Audit log delivery latency | Enterprise expectation gap; needs metric + clear comms | KB + metric/alert + comms alignment | Kira Thompson | Rachel Kim, Allison Grant |

**Not shortlisted (tracked for follow-up):**
- Model version pin vs fallback semantics (needs metrics + policy clarity; partially addressed via routing metrics improvements)
- Structured output validation errors (primarily docs + product error improvements; lower severity)
- Rerank 500s on large batches (good candidate for next wave; needs a short KB article + SDK example)
- SAML SSO login loops (needs consolidated enterprise auth troubleshooting page; requires careful customer-safe phrasing)

---

## Proposed docs/UX fixes (backlog, with suggested owners)

### Docs / information architecture
- **Known Issues & Workarounds hub** with tags, recency, and “escalate” callouts.
  - Owner: DevEx/Docs (Elliot Price)
  - Status: in progress (ENG-4826)

- **Cross-linking**: from Support internal pages and SDK troubleshooting sections.
  - Owner: DevEx/Docs

### SDK documentation + examples
- Streaming: safe retries, timeouts, idempotency guidance.
  - Owner: SDK maintainers + DevEx
  - Status: in progress (ENG-4827)

### Product / UX improvements (non-doc)
- Better gateway/runtime error messages for streaming disconnects.
  - Owner: Runtime team
  - Status: in progress (ENG-4822)

- Fix quota enforcement edge case and header consistency.
  - Owner: Platform control plane
  - Status: in progress (ENG-4825)

- Audit log lag metric + alert + dashboard.
  - Owner: Observability/SRE
  - Status: in progress (ENG-4824)

---

## Metrics to track (post-sprint)
We will measure whether these changes reduce repeat tickets and speed up mitigation.

**Primary metrics (Support-owned):**
- Ticket deflection: % of inbound issues resolved by linking KB without escalation.
- Repeat rate: number of tickets per theme per week after publication.
- Time-to-triage: time from ticket creation to “root cause suspected” or “workaround sent.”

**Secondary metrics (Engineering/DevEx):**
- Reduction in “need request ID/region/model” follow-up messages.
- For streaming: decrease in tickets missing request IDs (target: +30% improvement within 4 weeks).
- For audit logs: fewer escalations solely to confirm backlog (target: -50% within 6 weeks, contingent on lag metric rollout).

---

## Action items
1) Publish the shortlist KB articles and ensure they are linked from the known-issues index.
2) Update Support macros to point to the new KB pages and to enforce the minimum diagnostics.
3) Land SDK troubleshooting sections for streaming and cross-link to KB.
4) After 2–3 weeks, review metrics and decide next-wave shortlist (likely: rerank large batches, structured output validation errors, model pin vs fallback semantics).

---

## Appendix: example “minimum diagnostics” template (customer-safe)
When requesting details, keep it minimal and consistent:
- Region (e.g., us-east, eu-west)
- Endpoint (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank)
- Model + pinned version (if applicable)
- SDK + version (or raw HTTP client)
- Request ID(s) and timestamps (UTC)
- Whether streaming is enabled; any proxy/load balancer in between

This template is intentionally aligned with the Support intake checklist and the on-call request for standardized paging context.

- Audit log lag metric + alert + dashboard.
  - Owner: Observability/SRE
  - Status: in progress (ENG-4824)

---

## Metrics to track (post-sprint)
We will measure whether these changes reduce repeat tickets and speed up mitigation.

**Primary metrics (Support-owned):**
- Ticket deflection: % of inbound issues resolved by linking KB without escalation.
- Repeat rate: number of tickets per theme per week after publication.
- Time-to-triage: time from ticket creation to “root cause suspected” or “workaround sent.”

**Secondary metrics (Engineering/DevEx):**
- Reduction in “need request ID/region/model” follow-up messages.
- For streaming: decrease in tickets missing request IDs (target: +30% improvement within 4 weeks).
- For audit logs: fewer escalations solely to confirm backlog (target: -50% within 6 weeks, contingent on lag metric rollout).

---

## Action items
1) Publish the shortlist KB articles and ensure they are linked from the known-issues index.
2) Update Support macros to point to the new KB pages and to enforce the minimum diagnostics.
3) Land SDK troubleshooting sections for streaming and cross-link to KB.
4) After 2–3 weeks, review metrics and decide next-wave shortlist (likely: rerank large batches, structured output validation errors, model pin vs fallback semantics).

---

## Appendix: example “minimum diagnostics” template (customer-safe)
When requesting details, keep it minimal and consistent:
- Region (e.g., us-east, eu-west)
- Endpoint (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank)
- Model + pinned version (if applicable)
- SDK + version (or raw HTTP client)
- Request ID(s) and timestamps (UTC)
- Whether streaming is enabled; any proxy/load balancer in between

This template is intentionally aligned with the Support intake checklist and the on-call request for standardized paging context.

title: SDK Back-compatibility & Deprecation Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook defines Redwood's expectations, guardrails, and operational steps for SDK backward compatibility, controlled deprecation, and developer-facing migration tooling. It is intended for SDK maintainers, release engineers, developer docs authors, and support engineers who help customers migrate when an SDK behavior or API surface changes.

Goals
- Keep customer integrations stable: preserve commonly relied-on behavior across minor/patch releases.
- Provide predictable migration windows and clear communication for breaking changes.
- Deliver measurable tests and automated migrations where possible.
- Minimize support escalations with pre-release checks, canaries, and compatibility monitors.

Scope
- Applies to all Redwood client SDKs (JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, Go, Java, .NET) published by the Developer Experience team.
- Includes public SDK APIs, CLI tools distributed as SDKs, and canonical adapters maintained by Redwood.
- Does not cover internal-only helper packages prefixed with @redwood/internal (those follow internal change process).

Key definitions
- Backwards-compatible change: a non-breaking change that does not require code modifications for existing callers (e.g., adding optional parameters).
- Breaking change: any change that requires consumer code modification, configuration change, or different runtime expectations (e.g., renaming exported function, removing parameter, changing semantic behavior).
- Deprecation window: the announced minimum time between a public deprecation notice and removal.
- Golden tests: curated integration tests that exercise real-world SDK usage patterns against a live or simulated Redwood backend.

Policy summary (quick)
- Patch (x.y.z -> x.y.z+1): never introduce breaking changes.
- Minor (x.y -> x.y+1): may add new features and non-breaking changes; breaking changes require explicit major-version plan and opt-in beta first.
- Major (x -> x+1): breaking changes only after a minimum 90-day beta + opt-in period and communicated migration plan.
- Deprecation window: standard 90 days for runtime API removal; 180 days for endpoint/contract removals if customers depend on server-side behavior.

Minimum requirements before a breaking change is accepted
1. ADR or design doc summarizing rationale and migration approach (linked in release notes).
2. Migration guide (step-by-step) authored in docs repo and staged on dev docs site.
3. Automated codemod or sample patch where feasible (for JS/TS and Python we require a codemod unless impractical).
4. Golden test matrix updated and passing for both old and new behaviors.
5. Communication plan: email + release notes + in-console banner for customers on hosted API where applicable.

Operational playbook: releasing a deprecation + removal
1) Prepare
- Owner: SDK maintainer (team owner listed in SDK repository).
- Create PR: update changelog, add deprecation annotations in code, and add a deprecation note in the SDK README.
- Add codemod: place codemod scripts under tools/codemods/<sdk-name> with README and usage example.
- Run golden tests: include both old and new behavior runs in CI (see Compatibility Test Matrix below).

2) Announce (Day 0)
- Publish a deprecation notice in the release notes and the SDK docs page. Template subject line: "[Deprecation] <feature> in <sdk> will be removed on <date> — migration guidance included."
- Post in #sdk-announcements Slack channel and notify Customer Success for customers with known usage.
- If hosted customers are affected, schedule in-console banner for 14-day visibility starting Day 0.

3) Beta / opt-in period (Day 7–Day 90)
- Publish a beta release (major-version-beta) tagged and documented.
- Encourage customers to try migration via the codemod and opt-in migration instructions.
- Support team labels incoming issues as "migration" for triage priority.

4) Pre-removal checks (Day 60–Day 90)
- Run canary telemetry to identify active customers still calling deprecated surface. If active high-value customers are impacted, extend window and coordinate 1:1 outreach.
- Ensure all QA/Golden tests pass against the major release.

5) Removal (earliest Day 90)
- Cut and publish the major release that removes the deprecated surface.
- Update release notes, SDK docs, and place a post-release note in Slack + customer success queue.
- Tag the breaking release in the SDK repo and add an entry to the breaking-changes registry in the shared docs.

Compatibility Test Matrix (example)
- Purpose: show guarantees we must validate before publishing an SDK change. This matrix is maintained in the SDK repo under /ci/compatibility-matrix.yml and must be human-readable in the release PR.

ASCII table (single-source of truth format):
| Test Type             | Target       | Runs For                    | Pass condition | Responsible |
|-----------------------|--------------|-----------------------------|----------------|-------------|
| Unit                  | SDK package  | Every PR                    | 100% green     | SDK CI      |
| Integration (golden)  | Backend v1   | PR to major branch, release | No regression  | SDK owner   |
| Golden backward smoke  | Backend v1   | Pre-release                 | Key flows pass  | DevExp QE   |
| Codemod dry-run       | Repo sample  | Before public beta          | No parse errors | SDK owner   |

Required CI checks (minimum)
- unit, lint, typecheck
- golden-integration-old (runs against current stable backend config)
- golden-integration-new (runs against target behavior)
- codemod-dry-run (exit non-zero on parse or crash)

Migration templates and examples
- JS/TS codemod example (jscodeshift usage):

  # Run codemod
  npx jscodeshift -t ./tools/codemods/rename-api.js src/**/*.ts --parser=ts --dry

- Python sample migration snippet (manual):

  # Before
  from redwood import Client
  c = Client(api_key=REDACTED)
  r = c.generate("Hello")

  # After (new method name and explicit model param)
  from redwood import RedwoodClient
  c = RedwoodClient(api_key=REDACTED, model="r1-small")
  r = c.generate_text(prompt="Hello")

Deprecation notice template (to publish in docs and email)
Title: Deprecation notice — <feature> in <sdk>
Summary: Brief one-line summary of what is being removed and why.
Timeline:
- Announcement: <YYYY-MM-DD>
- Beta period: <YYYY-MM-DD> -> <YYYY-MM-DD>
- Removal: <YYYY-MM-DD> (earliest)
Migration steps:
1. Run the codemod: <command>
2. Update imports: replace X with Y
3. Run compatibility suite: ./ci/run-golden.sh --matrix old,new
Support: For one-on-one help, open a support ticket with tag "sdk-migration" and include your repository and sample failing stack trace.

Support & common issues (top canonical fixes)
- Symptom: ImportError after upgrade
  - Root cause: package renamed; common when module layout changed between major versions.
  - Fix: update imports via codemod or manually: replace imports from redwood.client -> redwood.clientv2

- Symptom: Typing/type-check errors for TS users after minor release
  - Root cause: stricter types or new generic parameters added non-breakingly but exposed in d.ts.
  - Fix: suggest pinning to prior minor until migration guide applied, provide TS shim typings: declare module 'redwood' { export type LegacyClient = any }

- Symptom: Streaming responses behavior changed (chunking, event names)
  - Root cause: internal streaming protocol evolution.
  - Fix: provide compatibility adapter in SDK (see tools/compat/stream-adapter.js) and document temporary usage.

Rollout checklist (pre-release)
- [ ] ADR + migration guide published
- [ ] Codemod available and smoke-tested on at least two open-source consumer repos
- [ ] CI golden tests pass for old and new behavior
- [ ] Release notes and deprecation template drafted
- [ ] Customer Success notified and in-console banner scheduled (if needed)
- [ ] Support runbook created and triage label added

Ownership and escalation
- SDK owner (primary): maintains PR, codemod, and CI changes.
- SDK on-call (rotating): primary responder for 72 hours post-release.
- Escalation path: SDK owner -> DevEx EM -> Platform SRE if backend contract issue.

Metrics and telemetry to monitor during the window
- Number of active calls to deprecated endpoint/function by tenant (daily)
- Number of migration attempts (codemod runs, telemetry tag: migration-codemod)
- Support tickets labeled sdk-migration and median time-to-resolve
- Regression rate in golden tests after release

Appendix: Example compatibility-remediation snippet (YAML style)
compatibility_matrix:
  sdk_name: redwood-js
  versions:
    - stable: 1.8.x
    - proposed_major: 2.0.0-beta
  tests:
    - unit: true
    - golden_old: true
    - golden_new: true
    - codemod_dry_run: true

FAQ (short)
Q: Why 90 days?
A: We chose 90 days as the standard tradeoff between safety for integrations and the need to iterate. For large enterprise customers we extend this on a case-by-case basis.

Q: Can I skip codemod if change is small?
A: Only with PM approval — small fixes may still cause friction. The codemod requirement can be waived for low-impact additions (non-breaking) or if the language ecosystem makes codemod infeasible. Document the waiver.

Change log
- 2025-11-03: Published initial draft.
- 2026-02-12: Updated to require codemod for JS/TS/Python and clarified CI checks.

Related resources
- SDK release templates: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/quickstarts-and-templates/sdk-patch-release-playbook-and-compat-rules-2025
- Codemod examples: repo tools/codemods/
- Golden tests guidance: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/quickstarts-and-templates/sdk-golden-test-guidelines-2025

Contact
- Primary owner: Maya Chen (maya@redwood.ai)
- Support triage: #sdk-support on Slack

title: Unified SDK Error Model and Mitigation Quickstart
preview: This quickstart defines a single, opinionated error model for Redwood client SDKs and examples for instrumenting mitigation strategies. It standardizes classification, retry/backoff, fallback policies, and telemetry events so product teams can build consistent, observable, and resilient integrations with the Redwood API.
