It standardizes classification, retry/backoff, fallback policies, and telemetry events so product teams can build consistent, observable, and resilient integrations with the Redwood API.

title: DevX docs sprout & canary pipeline playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook documents the DevX "sprout" content pipeline and the canary publishing flow used by the Docs Site team to safely publish SDK guides, API examples, and quickstart templates. It covers roles, automated gates, telemetry checks, rollback criteria, snippet management, and canonical fixes for the top support issues that commonly block releases.

Scope

- Applies to all docs-site content deployed to docs.redwood.ai and the internal preview environment (local-preview).
- Includes: SDK guides, quickstarts & templates, API examples, code snippets, and release notes.
- Excludes: ad-hoc customer-only patches stored in Customer Git repos (see Private Patches playbook).

Goals

1. Ensure high-confidence releases with measurable quality gates (latency, snippet execution, link integrity).
2. Detect regressions in examples and SDK snippets via automated test harnesses before public rollout.
3. Provide a fast path for urgent security or legal fixes with clear rollback playbook.

Key concepts

- Sprout: the content branch and lightweight CI that validates formatting, linting, and snippet compilation.
- Canary: a staged publish of a subset of pages and traffic to measure real-world behavior and capture telemetry anomalies.
- Golden snippets: canonical, executable snippet artifacts (language-tagged) stored in the snippet registry.
- Rollback window: 30 minutes from canary publish during which automatic rollback may be triggered based on telemetry thresholds.

Roles & responsibilities

- Docs Owner: assigns the release candidate and triages canary findings. (Primary: Maya Chen)
- Release Lead: coordinates canary start, monitors telemetry, decides rollback. (Rotation: Docs oncall)
- CI Engineer: maintains sprout & canary pipelines and fixes flakiness.
- SDK Owner: reviews SDK code snippets for compatibility with SDK releases.
- Support Liaison: surfaces top-customer issues and canonical fixes to be included in release notes.

Publishing flow (high-level)

1. Author creates a branch and opens a docs PR against main-sprout. The PR title must include the component tag (e.g., [SDK-python], [quickstart-embeddings]).
2. Sprout CI runs: formatting (prettier/remark), link-checker, mdx-lint, snippet-build, and snippet unit tests (see Snippet validation).
3. On green, PR is reviewed by Docs Owner and SDK Owner if snippets touch SDK code.
4. Merge to main-sprout triggers Sprout deployment to local-preview and a canary candidate build snapshot.
5. Release Lead schedules canary publish (see Canary orchestration) or marks as fast-track for critical fixes.

Sprout CI jobs and thresholds

- lint-format: fail on >0 style errors.
- link-checker: fail on broken internal links; warn on external link timeouts (>3s).
- snippet-build: build all language snippets (python, js, curl). Failure rate >1% fails the job.
- snippet-unit-tests: run unit tests embedded with snippets (pytest / jest). Failure on any test fails the CI.

Snippet validation (details)

- All snippets must be extracted into the snippet registry format: // SNIPPET-ID: <id> | lang: <lang> | version: <sdk-semver>
- CI extracts snippets and runs them inside ephemeral containers matching supported runtimes (ubuntu:22.04, node:18, python:3.11).
- Outputs are compared against golden outputs stored in the snippet registry. Mismatches create a non-blocking incident ticket (but may block canary if the snippet is part of a release-critical page).

Canary orchestration

- Canary target: 5% of production traffic weighted across 3 regions (us-west-2, eu-west-1, ap-south-1) for a 30-minute window.
- Canary pages set: a curated list from the release candidate (max 40 pages) that represents SDK surface area, quickstarts, and API examples.
- Monitoring: error-rate, snippet-runtime-exceptions, snippet-output-diff, page load time, user engagement delta (scroll depth within 5m).
- Canary pass criteria (all must be satisfied for automatic promote):
  - error-rate increase <= 0.5% absolute vs baseline
  - snippet-runtime-exceptions: 0 for critical snippets; <= 1 non-critical exceptions per region
  - snippet-output-diff: <= 0.1% of executed snippets differ from golden outputs
  - page load time P95 <= baseline + 200ms

Manual checks (Release Lead)

1. Review New Relic dashboard for spike patterns in the canary pages.
2. Inspect snippet-execution logs stored in S3 under s3://redwood-docs-canary-logs/<canary-id> for stack traces.
3. Spot-check 5 sample code snippets in each language by executing them locally against the snapshot.
4. Confirm no new support tickets referencing the canary pages have been opened in the last 15 minutes.

Automatic rollback conditions

- If any of the canary pass criteria fail, the pipeline triggers an automatic rollback to the previous snapshot within the rollback window.
- Emergency rollback (skip analysis) if a security/legal tag is present in the release and a failure is observed.
- Post-rollback: create a postmortem stub and tag the release with reason codes (e.g., SNIPPET_REGRESSION, LINK_ROT, PERFORMANCE_DEGRADATION).

Fast-track path (critical fixes)

- Criteria: security patch, licensing/legal text, or customer-impacting data leak.
- Process: skip canary after a documented 2-person approval (Docs Owner + Legal or Security) and deploy to production with 24h follow-up canary snapshot.

Observability and telemetry

- Dashboards: Docs Canary (New Relic), Snippet Health (Grafana), Link Integrity (Datadog Synthetic checks).
- Alerts: page-error-rate > 1% above baseline -> severity P2; snippet-exception frequency -> P3.
- Logging: all snippet executions log to structured JSON including snippet_id, lang, runtime, exit_code, stdout_hash, golden_hash.

Top support issues & canonical fixes (summary)

- Issue: SDK snippet failing due to version skew.
  - Canonical fix: pin snippet metadata to SDK-compatible tag (example: version: "2.4.x") and add pre-run install step to CI.
  - Owner: SDK Owner.

- Issue: Broken external link in quickstart causing SEO penalty.
  - Canonical fix: add external-link-check job with 7-day crawl; auto-create PR to replace or archive link.
  - Owner: Docs Owner.

- Issue: Command-line examples with OS-specific flags (mac/linux) produce inconsistent outputs.
  - Canonical fix: provide multi-line OS fence with explicit markers and add a snippet that verifies both flags.
  - Owner: DevX Snippet Team.

- Issue: Streaming example produces partial output under production network conditions.
  - Canonical fix: include a streaming test harness in snippet unit-tests and add a warning note in the docs with recommended timeout settings.
  - Owner: Runtime Integration Team.

Release checklist (pre-canary)

- [ ] PR title includes component tag and changelog summary.
- [ ] All sprout CI jobs green.
- [ ] Snippet registry updated with any new snippet IDs.
- [ ] SDK owners signed off on snippet compatibility.
- [ ] Release notes drafted and support brief prepared.

Rollback procedure (step-by-step)

1. Initiate rollback via the docs-publisher admin panel: select previous snapshot and click "Promote".
2. Notify #docs-alerts and pager duty (docs-oncall) with rollback ID and reason code.
3. Create a blocker ticket in JIRA with label docs-rollback and assign to Release Lead.
4. Run the rollback diagnostics script: ./scripts/rollback-diagnostics.sh --canary <id> and attach the diagnostics output to the ticket.
5. After rollback, schedule a postmortem within 72 hours.

Appendix A — Representative CI snippet (bash)

```bash
# extract snippets and run python snippets in container
./scripts/extract-snippets.py --source content/ --out artifacts/snippets.json
jq -c '.[]' artifacts/snippets.json |
  while read -r s; do
    id=$(echo $s | jq -r .id); lang=$(echo $s | jq -r .lang); echo "Running $id ($lang)";
    if [ "$lang" = "python" ]; then
      docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/work python:3.11 bash -lc "cd /work && python -m pip install -r requirements-snippets.txt && python tests/run_snippet.py $id"
    fi
  done
```

Appendix B — Sample canary config (yaml)

```yaml
canary_id: docs-canary-2026-02
traffic_percent: 5
regions: [us-west-2, eu-west-1, ap-south-1]
pages: ["/sdk/python/getting-started", "/quickstarts/embed-collections", "/api/streaming-guide"]
window_minutes: 30
thresholds:
  error_rate_abs: 0.005
  snippet_output_diff_pct: 0.1
  p95_page_latency_ms: 200
```

Appendix C — Links & references

- Snippet registry: git+ssh://git@internal.redwood/sr/snippet-registry
- Docs publisher admin: https://admin.docs.redwood.internal/publisher
- Canary dashboards: https://newrelic.com/our-org/dashboards/docs-canary
- Support playbook for triage: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/support-process/triage-playbook

Contact & escalation

- Primary: Maya Chen (docs-owner) — maya@redwood.ai — Pager: docs-oncall
- Secondary: Release Lead rotation — #docs-oncall in Slack

Change log

- 2025-09-12: Initial draft and pilot rules.
- 2025-11-04: Added snippet golden outputs and CI container runtimes.
- 2026-02-10: Updated canary thresholds and added ap-south-1 to regional sampling.
text:
Overview

This playbook documents the DevX "sprout" content pipeline and the canary publishing flow used by the Docs Site team to safely publish SDK guides, API examples, and quickstart templates. It covers roles, automated gates, telemetry checks, rollback criteria, snippet management, and canonical fixes for the top support issues that commonly block releases.

Scope

- Applies to all docs-site content deployed to docs.redwood.ai and the internal preview environment (local-preview).
- Includes: SDK guides, quickstarts & templates, API examples, code snippets, and release notes.
- Excludes: ad-hoc customer-only patches stored in Customer Git repos (see Private Patches playbook).

Goals

1. Ensure high-confidence releases with measurable quality gates (latency, snippet execution, link integrity).
2. Detect regressions in examples and SDK snippets via automated test harnesses before public rollout.
3. Provide a fast path for urgent security or legal fixes with clear rollback playbook.

Key concepts

- Sprout: the content branch and lightweight CI that validates formatting, linting, and snippet compilation.
- Canary: a staged publish of a subset of pages and traffic to measure real-world behavior and capture telemetry anomalies.
- Golden snippets: canonical, executable snippet artifacts (language-tagged) stored in the snippet registry.
- Rollback window: 30 minutes from canary publish during which automatic rollback may be triggered based on telemetry thresholds.

Roles & responsibilities

- Docs Owner: assigns the release candidate and triages canary findings. (Primary: Maya Chen)
- Release Lead: coordinates canary start, monitors telemetry, decides rollback. (Rotation: Docs oncall)
- CI Engineer: maintains sprout & canary pipelines and fixes flakiness.
- SDK Owner: reviews SDK code snippets for compatibility with SDK releases.
- Support Liaison: surfaces top-customer issues and canonical fixes to be included in release notes.

Publishing flow (high-level)

1. Author creates a branch and opens a docs PR against main-sprout. The PR title must include the component tag (e.g., [SDK-python], [quickstart-embeddings]).
2. Sprout CI runs: formatting (prettier/remark), link-checker, mdx-lint, snippet-build, and snippet unit tests (see Snippet validation).
3. On green, PR is reviewed by Docs Owner and SDK Owner if snippets touch SDK code.
4. Merge to main-sprout triggers Sprout deployment to local-preview and a canary candidate build snapshot.
5. Release Lead schedules canary publish (see Canary orchestration) or marks as fast-track for critical fixes.

Sprout CI jobs and thresholds

- lint-format: fail on >0 style errors.
- link-checker: fail on broken internal links; warn on external link timeouts (>3s).
- snippet-build: build all language snippets (python, js, curl). Failure rate >1% fails the job.
- snippet-unit-tests: run unit tests embedded with snippets (pytest / jest). Failure on any test fails the CI.

Snippet validation (details)

- All snippets must be extracted into the snippet registry format: // SNIPPET-ID: <id> | lang: <lang> | version: <sdk-semver>
- CI extracts snippets and runs them inside ephemeral containers matching supported runtimes (ubuntu:22.04, node:18, python:3.11).
- Outputs are compared against golden outputs stored in the snippet registry. Mismatches create a non-blocking incident ticket (but may block canary if the snippet is part of a release-critical page).

Canary orchestration

- Canary target: 5% of production traffic weighted across 3 regions (us-west-2, eu-west-1, ap-south-1) for a 30-minute window.
- Canary pages set: a curated list from the release candidate (max 40 pages) that represents SDK surface area, quickstarts, and API examples.
- Monitoring: error-rate, snippet-runtime-exceptions, snippet-output-diff, page load time, user engagement delta (scroll depth within 5m).
- Canary pass criteria (all must be satisfied for automatic promote):
  - error-rate increase <= 0.5% absolute vs baseline
  - snippet-runtime-exceptions: 0 for critical snippets; <= 1 non-critical exceptions per region
  - snippet-output-diff: <= 0.1% of executed snippets differ from golden outputs
  - page load time P95 <= baseline + 200ms

Manual checks (Release Lead)

1. Review New Relic dashboard for spike patterns in the canary pages.
2. Inspect snippet-execution logs stored in S3 under s3://redwood-docs-canary-logs/<canary-id> for stack traces.
3. Spot-check 5 sample code snippets in each language by executing them locally against the snapshot.
4. Confirm no new support tickets referencing the canary pages have been opened in the last 15 minutes.

Automatic rollback conditions

- If any of the canary pass criteria fail, the pipeline triggers an automatic rollback to the previous snapshot within the rollback window.
- Emergency rollback (skip analysis) if a security/legal tag is present in the release and a failure is observed.
- Post-rollback: create a postmortem stub and tag the release with reason codes (e.g., SNIPPET_REGRESSION, LINK_ROT, PERFORMANCE_DEGRADATION).

Fast-track path (critical fixes)

- Criteria: security patch, licensing/legal text, or customer-impacting data leak.
- Process: skip canary after a documented 2-person approval (Docs Owner + Legal or Security) and deploy to production with 24h follow-up canary snapshot.

Observability and telemetry

- Dashboards: Docs Canary (New Relic), Snippet Health (Grafana), Link Integrity (Datadog Synthetic checks).
- Alerts: page-error-rate > 1% above baseline -> severity P2; snippet-exception frequency -> P3.
- Logging: all snippet executions log to structured JSON including snippet_id, lang, runtime, exit_code, stdout_hash, golden_hash.

Top support issues & canonical fixes (summary)

- Issue: SDK snippet failing due to version skew.
  - Canonical fix: pin snippet metadata to SDK-compatible tag (example: version: "2.4.x") and add pre-run install step to CI.
  - Owner: SDK Owner.

- Issue: Broken external link in quickstart causing SEO penalty.
  - Canonical fix: add external-link-check job with 7-day crawl; auto-create PR to replace or archive link.
  - Owner: Docs Owner.

- Issue: Command-line examples with OS-specific flags (mac/linux) produce inconsistent outputs.
  - Canonical fix: provide multi-line OS fence with explicit markers and add a snippet that verifies both flags.
  - Owner: DevX Snippet Team.

- Issue: Streaming example produces partial output under production network conditions.
  - Canonical fix: include a streaming test harness in snippet unit-tests and add a warning note in the docs with recommended timeout settings.
  - Owner: Runtime Integration Team.

Release checklist (pre-canary)

- [ ] PR title includes component tag and changelog summary.
- [ ] All sprout CI jobs green.
- [ ] Snippet registry updated with any new snippet IDs.
- [ ] SDK owners signed off on snippet compatibility.
- [ ] Release notes drafted and support brief prepared.

Rollback procedure (step-by-step)

1. Initiate rollback via the docs-publisher admin panel: select previous snapshot and click "Promote".
2. Notify #docs-alerts and pager duty (docs-oncall) with rollback ID and reason code.
3. Create a blocker ticket in JIRA with label docs-rollback and assign to Release Lead.
4. Run the rollback diagnostics script: ./scripts/rollback-diagnostics.sh --canary <id> and attach the diagnostics output to the ticket.
5. After rollback, schedule a postmortem within 72 hours.

Appendix A — Representative CI snippet (bash)

```bash
# extract snippets and run python snippets in container
./scripts/extract-snippets.py --source content/ --out artifacts/snippets.json
jq -c '.[]' artifacts/snippets.json |
  while read -r s; do
    id=$(echo $s | jq -r .id); lang=$(echo $s | jq -r .lang); echo "Running $id ($lang)";
    if [ "$lang" = "python" ]; then
      docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/work python:3.11 bash -lc "cd /work && python -m pip install -r requirements-snippets.txt && python tests/run_snippet.py $id"
    fi
  done
```

Appendix B — Sample canary config (yaml)

```yaml
canary_id: docs-canary-2026-02
traffic_percent: 5
regions: [us-west-2, eu-west-1, ap-south-1]
pages: ["/sdk/python/getting-started", "/quickstarts/embed-collections", "/api/streaming-guide"]
window_minutes: 30
thresholds:
  error_rate_abs: 0.005
  snippet_output_diff_pct: 0.1
  p95_page_latency_ms: 200
```

Appendix C — Links & references

- Snippet registry: git+ssh://git@internal.redwood/sr/snippet-registry
- Docs publisher admin: https://admin.docs.redwood.internal/publisher
- Canary dashboards: https://newrelic.com/our-org/dashboards/docs-canary
- Support playbook for triage: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/support-process/triage-playbook

Contact & escalation

- Primary: Maya Chen (docs-owner) — maya@redwood.ai — Pager: docs-oncall
- Secondary: Release Lead rotation — #docs-oncall in Slack

Change log

- 2025-09-12: Initial draft and pilot rules.
- 2025-11-04: Added snippet golden outputs and CI container runtimes.
- 2026-02-10: Updated canary thresholds and added ap-south-1 to regional sampling.

title: SDK lightweight instrumentation adoption playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose
This playbook describes a low-friction, repeatable approach for adopting lightweight SDK instrumentation for Redwood Inference customers and internal integrators. It is targeted at SDK owners, integrators, and Developer Experience engineers who want to add dependable telemetry with minimal code changes.

Goals
- Minimize developer effort to get meaningful telemetry (3–5 events + error traces).
- Provide clear sampling, retention, and PII/redaction guidance so telemetry is safe by default.
- Offer canonical fixes for top support issues encountered during SDK telemetry onboarding.
- Provide a short canary/rollout path with monitoring and rollback guidance.

Audience
- SDK engineers and maintainers (Python, Node, Java)
- DevX engineers who help customers integrate Redwood APIs
- Support engineers responsible for triage and canonical fixes

Principles (high level)
1) Instrument only what yields actionable signal. Start small: request lifecycle, latency, token counts, errors, and sample of responses.
2) Default to privacy-first: do not capture raw prompts or customer data unless explicitly configured by the tenant. Use hashing/redaction helpers.
3) Make events cheap and asynchronous: non-blocking emit, batched transport, backpressure-safe.
4) Provide deterministic schema and semantic names so dashboards/alerts are stable. Version schemas.

Suggested minimal event set (first 30 minutes of integration)
- sdk.request.start (event) — when client sends an API call
- sdk.request.end (event) — when client receives final response or error
- sdk.request.kvcache.hit (counter) — optional: KV cache hits for embeddings/prefixes
- sdk.error (event) — captures categorized error class + safe metadata
- sdk.streaming.chunk (counter) — streamed chunk emitted (optional)

Event schema (recommended fields)
- event_name: string
- timestamp_iso: RFC3339 string
- sdk_version: semantic version string
- language: one of [python,node,java]
- operation: one of [generate,embed,chat,rerank]
- model: short model identifier (no raw model weights or artifacts)
- tenant_id: hashed tenant id (sha256 hex) or empty for unauthenticated clients
- request_id: id generated by SDK (uuid4)
- latency_ms: integer (only on sdk.request.end)
- tokens_sent: integer (optional)
- tokens_generated: integer (optional)
- error_class: short tag (e.g., RateLimit, Timeout, BackendError)
- sample_reason: string (why this request was sampled)

Table: example events and usage (text table)
| event | recommended use | minimal fields | sample_rate |
|------|-----------------|----------------|-------------|
| sdk.request.start | drive latency heatmaps, distribution by operation | event_name,timestamp_iso,sdk_version,operation,model,request_id | 100% |
| sdk.request.end | SLA measurement, p90/p99 latency, cost estimation | +latency_ms,tokens_sent,tokens_generated,error_class | 100% for errors, 5–10% for successes |
| sdk.error | triage and alerting | error_class,stack_hash,request_id | 100% |
| sdk.streaming.chunk | streaming drop/failure detection | count,request_id | 10% sample |

Quickstart: adding the minimal flow (Python example)
1) Install the instrumentation helper package (internal): redwood-sdkinstrument==0.3.0
2) Initialize a lightweight emitter that batches and sends async to telemetry proxy
3) Emit start and end events around the API call

Python snippet (minimal, non-blocking):

from redwood_sdkinstrument import TelemetryClient
from uuid import uuid4

telemetry = TelemetryClient(batch_size=50, flush_interval_ms=2000)  # defaults: non-blocking

def generate_text(client, prompt, model="rw-1-medium"):
    req_id = str(uuid4())
    telemetry.emit("sdk.request.start", {
        "request_id": req_id,
        "operation": "generate",
        "model": model,
        "sdk_version": "1.2.0",
    })
    try:
        resp = client.generate(prompt)  # synchronous call into SDK core
        telemetry.emit("sdk.request.end", {
            "request_id": req_id,
            "latency_ms": resp.latency_ms,
            "tokens_generated": resp.tokens_generated,
            "model": model,
            "operation": "generate",
        })
        return resp
    except Exception as e:
        telemetry.emit("sdk.error", {
            "request_id": req_id,
            "error_class": classify_error(e),
            "stack_hash": hash_stacktrace(e),
        })
        raise

Notes on privacy: never include the prompt text. If a customer asks for raw response capture (for debugging), require an explicit opt-in flag and a retention TTL.

Node snippet (Express middleware example)

const { TelemetryClient } = require('redwood-sdkinstrument');
const telemetry = new TelemetryClient({batchSize:50,flushIntervalMs:2000});

app.post('/generate', async (req, res) => {
  const requestId = uuidv4();
  telemetry.emit('sdk.request.start', {request_id: requestId, operation: 'generate', sdk_version:'2.0.0', model: req.body.model});
  try {
    const resp = await redwoodClient.generate(req.body);
    telemetry.emit('sdk.request.end', {request_id: requestId, latency_ms: resp.latencyMs, tokens_generated: resp.tokens});
    res.json(resp);
  } catch (err) {
    telemetry.emit('sdk.error', {request_id: requestId, error_class: classify(err)});
    res.status(500).send('error');
  }
});

Sampling strategy recommendations
- Errors: 100% (always send)
- Latency and success events: target 5–10% by default for high-volume clients (adjustable by SDK or tenant)
- Long tail traces: deterministic 1-in-N sampling keyed by hashed request_id to preserve reproducibility in logs
- Burst protection: if buffered telemetry queue exceeds 5k events, upsample error events and drop low-value success events first

Schema versioning and evolution
- Use a top-level schema_version field. Increment minor for additive fields, major for breaking changes.
- Maintain a compatibility matrix in repo: schema_version -> sdk_min_version.
- Backfill guidance: do not backfill sensitive fields retroactively; instead surface a migration job for aggregated metrics.

Transport and privacy gates
- Always send through an internal telemetry proxy (hosted or customer-hosted for Private deployments). Proxy enforces redaction rules, sampling policy, and tenant access controls.
- For Private/VPC deployments, provide a local agent that writes to customer-owned storage and forwards aggregated metrics to Redwood control plane only with explicit consent.

Support: top canonical issues and fixes
1) Missing telemetry (SDK emits but nothing appears in dashboard)
  - Check SDK config: telemetry.enabled (default true) and telemetry.endpoint (should point to internal proxy).
  - Verify network: agent/proxy DNS and egress rules for Private. Confirm TLS certificate pinning if enabled.
  - Validate batched delivery: sometimes telemetry is buffered; trigger a flush or restart test client.
  - If using tenant hashing, mismatched tenant_id salt will hide events. Verify salt/version in telemetry config.

2) Duplicate request_id or high cardinality IDs
  - Root cause: multiple SDK layers generating request_id per retry path. Canonical fix: SDK should generate one request_id at the outermost call and pass through.

3) High volume + billing/ingestion spikes
  - Implement rate-limited sampling in SDK. Increase flush interval and batch size temporarily. On server side, prioritize errors and slowly-consumed metrics.

4) Alerts firing due to developer test traffic
  - Provide an environment tag (env=dev) and default to 0% alerting on dev environments. Recommend customers instrument an environment filter in dashboards.

Onboarding checklist (for a new SDK or integrator)
1) Implement SDK client hooks: start/end/error as shown above.
2) Add default safe redaction helpers and test cases that assert no prompt text is included in events.
3) Wire TelemetryClient to internal telemetry proxy with test endpoint.
4) Configure default sampling rates: errors=100%, success=5%, streaming=10%.
5) Run a 1-hour canary with 1% traffic routed to new telemetry path and monitor: event loss (<1%), p99 latency delta, error rate delta.
6) Document schema_version in CHANGELOG and release notes.
7) Add integration smoke tests that assert event emission under simulated failures (timeouts, rate limits, partial responses).

Rollout and canary guidance
- Canary size: start at 0.5–1% of production traffic for new telemetry pipelines.
- Observability gates: event delivery rate within 2σ of baseline, no increase in false-positive alerts, CPU/memory overhead <2% on clients.
- Automatic rollback: if event loss >2% or consumer lag >30s, switch to previous telemetry endpoint and notify owners.

Ownership and SLAs
- SDK telemetry client: owned by Developer Experience team. Response SLA for bug triage: 48 hours.
- Telemetry proxy: owned by Eng Platform. Incident SLA: P1 ack <15 min, mitigation <2 hours.

Telemetry cost-control knobs for customers
- Per-tenant sampling override via config API
- Batch size and flush interval exposed as client options
- On-demand snapshot capture (short TTL, opt-in) for debugging

FAQ and examples
Q: Can we capture prompt text for debugging?
A: Only with explicit customer opt-in. When enabled, prompts are stored with a 7-day TTL and redaction pipeline runs to remove high-risk PII. Defaults are opt-out.

Q: What about Private deployments?
A: Provide an agent that writes to a local mTLS endpoint and exposes aggregated metrics to Redwood only when the customer enables sharing. The SDK uses the local proxy URL if configured.

Links and references
- SDK telemetry schema repo: internal git link: git.internal.redwood/sdks/telemetry-schema
- Proxy deployment runbook: confluence:eng-platform/runbooks/telemetry-proxy-deploy
- Support triage playbook: confluence:eng-developer-experience/support-top-issues/support-repro-harness-and-triage-guides-2026

Appendix: troubleshooting commands and quick tests
- Force a flush (Python): telemetry.flush(timeout=5)
- Local proxy health: curl -v https://telemetry-proxy.internal/healthz
- Event inspection (dev): telemetry.inspect_last(n=50)  # returns redacted JSON

Contact
- Primary author: Aisha Patel (DevX)
- Platform contact: Marcus Lee (Eng Platform)

Document history
- 2025-09-15 created by Aisha Patel
- 2026-02-10 reviewed and published (schema updates and Private deployment agent notes)

- SDK telemetry schema repo: internal git link: git.internal.redwood/sdks/telemetry-schema
- Proxy deployment runbook: confluence:eng-platform/runbooks/telemetry-proxy-deploy
- Support triage playbook: confluence:eng-developer-experience/support-top-issues/support-repro-harness-and-triage-guides-2026

Appendix: troubleshooting commands and quick tests
- Force a flush (Python): telemetry.flush(timeout=5)
- Local proxy health: curl -v https://telemetry-proxy.internal/healthz
- Event inspection (dev): telemetry.inspect_last(n=50)  # returns redacted JSON

Contact
- Primary author: Aisha Patel (DevX)
- Platform contact: Marcus Lee (Eng Platform)

Document history
- 2025-09-15 created by Aisha Patel
- 2026-02-10 reviewed and published (schema updates and Private deployment agent notes)

title: SDK Patch Release Playbook and Compatibility Rules
preview:
Overview:

This playbook describes the lightweight, repeatable process we use to ship SDK patch releases (hotfixes, security patches, backward-compatible minor fixes) and the compatibility rules that must be enforced by the devx team. The goal is to minimize user impact, ensure consistent docs/website updates, and provide a clear triage + rollback path for incidents tied to SDK releases.

When to use this playbook:
- Patches for regressions in Official Redwood SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go) that affect runtime correctness or cause high-severity developer experience regressions (errors in auth, streaming breaking, incorrect typing).
- Security fixes in dependency tree that require immediate release.
- Minor quality-of-life fixes that need to go out in the next patch window (semver PATCH).

High-level constraints and rules:
1) Semver policy: follow semver strictly. Patches must be release-type PATCH and must not introduce public API changes. Any change that modifies signature/behavior requires MINOR and must go through the normal feature release cadence.
2) Compatibility matrix: maintainers must run the automated compat matrix against supported runtime targets before cutting a release. Supported targets: Python 3.9–3.11, Node 16/18, Go 1.19–1.21.
3) Documentation parity: every published patch must include a docs-site short changelog entry and, where relevant, updated API snippets and migration notes.
4) Telemetry & opt-in beta flags: if a fix touches telemetry or experimental features, include an explicit opt-in flag and a developer note in the changelog.

Release checklist (short-form):
- [ ] Confirm issue severity and categorize as PATCH-worthy (owner + eng oncall)
- [ ] Create release branch: release/sdk/<sdk>-patch-YYYYMMDD-<issue-id>
- [ ] Update changelog entry (docs short entry + repo CHANGELOG.md)
- [ ] Run unit + integration + compatibility matrix tests (see below)
- [ ] Run package signing and vulnerability scan (SNYK/OSV)
- [ ] Publish pre-release to internal package registry for Canary users (npm:@redwood/sdk-canary, pypi:internal/test)
- [ ] Monitor canary for 24 hours (or agreed observation window)
- [ ] Publish public patch, update docs-site, tag release, notify #devx-releases
- [ ] Update support KB and canonical fixes list

Step-by-step playbook: detailed procedure

Phase A — Triage and branch
1) Triage in issue tracker: assign severity, set target release date (ASAP or next daily patch window).
2) Label issue with \"patch-candidate\" and create a short patch RFC in PR description describing the fix and verifying non-breaking guarantee.
3) Create release branch from main: git checkout -b release/sdk/<sdk>-patch-YYYYMMDD-<issue-id>

Phase B — Development and testing
1) Code: keep changes scoped; avoid refactors. Ensure public API surface untouched (types, exported functions).
2) Local test matrix: run full unit suite + integration mocks. Commands:
- Python: tox -e py39,py310,py311
- Node: npm run test --all-targets (Node 16/18 via nvm use)
- Go: go test ./... -tags=integration
3) Compatibility matrix job (CI): must succeed before merge. Matrix includes:
- installing SDK into blank sample apps for each runtime version
- running a small prompt/streaming sample against the staging Redwood hosted endpoint
- verifying artifact sizes and ABI-compatible native deps

Phase C — Canary and verification
1) Publish to internal registry with canary tag. Example commands:
- npm publish --tag canary --registry https://npm.internal.redwood.local
- python -m pip wheel . && twine upload --repository-url https://pypi.internal.redwood.local --verbose dist/* --skip-existing --repository test
2) Notify canary users and DevX stakeholders with instructions to opt-in. Provide sample install snippets for each SDK.
3) Monitoring: watch these signals for 24 hours (or configured window):
- telemetry error rate (alert if > 2x baseline)
- support tickets referencing the new canary
- automated smoke test failures in downstream integrations

Phase D — Publish and post-release tasks
1) Tag release: git tag -a vX.Y.Z -m \"Patch: <short-desc>\" && git push --tags origin release/...
2) Create release on GitHub/GitLab and attach artifacts (wheels, tarballs).
3) Update docs-site: add short changelog on /docs/sdk/changelog and update any affected API examples. Use docs-site cli: docsctl release-changelog --sdk <sdk> --version X.Y.Z --notes \"<one-line>\"
4) Post to #devx-releases with step-by-step summary and links to docs + artifact.
5) Mark issue resolved and link release artifacts in ticket.

Rollback and emergency mitigation:
- If a critical regression is detected post-release (error rate spike, data corruption, major functionality broken), follow emergency rollback:
  1) Revert tag and release on package registry: npm deprecate <pkg>@X.Y.Z \"broken release\"; publish X.Y.Z-rc2 if needed.
  2) Inform SRE and Eng Oncall and open incident (link to confluence incident template).
  3) Roll forward hotfix only if root cause understood; otherwise publish a tiny shim that restores previous behavior without wide feature changes.

Standards for API changes in patches (must be followed):
- No signature removal.
- No behavior inversion (e.g. change default of boolean).
- Only internal helpers may be added.
- If a necessary fix modifies behavior for correctness, include an automated compatibility test demonstrating prior behavior problem and new behavior expectation.

Canonical support triage mapping (common patch-related support issues and fixes):
1) Auth errors after SDK upgrade
   - Likely causes: dependency bump changed default header; user-perceived error: 401/403.
   - Quick fix: Verify token header key, roll back dependency to previous working version, publish immediate patch that restores header mapping.
2) Streaming stalls or truncated chunks
   - Cause: changed chunk framing or backpressure handling in streaming helper.
   - Fix: revert to previous chunking loop and add test that streams 10k tokens in CI sample app.
3) TypeScript types not matching runtime
   - Cause: .d.ts generation change.
   - Fix: pin types generation step in release pipeline; republish patch with correct types only.

Changelog and release notes templates (short):
- Title: SDK <sdk> vX.Y.Z (patch) — YYYY-MM-DD
- One-line summary: Fixes authentication header regression causing 401s in streaming scenarios.
- Backwards compatibility: fully compatible (semver PATCH).
- Impact: users on >= vX.Y.(Z-1) should upgrade; no migration steps required.
- Snippets: (include pre/post example if behavior changed in edge case)

Sample PR description snippet to copy into PR body:
\"\"\"
Patch PR: <one-line>
Related Issue: #<id>
Motivation: short explanation of why this is a patch (no public API changes)
Testing: unit + integration + compat matrix results (link to CI)
Canary plan: list of canary users / internal packages to test
Rollback plan: steps to revert should regression be observed
\"\"\"

Ops & docs-site considerations (must not be skipped):
- Docs update minimal: add one-line changelog entry and update code snippets if they were modified. Avoid changing example app flows for patches unless required.
- Docs deployment: staging -> smoke -> prod. Use docsctl preview and verify sample pages referencing the SDK examples.

Automation and CI jobs required for every patch:
- patch-compat-matrix (parallel matrix across runtimes)
- patch-canary-publish (internal registry publish)
- patch-vuln-scan (SNYK/OSV)
- patch-sign-artifacts (GPG/HSM signing)

Compatibility matrix example (ASCII table):
| SDK | Python 3.9 | Python 3.10 | Python 3.11 | Node 16 | Node 18 | Go 1.20 |
|-----|-----------:|-----------:|-----------:|--------:|--------:|-------:|
| python-sdk | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | n/a | n/a | n/a |
| node-sdk | n/a | n/a | n/a | ✅ | ✅ | n/a |
| go-sdk | n/a | n/a | n/a | n/a | n/a | ✅ |

Common QA tests (smoke):
- auth flow test (happy path + expired token)
- streaming smoke test (1 token, 1k tokens, intermittent network)
- embeddings encode/decode roundtrip (for embedding SDKs)
- example app integration test (install SDK and run sample end-to-end request)

Maintenance: periodic items
- Quarterly review of supported runtimes and update matrix (owner: devx-maintainers).
- Archive canary tags older than 30 days.
- Monthly run of 'compat drift' job that installs latest SDKs into sample apps and reports warnings for any runtime deprecations.

Appendix A — Example CLI snippets and release commands
- Create branch: git checkout -b release/sdk/python-patch-20260203-1234
- Bump version: poetry version patch && git commit -am \"bump: patch X.Y.Z\"
- Build wheel and sign: python -m build && gpg --detach-sign --armor dist/*
- Publish (internal): twine upload --repository-url https://pypi.internal.redwood.local dist/*

Appendix B — Example automated changelog entry (YAML snippet used by docsctl):
changelog_entry: |
  - sdk: python
    version: \"X.Y.Z\"
    date: \"2026-02-03\"
    summary: \"Fix streaming header regression affecting auth for long-running streams.\"
    compatibility: \"patch\"

Contact and ownership:
- Primary owners: devx-releases@redwood.internal (team)
- Secondary: runtime-team@redwood.internal (for runtime-related regressions)

If you are uncertain whether a change qualifies as a patch, escalate to the devx oncall and include a short compatibility justification in the PR description.

title: SDK compact builds and runtime-stub playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook documents the standard approach for producing compact SDK builds and lightweight runtime stubs used by Redwood customer integrations and internal sample apps. It targets two immediate goals: minimize client-side artifact size for web and edge consumers, and provide deterministic runtime stubs for local development and CI that reproduce production behavior without requiring a full runtime backend.

Audience

- SDK engineers responsible for packaging and releases
- Developer advocates creating sample apps and quickstarts
- Support engineers shipping canonical fixes for client-side issues

When to use this playbook

- New SDK languages or client bindings are being introduced
- Bundle size regressions are detected in CI (>20% increase)
- Customers report long cold-load times for browser-based SDKs
- Local developer loops require deterministic handing of streaming/fallback behavior

What you will find in this document

1) Build targets and thresholds
2) Recommended toolchain configurations (Rollup / esbuild / swc)
3) Minimal runtime stub reference and behaviour matrix
4) CI release checks and gating rules
5) Troubleshooting and canonical fixes for top support issues
6) Ownership and rollout steps

1) Build targets and thresholds

- Primary artifact goals (web/browser bundles):
  - Max gzipped bundle size (core SDK + runtime adapter): 200KB for core build, 300KB including optional adapters
  - Tree-shaken ESM output and a single UMD for legacy consumers
  - API surface exported by package must not increase major version without a migration document

- Edge/Serverless adapters:
  - Unminified dev build for source maps is allowed up to 1.2MB; production artifact max 450KB gzipped

- Telemetry and stability SLOs for SDK releases:
  - CI package integrity checks: 100% pass for lint/build/test gates
  - Post-release client error rate (first 48 hours): <= 0.5% increase in observed API errors from SDK-tagged requests

2) Recommended toolchain configs (examples)

- Default choices (recommended order): esbuild -> Rollup (with terser) -> swc (for advanced transforms). Pick one primary tool per repo and standardize on config presets below.

- Minimal Rollup config (text example):
  // rollup.config.js
  export default {
    input: 'src/index.ts',
    output: [
      {file: 'dist/index.esm.js', format: 'esm', sourcemap: true},
      {file: 'dist/index.umd.js', format: 'umd', name: 'RedwoodSDK', sourcemap: false} 
    ],
    external: ['events','buffer'],
    plugins: [require('@rollup/plugin-node-resolve')(), require('@rollup/plugin-commonjs')(), require('rollup-plugin-terser').terser()]
  }

- Example esbuild command (CI friendly):
  esbuild src/index.ts --bundle --minify --target=es2019 --format=esm --outfile=dist/index.esm.js --sourcemap=external

- Package.json snippet for publishing (small):
  {
    "name": "@redwood/sdk-client",
    "version": "1.6.2",
    "main": "dist/index.umd.js",
    "module": "dist/index.esm.js",
    "types": "dist/index.d.ts",
    "files": ["dist/*"]
  }

3) Runtime stub reference

Purpose: provide a tiny deterministic implementation of network and streaming semantics so that developer local loops and CI tests can exercise retry, backpressure and fallback logic without contacting Redwood runtime. Stubs live under /dev-stubs in the SDK repo and are shipped as a separate dev-only package @redwood/sdk-stubs.

Behavior matrix (summary):

| Feature | Prod runtime behavior | Stub behavior | Config knobs |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Streaming tokens | Chunked HTTP/2 or server-sent events with resume tokens | Emit pre-recorded chunks at configurable intervals | REDWOOD_STUB_LATENCY_MS (default 40) |
| Retry/backoff | Exponential backoff with jitter, secondary model fallback | Deterministic fixed backoff for fast CI | REDWOOD_STUB_FAIL_AFTER_N (default 0 for success) |
| KV cache hits | Backend-driven cache hits based on prefix | Local in-memory prefix cache with transparent hits/misses | REDWOOD_STUB_CACHE_HIT_RATE (0.3) |
| Auth errors | 401/403 from KMS/OIDC gates | Optionally inject 401 for testing via env var | REDWOOD_STUB_INJECT_AUTH_ERROR=true |

Stub usage examples

- Start local dev server using stubs (node):
  REDWOOD_USE_STUBS=1 REDWOOD_STUB_LATENCY_MS=30 node examples/playground.js

- Jest unit test example using stubbed fetch:
  import {setupStub} from '@redwood/sdk-stubs'
  beforeAll(() => setupStub({latencyMs: 10, failAfter: 0}))

4) CI release checks and gating rules

Required CI pipeline steps (order matters):
1. typecheck (tsc)
2. lint (eslint + custom rules in devx/eslint-plugin)
3. unit tests + coverage (threshold 90%)
4. bundle analysis job (generates size report and fails if thresholds exceeded)
5. runtime-stub integration smoke tests (fast, < 30s)
6. telemetry contract test (validate event names and schema)

Failing a gate: branch must be fixed, and the author opens a PR with either the fix or a documented exception with a reviewer from the release leads team. Exceptions are temporary and expire after 7 days.

Bundle analysis (implementation):
- CI job runs 'node tools/bundle-check.js' which computes gzipped sizes and compares to baseline artifact in artifacts/baseline.json.
- Baseline update requires a documented release note and approval from the SDK release lead.

5) Troubleshooting and canonical fixes for top support issues

Canonical Issue 1: Browser bundle causes main thread jank on page load
- Symptom: first paint delayed, SDK initialization synchronous for >100ms on slow CPUs
- Fix: convert heavy synchronous initialization (telemetry reporter construction and config fetch) into async background init. Provide a lazy init API: await Redwood.init({lazy:true}) or Redwood.getClient() triggers real init.

Canonical Issue 2: Streaming responses truncated in browser builds
- Symptom: client receives only the first chunk when using fetch polyfills or service workers
- Fix: Ensure the SDK uses native ReadableStream when available and falls back to a stream adapter that preserves backpressure. Add tests that run in headless Chromium in CI.

Canonical Issue 3: Local dev tests flake because of non-deterministic retry timing
- Symptom: CI tests intermittently fail on timeout due to jittered backoff in production retry logic
- Fix: Use the runtime stub with deterministic backoff in test environments (REDWOOD_USE_STUBS=1). Add environment gating so tests never hit production retry timers.

Canonical Issue 4: Bundle size regressed after adding a third-party adapter
- Symptom: PR passes tests but bundle-check fails with +120KB gzipped
- Fix: Move adapter to optional peer dependency and expose a lazy-loading factory: importAdapter('speech') which dynamic-imports the adapter only when used. Update docs and migration notes.

6) Ownership and rollout steps

- Owners:
  - SDK packaging & CI: @sdk-release-team (team lead: Mateo Alvarez)
  - Runtime stubs: @runtime-tools (owner: Priya Shah)
  - Telemetry contract: @observability (owner: Jordan Li)

Release checklist (minor patch):
1. Branch off main and implement changes.
2. Run local bundle-check and stub smoke tests.
3. Open PR and add 'sdk-bundle-check' label.
4. Reviewers: one SDK packaging reviewer + one runtime-tools reviewer.
5. Merge when CI green. The merge trigger publishes a canary alpha to npm with tag next.
6. Canary verification: automated smoke runs against sample-apps playground for 24 hours.
7. Promote to stable after no regressions for 48 hours.

Rollback plan

- If post-release error rate > 0.5% above baseline or bundle-size increase is detected in downstream apps:
  1. Immediately unpublish newest package (mark deprecated) and repoint sample apps to previous tag.
  2. Postmortem: 72-hour incident review with owners, include root cause, rollout telemetry, and mitigation steps.

Appendix A: Small scripts and helpers

- bundle-check.js (concept):
  - Computes sums of gzipped sizes for dist/*.js
  - Reads artifacts/baseline.json and compares; exits 1 on threshold breach

- telemetry-schema.json example fields to assert in CI:
  ["sdk.init","sdk.request","sdk.error","sdk.streaming.chunk"]

Appendix B: Frequently asked questions

Q: Can stubs be used in staging environments?
A: Yes, but only for dev-mode smoke tests. Staging should validate production integration against the runtime unless connector costs or data residency prevents it.

Q: Who approves bundle baseline updates?
A: SDK release lead (Mateo Alvarez) plus one reviewer from devx or observability. Document reasons in the release notes.

Contact and feedback

- Primary author: Elena Rivera (elena@redwood.ai)
- SDK packaging Slack channel: #sdk-release

Change log

- 2025-11-12: Drafted initial thresholds and stub design (Elena Rivera)
- 2025-12-18: Added CI gating rules and bundle-check script template (Mateo Alvarez)
- 2026-02-03: Finalized SLOs and promoted to published (Priya Shah, Jordan Li)

- Primary artifact goals (web/browser bundles):
  - Max gzipped bundle size (core SDK + runtime adapter): 200KB for core build, 300KB including optional adapters
  - Tree-shaken ESM output and a single UMD for legacy consumers
  - API surface exported by package must not increase major version without a migration document

- Edge/Serverless adapters:
  - Unminified dev build for source maps is allowed up to 1.2MB; production artifact max 450KB gzipped

- Telemetry and stability SLOs for SDK releases:
  - CI package integrity checks: 100% pass for lint/build/test gates
  - Post-release client error rate (first 48 hours): <= 0.5% increase in observed API errors from SDK-tagged requests

2) Recommended toolchain configs (examples)

- Default choices (recommended order): esbuild -> Rollup (with terser) -> swc (for advanced transforms). Pick one primary tool per repo and standardize on config presets below.

- Minimal Rollup config (text example):
  // rollup.config.js
  export default {
    input: 'src/index.ts',
    output: [
      {file: 'dist/index.esm.js', format: 'esm', sourcemap: true},
      {file: 'dist/index.umd.js', format: 'umd', name: 'RedwoodSDK', sourcemap: false} 
    ],
    external: ['events','buffer'],
    plugins: [require('@rollup/plugin-node-resolve')(), require('@rollup/plugin-commonjs')(), require('rollup-plugin-terser').terser()]
  }

- Example esbuild command (CI friendly):
  esbuild src/index.ts --bundle --minify --target=es2019 --format=esm --outfile=dist/index.esm.js --sourcemap=external

- Package.json snippet for publishing (small):
  {
    "name": "@redwood/sdk-client",
    "version": "1.6.2",
    "main": "dist/index.umd.js",
    "module": "dist/index.esm.js",
    "types": "dist/index.d.ts",
    "files": ["dist/*"]
  }

3) Runtime stub reference

Purpose: provide a tiny deterministic implementation of network and streaming semantics so that developer local loops and CI tests can exercise retry, backpressure and fallback logic without contacting Redwood runtime. Stubs live under /dev-stubs in the SDK repo and are shipped as a separate dev-only package @redwood/sdk-stubs.

Behavior matrix (summary):

| Feature | Prod runtime behavior | Stub behavior | Config knobs |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Streaming tokens | Chunked HTTP/2 or server-sent events with resume tokens | Emit pre-recorded chunks at configurable intervals | REDWOOD_STUB_LATENCY_MS (default 40) |
| Retry/backoff | Exponential backoff with jitter, secondary model fallback | Deterministic fixed backoff for fast CI | REDWOOD_STUB_FAIL_AFTER_N (default 0 for success) |
| KV cache hits | Backend-driven cache hits based on prefix | Local in-memory prefix cache with transparent hits/misses | REDWOOD_STUB_CACHE_HIT_RATE (0.3) |
| Auth errors | 401/403 from KMS/OIDC gates | Optionally inject 401 for testing via env var | REDWOOD_STUB_INJECT_AUTH_ERROR=true |

Stub usage examples

- Start local dev server using stubs (node):
  REDWOOD_USE_STUBS=1 REDWOOD_STUB_LATENCY_MS=30 node examples/playground.js

- Jest unit test example using stubbed fetch:
  import {setupStub} from '@redwood/sdk-stubs'
  beforeAll(() => setupStub({latencyMs: 10, failAfter: 0}))

4) CI release checks and gating rules

Required CI pipeline steps (order matters):
1. typecheck (tsc)
2. lint (eslint + custom rules in devx/eslint-plugin)
3. unit tests + coverage (threshold 90%)
4. bundle analysis job (generates size report and fails if thresholds exceeded)
5. runtime-stub integration smoke tests (fast, < 30s)
6. telemetry contract test (validate event names and schema)

Failing a gate: branch must be fixed, and the author opens a PR with either the fix or a documented exception with a reviewer from the release leads team. Exceptions are temporary and expire after 7 days.

Bundle analysis (implementation):
- CI job runs 'node tools/bundle-check.js' which computes gzipped sizes and compares to baseline artifact in artifacts/baseline.json.
- Baseline update requires a documented release note and approval from the SDK release lead.

5) Troubleshooting and canonical fixes for top support issues

Canonical Issue 1: Browser bundle causes main thread jank on page load
- Symptom: first paint delayed, SDK initialization synchronous for >100ms on slow CPUs
- Fix: convert heavy synchronous initialization (telemetry reporter construction and config fetch) into async background init. Provide a lazy init API: await Redwood.init({lazy:true}) or Redwood.getClient() triggers real init.

Canonical Issue 2: Streaming responses truncated in browser builds
- Symptom: client receives only the first chunk when using fetch polyfills or service workers
- Fix: Ensure the SDK uses native ReadableStream when available and falls back to a stream adapter that preserves backpressure. Add tests that run in headless Chromium in CI.

Canonical Issue 3: Local dev tests flake because of non-deterministic retry timing
- Symptom: CI tests intermittently fail on timeout due to jittered backoff in production retry logic
- Fix: Use the runtime stub with deterministic backoff in test environments (REDWOOD_USE_STUBS=1). Add environment gating so tests never hit production retry timers.

Canonical Issue 4: Bundle size regressed after adding a third-party adapter
- Symptom: PR passes tests but bundle-check fails with +120KB gzipped
- Fix: Move adapter to optional peer dependency and expose a lazy-loading factory: importAdapter('speech') which dynamic-imports the adapter only when used. Update docs and migration notes.

6) Ownership and rollout steps

- Owners:
  - SDK packaging & CI: @sdk-release-team (team lead: Mateo Alvarez)
  - Runtime stubs: @runtime-tools (owner: Priya Shah)
  - Telemetry contract: @observability (owner: Jordan Li)

Release checklist (minor patch):
1. Branch off main and implement changes.
2. Run local bundle-check and stub smoke tests.
3. Open PR and add 'sdk-bundle-check' label.
4. Reviewers: one SDK packaging reviewer + one runtime-tools reviewer.
5. Merge when CI green. The merge trigger publishes a canary alpha to npm with tag next.
6. Canary verification: automated smoke runs against sample-apps playground for 24 hours.
7. Promote to stable after no regressions for 48 hours.

Rollback plan

- If post-release error rate > 0.5% above baseline or bundle-size increase is detected in downstream apps:
  1. Immediately unpublish newest package (mark deprecated) and repoint sample apps to previous tag.
  2. Postmortem: 72-hour incident review with owners, include root cause, rollout telemetry, and mitigation steps.

Appendix A: Small scripts and helpers

- bundle-check.js (concept):
  - Computes sums of gzipped sizes for dist/*.js
  - Reads artifacts/baseline.json and compares; exits 1 on threshold breach

- telemetry-schema.json example fields to assert in CI:
  ["sdk.init","sdk.request","sdk.error","sdk.streaming.chunk"]

Appendix B: Frequently asked questions

Q: Can stubs be used in staging environments?
A: Yes, but only for dev-mode smoke tests. Staging should validate production integration against the runtime unless connector costs or data residency prevents it.

Q: Who approves bundle baseline updates?
A: SDK release lead (Mateo Alvarez) plus one reviewer from devx or observability. Document reasons in the release notes.

Contact and feedback

- Primary author: Elena Rivera (elena@redwood.ai)
- SDK packaging Slack channel: #sdk-release

Change log

- 2025-11-12: Drafted initial thresholds and stub design (Elena Rivera)
- 2025-12-18: Added CI gating rules and bundle-check script template (Mateo Alvarez)
- 2026-02-03: Finalized SLOs and promoted to published (Priya Shah, Jordan Li)

title: Docs plan: Redwood Private offline bundles (signed) — public vs private docs, examples, and release alignment
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the documentation plan for **Redwood Private offline bundles with cryptographic signing** (air-gapped / restricted networks). It clarifies:
- What content belongs in **public docs** vs **private/customer docs**
- The **minimum set of guides** required for pilots and GA
- Canonical **CLI examples** (installer preflight, bundle inspection, signature verification)
- How we keep docs **synchronized with shipped artifacts** (bundle layout, manifest schema, installer flags, error codes)

This is a DevEx/Docs plan (not the spec). Technical source-of-truth is in the engineering design/ADR pages linked above.

---

## Audience
Primary:
- Customer platform operators installing Redwood Private in **air-gapped** or **egress-restricted** environments
- Solutions Engineering / Professional Services supporting pilots
- Support engineers triaging install failures

Secondary:
- Security reviewers evaluating Redwood’s supply-chain controls (artifact signing, provenance, auditability)

---

## Documentation surfaces & access model
We will split content across three surfaces. The same content should not be duplicated unless required for access control; when duplicated, it must reference a single canonical source section.

### 1) Public docs (docs.redwoodinference.com)
**Goal:** Provide high-level understanding of offline install support and security posture without leaking customer-only operational details.
Include:
- One overview page: “Redwood Private deployment modes (Connected vs Offline/Air-gapped)”
- A short security statement: “Signed offline bundles: integrity + provenance”
- A minimal prerequisites list (high-level) and a pointer: “Contact Support/SE for the air-gapped install guide”

Exclude (keep private):
- Exact bundle directory layout, manifest schema details, and error-code taxonomy
- Break-glass override instructions and approval workflow
- Trust-store update procedures (key rotation) beyond a conceptual statement

### 2) Private/customer docs (Customer portal / authenticated docs)
**Goal:** Provide the step-by-step operational guide needed to install, upgrade, and troubleshoot offline.
Include:
- Full end-to-end air-gapped install and upgrade guides
- Offline bundle verification and inspection commands with expected output
- Troubleshooting (hash mismatch, unknown key, time skew) and log locations
- Key rotation / trust store update procedure (customer action steps)
- Support escalation and “what to collect” checklist

### 3) Internal-only docs (Confluence + internal runbooks)
**Goal:** Enable Support/SE/Engineering to execute pilots, triage incidents, and maintain release quality.
Include:
- Detailed runbooks and error taxonomy
- Break-glass process (policy, approvals, audit markers)
- Release checklist/gating and how docs are validated per release

---

## Information architecture (IA)
### Private/customer docs pages (must-have for GA)
1) **Overview: Offline bundles (signed) for Redwood Private**
   - What’s inside a bundle (high level)
   - What signing protects (manifest + referenced artifacts)
   - What verification does and when it runs (preflight, install, upgrade)

2) **Install: Air-gapped installation with signed bundles**
   - Prereqs (Kubernetes version range, container runtime, storage, CPU/RAM, OS tooling)
   - Transfer guidance (USB / secure media) and integrity preservation tips
   - Step-by-step install

3) **Preflight + Verify: Verify bundle signature and manifest before install**
   - Mandatory verification flow
   - Expected success output
   - Common failure messages → next steps

4) **Upgrade & rollback: Offline bundle upgrade flow**
   - Supported upgrade paths
   - Downgrade prevention behavior
   - Rollback procedure (and what is / isn’t allowed offline)

5) **Key rotation / Trust store updates (offline environments)**
   - How Redwood rotates signing keys
   - How customer updates the installer trust store offline
   - What happens if trust store is not updated (unknown key failure)

6) **Troubleshooting: Offline bundle verification failures**
   - Hash mismatch
   - Signature invalid/untrusted
   - Not-yet-valid / expired due to time skew
   - Bundle corruption due to unzip tooling or line ending conversion

7) **FAQ & common errors**
   - Error codes reference (stable identifiers)
   - “What logs do I send Support?”

### Public docs pages (minimal)
- “Redwood Private: Offline / air-gapped deployments (overview)”
- “Security: Signed offline bundles (integrity & provenance overview)”

### Internal-only pages (examples)
- “Support playbook: verification failures” (detailed triage + escalations)
- “Release docs checklist: signed bundles” (gating, validation steps)
- “Exception process: unsigned bundle hotfix (break-glass)”

---

## Canonical examples (CLI snippets)
**Note:** Examples below use proposed/implemented CLI patterns from the installer workstream. Docs must match the currently shipped installer. If flags or subcommands differ for a given release, the release notes and versioned docs must call that out explicitly.

### Bundle inspection (recommended first step)
Use when validating transfer media or when opening a support ticket.

Command:
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect --bundle /mnt/media/redwood-private-offline-bundle-2026.01.0.tar.zst`

Expected output (example):
- Shows bundle version, build ID, manifest digest
- Shows signature verification result and signing key ID
- Prints a short artifact summary (charts/images/models/runtime)

### Explicit signature verification (preflight mode)
Command:
- `redwood-private-installer preflight --airgap --bundle /mnt/media/bundle.tar.zst --verify-only`

Expected behaviors:
- Fails closed if signature is invalid, manifest hash mismatch, or key is untrusted
- Prints remediation steps including log path

### Verify using an updated trust store (after key rotation)
Command:
- `redwood-private-installer preflight --airgap --bundle /mnt/media/bundle.tar.zst --trust-store /etc/redwood/trust/keys.json`

Docs must include:
- Trust store file location options (embedded default vs customer-supplied path)
- How to stage trust store updates offline

### Time skew diagnostics (air-gapped clusters)
When customer has inaccurate system time and signature validation fails due to validity window.

Command (diagnostic only; do not recommend as a permanent configuration):
- `redwood-private-installer preflight --airgap --bundle /mnt/media/bundle.tar.zst --time-override "2026-01-27T12:00:00Z"`

Docs must:
- Clearly label this as **diagnostic**
- Provide guidance on fixing system time source and rerunning verification normally

### Break-glass override (internal/private docs only)
Break-glass must never appear in public docs. In private docs, it should be behind an “Emergency only” section and require explicit warnings.

Command (example):
- `redwood-private-installer install --airgap --bundle /mnt/media/bundle.tar.zst --break-glass-allow-unsigned`

Docs requirements:
- Must include the approval path and required audit annotations
- Must state that Support may ask for additional evidence/logs and that the cluster may be marked “unsupported until remediated”

---

## Keeping docs aligned with release artifacts
### Principle
Docs should be treated as a **versioned interface** to a release artifact. If the installer behavior or bundle layout changes, docs must change in the same release train.

### Source-of-truth mapping
- Bundle layout + manifest fields: Engineering spec page (Confluence) and schema in repo
- Verification behaviors + error codes: Installer repo + docs error reference page
- Key rotation/trust store: Security/Release runbooks + customer-facing procedure

### Versioning strategy
- Customer docs should be **versioned by Redwood Private release** (e.g., 2026.01, 2026.02) when installer flags, manifests, or error codes change.
- Always provide:
  - “Latest” (default)
  - A version selector (at least last 3 minor releases)

### Release gating checklist (docs)
For every Redwood Private release that includes offline bundles:
1) Docs PR merged for:
   - Install (offline)
   - Verify/preflight
   - Upgrade/rollback
   - FAQ + error codes
2) Commands and outputs validated against the **release candidate** bundle:
   - Run `bundle inspect` against RC artifact and compare to docs
   - Validate at least one failure mode screenshot/output (tampered bundle) to ensure messages match
3) Confirm that the docs include the correct:
   - Minimum installer version required
   - Bundle version string format and build ID location
   - Trust store update instructions (if new key introduced)

Ownership:
- Docs owner: DevEx Docs (Elliot Price / Kira Thompson)
- Validation sign-off: Release Engineering (Connor O’Brien) + Installer Eng (Ethan Park)
- Security wording review: Marcus Lin (esp. break-glass + trust root statements)

### Automation (recommended)
To prevent drift, we should generate docs fragments directly from build artifacts where feasible:
- Auto-generate a “Bundle contents summary” table from the manifest (artifact names + versions)
- Auto-sync error code reference from installer constants
- Publish checksums/signature metadata (read-only) as an appendix section

Implementation suggestions:
- Add a CI job in `redwood-docs` that pulls the RC manifest (or a fixture manifest checked in per release) and validates:
  - CLI snippets compile (command exists)
  - Referenced flags exist
  - Error codes referenced exist

---

## Content standards (tone, precision, and security)
### Tone and clarity
- Use direct, operator-focused language.
- Prefer “Do X, then Y” procedures with expected output and next steps.

### Security constraints
- Do not publish:
  - Internal key ceremony details
  - Internal signing infrastructure identifiers
  - Break-glass flags in public docs
  - Any internal-only log redaction policies or SIEM exporter internals

### Required doc elements for install/verify pages
Each operational page must include:
- Preconditions and “What you need on the air-gapped side”
- A “Verify before install” step (signature + manifest)
- Log locations
- A “What to send Support” checklist

---

## Draft outline for the private/customer “Air-gapped install” page
1) Overview
2) Requirements
3) Acquire the offline bundle (from Redwood)
4) Transfer to secure media
5) Verify bundle:
   - `bundle inspect`
   - `preflight --verify-only`
6) Install
7) Post-install validation
8) Troubleshooting

---

## Open questions / follow-ups
1) Finalize canonical command names and flags (installer): confirm `bundle inspect`, `preflight --verify-only`, trust store path flag.
2) Decide where the trust store lives by default (embedded vs file) and document exactly one recommended path for customers.
3) Confirm whether we expose signing key IDs / fingerprints in customer output and if yes, define the stable formatting (and redaction rules).
4) Decide how we present downgrade prevention behavior in customer docs (what is blocked vs allowed rollback steps).

Tracking:
- Docs execution ticket: ENG-4850 (Docs and runbooks for offline bundle verification)
- GA criteria: PM-894

---

## Appendix: quick reference tables
### What goes where
Public docs:
- Conceptual overview of offline install support
- High-level explanation of signed bundles

Private/customer docs:
- Step-by-step procedures, commands, expected output
- Troubleshooting, error codes, trust store updates

Internal Confluence:
- Runbooks, break-glass policy, release gating checklist, internal triage

### Definition of “aligned with release artifacts”
Docs are considered aligned when:
- Commands and flags match the shipped installer version
- Bundle layout references match the actual bundle
- Error codes/messages match installer output closely enough for Support scripts
- Release notes mention any required doc-visible behavior changes (new trust root, verification behavior changes)

- Step-by-step procedures, commands, expected output
- Troubleshooting, error codes, trust store updates

Internal Confluence:
- Runbooks, break-glass policy, release gating checklist, internal triage

### Definition of “aligned with release artifacts”
Docs are considered aligned when:
- Commands and flags match the shipped installer version
- Bundle layout references match the actual bundle
- Error codes/messages match installer output closely enough for Support scripts
- Release notes mention any required doc-visible behavior changes (new trust root, verification behavior changes)

title: SDK Offline Testbed & Integration Scenario Library
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This page documents a reproducible, developer-friendly offline testbed and a curated scenario library for validating SDK integrations (embeddings, generation, streaming, and multi-model orchestration) without requiring access to hosted Redwood environments. The testbed supports fast-feedback loops, deterministic regression checks, and canonical fixes used by Support and DX.

Audience: SDK engineers, client integration engineers, support agents, and developer advocates.

What this document contains:
- High-level architecture and components of the offline testbed.
- Quickstart instructions to spin up a local harness.
- Scenario library (table of concrete test-cases with expected telemetry).
- Template stubs and sample code for Python and Node SDKs.
- Observability contract and canonical-fix triage playbook.
- Maintenance and ownership notes.

Design goals
- Deterministic: deterministic inputs and golden traces for regression detection.
- Low-friction: one-command start for common flows.
- Extensible: add new scenarios and model stubs easily.
- Telemetry-aligned: injects the same telemetry keys used in production to validate instrumentation.

Architecture and components

The testbed is intentionally minimal and portable:
1) Harness CLI: a single binary/script that orchestrates local services and scenarios.
2) Model stubs: small HTTP services that implement a subset of Redwood API surface (streams, chunked responses, embeddings).
3) Request replayer: records and replays client-to-runtime interactions with configurable timings.
4) Golden trace store: a filesystem-based store of request/response + token-level timing and cost metadata.
5) Telemetry shim: local collector that receives SDK telemetry (sampler hooks) and validates presence of required keys.
6) Scenario library: YAML manifests describing input, expected output assertions, and pass/fail thresholds.

Quickstart: local harness (macOS / Linux)
Prerequisites: Docker (for optional containerized stubs), Python 3.10+, Node 18+, git.

1) Clone the repo and install the harness: 
   git clone git@internal:redwood/devx/offline-testbed.git
   cd offline-testbed
   ./scripts/bootstrap.sh

2) Start the testbed (single command):
   ./bin/harness start --profile=fast

   Expected outcome: harness prints three endpoints: STUB_BASE=http://localhost:8081, TELEMETRY=http://localhost:4318, REPLAYER=http://localhost:8090

3) Run a curated scenario:
   ./bin/harness run --scenario=generate-classic-short-completion

   Harness output includes: scenario manifest, run id, PASS/FAIL summary and path to generated golden trace.

4) Run SDK integration smoke (Python):
   python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
   pip install -r examples/python/requirements.txt
   export REDWOOD_API_URL=http://localhost:8081
   python examples/python/generate_smoke.py --api-key test-key --timeout 5s

   Expected: script prints deterministic completion and telemetry assertion status.

Scenario library (selection)

| id | purpose | input summary | expected assertion | telemetry keys validated | notes |
|----|---------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|-------|
| generate-classic-short-completion | Validate single-turn generation and token budgeting | short prompt (<= 64 tokens) | exact-match against golden sample (normalized) | trace_id, latency_ms, tokens_input, tokens_output | used in CI smoke on PRs |
| streaming-chat-chunk-resume | Validate partial-chunk streaming + resume semantics | 3-turn chat stream with cut mid-token | reassembled output equals golden; resume flow produces same final text | stream_id, chunk_index, resume_flag | tests client backpressure handling |
| embeddings-stability-128 | Validate embedding determinism for small vectors | 10 sample sentences | L2 distance to golden <= 1e-6 | embed_set_id, model_ctx | used to detect quantization regressions |
| multi-model-fallback | Validate fallback routing when preferred model returns 503 | generation call to model-A that 503s; fallback to model-B | response from model-B with expected schema | route_policy, fallback_used | exercises Retry/Backoff wiring |
| long-context-kv-cache | Validate KV cache correctness across >8k tokens | staged conversation >10k tokens | KV cache hit rate >= 95% and correctness check | kv_cache_hit, seq_len | heavy memory-eviction scenario |
| embedding-batch-split | Validate batching semantics when client sends 500 items | large batch of sentences | batching windows created; final order preserved | batch_id, batch_size, split_count | asserts idempotent ordering |

Adding a scenario: manifest template (YAML)

# examples/scenarios/template.yaml
name: my-scenario
description: Short description
owner: team-name
runtime: python|node|bash
stages:
  - name: start-stubs
    steps:
      - run: scripts/start-stub --model foo --port 8082
  - name: execute-client
    steps:
      - run: examples/python/run_my_test.py --endpoint ${STUB_BASE}
assertions:
  - type: equals
    path: response.text
    expected: "Hello world"
telemetry_expectations:
  - key: trace_id
    present: true
  - key: tokens_output
    max: 128

SDK stubs and local adapter patterns

Python sample stub (Flask minimal streaming):

from flask import Flask, Response, request
app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/v1/generate', methods=['POST'])
def generate():
    prompts = request.json.get('prompts', [])
    def stream():
        for i, p in enumerate(prompts):
            # deterministic output for test harness
            chunk = f"{p[::-1]}"  # reversed prompt as canonical transformation
            yield f"data: {chunk}

"

    return Response(stream(), mimetype='text/event-stream')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(port=8081)

Node sample: express stub that supports chunked transfer and a /health endpoint. See examples/node/stub.js.

Observability contract (required keys)

Use these checks in scenarios and CI. Telemetry keys below must be emitted by SDKs and the harness shim.
- trace_id (string): unique run id present on every request.
- sdk_version (string): semver of the SDK used for the run.
- latency_ms (number): wall-clock request latency.
- tokens_input, tokens_output (number): token accounting for unit economics tests.
- model_id (string): the model identifier used by the request.
- route_policy (string): routing decision tag when multi-model tests run.

Canonical-fix triage playbook (when a scenario regresses)

1) Reproduce: Run the scenario locally using the same harness commit and run id recorded by CI. Use the golden trace path recorded in the CI artifact.
2) Compare traces: run tools/trace_diff.py <golden> <current> to get token-level diffs and timing deltas.
3) Narrow root cause: check these layers in order: SDK surface (client-side changes), stub runtime (testbed changes), orchestration script, local shim telemetry.
4) If SDK client code changed in last PR: create a focused unit test using the failing scenario and add to SDK PR as required verification.
5) If stub or harness regression: revert the harness commit in a controlled branch and run CI smoke to confirm.
6) Publish the canonical fix: update the scenario manifest and golden trace, increment the scenario schema version, and add an entry to the changelog (scenarios/CHANGELOG.md).

Debugging commands and utilities

- List running services:
  curl http://localhost:8090/status
- Dump last golden trace:
  cat runs/<run-id>/trace.json | jq .
- Run trace comparator:
  python tools/trace_diff.py runs/<golden>/trace.json runs/<failed>/trace.json --summary
- Replay a recorded client session with original timings:
  ./bin/replayer replay --run runs/<id>/client_capture.har

CI integration guidance

- Include quick harness start (profile=ci) in PR checks that touch SDK serialization, streaming, or telemetry.
- Mark stable scenarios as "ci:smoke" in the YAML so they run on fast PR lanes.
- For heavier scenarios (long-context, embedding-batch-split), run nightly with artifacts uploaded to the golden trace bucket.

Ownership and maintenance

- Primary owner: eng-devx-testbed (rotation) — contact: devx-testbed@redwood.com
- Scenario curation: SDK product engineers and Support must jointly review new scenarios. Use the template in CONTRIBUTING.md.
- Versioning: scenario manifests carry a schema_version field; breaking changes require bumping and adding migration notes.

Governance and access

- Golden traces are stored in an internal S3 bucket with read-only access for support and read-write for devx-testbed.
- Do not push secrets into scenario manifests. Use secret placeholders and the local .env loader.

FAQ

Q: Why reverse prompts in the stub example?
A: We use a deterministic transformation (reverse, hash, or predictable tokenization) to make regression diffs obvious while avoiding shipping licensed model artifacts.

Q: Can I add a long-running real-model integration to this testbed?
A: Not in the public harness. For regulated or hosted-model tests, use the dedicated CI that runs against the staging Redwood API and tagged credentials. See related pages for that flow.

Appendix: scenario maintenance checklist

- Confirm deterministic seed and random sources are disabled.
- Validate telemetry keys are emitted for every upstream call.
- Produce and store golden trace under runs/<scenario>/<date>-<commit>.json
- Add a changelog entry and reviewer sign-off before merging updates.

Document history

- 2025-03-11: Created by Priya Natarajan
- 2025-09-02: Added streaming and resume scenario (review: Ethan Morales)
- 2026-01-12: Expanded observability contract and CI guidance (review: Leila Chen)
