  - Onboarding checklist (canonical)
  - Quickstart (language selector)
  - Production readiness (overview)
- **Guides**
  - Streaming
  - Retries & timeouts
  - Error handling (4xx/5xx)
- **Usage & Billing**
  - Usage dashboard
  - Quotas, budgets & alerts
- **Models & Routing**
  - Model selection & pinning
  - Fallback policies
- **Observability**
  - Logging & tracing hooks
  - Metrics & latency breakdown

### Canonical checklist page
Create a single canonical page that mirrors the Console checklist and serves as the docs hub:
- **`/docs/onboarding/checklist`**
  - This page lists steps in order, each with:
    - Short explanation
    - Acceptance criteria (“How to know you’re done”)
    - Links to the canonical supporting page

## Step → docs mapping (Console checklist deep links)
The table below is the required mapping for MVP. URLs are the canonical doc slugs; anchors are included when the deep link should jump into a specific subsection.

> Note: “Console deep link” refers to in-product links into Redwood Console. Those are handled separately; this doc only covers docs destinations.

| Checklist step (Console) | Canonical docs destination | Anchor (if required) | Primary owner (DRI) | Support reviewer | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Create an API key | `/docs/auth/api-keys` | `#create-an-api-key` | Kaitlyn Nguyen | Priya Shah | Include 401/403 troubleshooting + env var naming conventions. |
| Set environment variables | `/docs/auth/api-keys` | `#set-environment-variables` | Kaitlyn Nguyen | Priya Shah | Avoid duplicating across SDK pages; SDK pages link back here. |
| Make your first request (2xx) | `/docs/onboarding/quickstart` | `#first-request` | Wei Chen | Dev Patel | Quickstart is the entry point for first call, but should link to retries/streaming guides. |
| Enable streaming | `/docs/guides/streaming` | `#server-sent-events` | Ibrahim Khan | Priya Shah | Include common disconnect causes, heartbeats, and client timeouts. |
| Add retries & timeouts | `/docs/guides/retries-and-timeouts` | `#recommended-defaults` | Monica Patel | Dev Patel | MUST include language-specific snippets with consistent semantics. |
| Handle rate limits (429) | `/docs/guides/error-handling` | `#rate-limits-429` | Brian Murphy | Priya Shah | Cross-link to quotas/budgets; call out retry-after behavior. |
| View usage dashboard | `/docs/usage/usage-dashboard` | `#data-freshness-and-lag` | Cole Summers | Dev Patel | Explicitly document expected lag + where to confirm requests. |
| Configure quotas & budgets | `/docs/usage/quotas-budgets-alerts` | `#set-a-budget-alert` | Cole Summers | Dev Patel | Include Terraform example link and Console screenshots. |
| Pin a model version | `/docs/models/model-pinning` | `#pinning-in-requests` | Ethan Park | Dev Patel | Clarify “pin by model ID/version” vs “latest”. |
| Configure fallback policy | `/docs/models/fallback-policies` | `#recommended-policies` | Benji Okafor | Sean Gallagher | Tie to reliability: capacity constraints/region fallback; keep it practical. |
| Add basic observability hooks | `/docs/observability/instrumentation` | `#request-ids-and-tracing` | Logan Wright | Aisha Bello | Document request IDs, tracing headers, and log fields (PII-safe). |
| Production launch review (final) | `/docs/onboarding/production-readiness` | `#go-live-checklist` | Jean-Paul Laurent | Dev Patel | A narrative page that summarizes “ready for prod” criteria and common launch pitfalls. |

## Canonical pages to add/update (MVP list)
### New or materially updated pages
1. `/docs/onboarding/checklist`
   - Owner: Jean-Paul Laurent
   - Purpose: mirror Console steps; links to all canonical supporting pages.

2. `/docs/onboarding/production-readiness`
   - Owner: Jean-Paul Laurent
   - Purpose: “before you send prod traffic” overview: quotas/budgets, retries/timeouts, fallbacks, observability.

3. `/docs/guides/retries-and-timeouts`
   - Owner: Monica Patel
   - Purpose: production-safe defaults + rationale; examples per SDK.

4. `/docs/guides/streaming`
   - Owner: Ibrahim Khan
   - Purpose: SSE streaming semantics, disconnect/reconnect, timeouts, proxy considerations.

5. `/docs/usage/usage-dashboard`
   - Owner: Cole Summers
   - Purpose: how to interpret usage; freshness/lag explanation; mismatch troubleshooting.

6. `/docs/usage/quotas-budgets-alerts`
   - Owner: Cole Summers
   - Purpose: rate limits vs quotas vs budgets; recommended alert setup.

7. `/docs/models/model-pinning`
   - Owner: Ethan Park
   - Purpose: model version pinning; rollout guidance; safe upgrades.

8. `/docs/models/fallback-policies`
   - Owner: Benji Okafor
   - Purpose: fallback rules; regions; compatible model variants; failure modes.

9. `/docs/observability/instrumentation`
   - Owner: Logan Wright
   - Purpose: request IDs, tracing headers, metrics names; minimal “first hooks” section.

### Pages to keep but re-scope (reduce duplication)
- `/docs/onboarding/quickstart`
  - Keep as “first call” and “minimal working app”, but:
    - Link out to streaming + retries pages instead of embedding full guidance.
    - Add callouts: “If you saw 401/403…”, “If you saw 429…”, “If usage didn’t show up yet…”.

## Redirect plan
We expect existing customers and search traffic to hit older “getting started” and SDK snippet pages. We must preserve SEO and avoid 404s.

### Redirect policy
- Use **301 redirects** for renamed/moved pages.
- Avoid chain redirects (A → B → C). Always redirect legacy → canonical.
- Keep redirects for at least **12 months** post-launch; evaluate removal based on traffic.

### Known legacy paths to redirect (initial set)
| Legacy path | Redirect to | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `/docs/getting-started` | `/docs/onboarding/quickstart` | Consolidate entry point. |
| `/docs/quickstart` | `/docs/onboarding/quickstart` | Normalize nav + URL. |
| `/docs/authentication` | `/docs/auth/api-keys` | Remove duplicate auth pages. |
| `/docs/api-keys` | `/docs/auth/api-keys` | Canonicalize. |
| `/docs/streaming-chat` | `/docs/guides/streaming` | Old guide slug. |
| `/docs/retries` | `/docs/guides/retries-and-timeouts` | Old guide slug. |
| `/docs/rate-limits` | `/docs/guides/error-handling#rate-limits-429` | Direct users to 429 section. |
| `/docs/usage` | `/docs/usage/usage-dashboard` | Reduce ambiguity. |
| `/docs/quotas` | `/docs/usage/quotas-budgets-alerts` | Consolidate quota/budget content. |

### Redirect implementation (example)
Implementation depends on the docs stack configuration. For `redwood-docs`, redirects are typically declared in a central config file (example only):

- `redirects.yml`
  - `- from: /docs/getting-started`
  - `  to: /docs/onboarding/quickstart`

Owners should add a small unit check (CI) to prevent:
- Duplicate `from` entries
- Redirect chains
- Redirects to non-existent slugs

## Ownership and maintenance
### Page-level ownership expectations
Each canonical page must have:
- A DRI (owner) accountable for:
  - Accuracy and freshness
  - Quarterly review
  - Responding to Support escalations related to that page
- A secondary reviewer from Support/CS for troubleshooting quality (esp. auth/429/streaming)

### Ownership table (MVP)
| Area | DRI | Backup | Review cadence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Onboarding checklist hub | Jean-Paul Laurent | Wei Chen | Monthly during rollout; quarterly after |
| Quickstart | Wei Chen | Sabrina James | Quarterly |
| SDK production defaults | Monica Patel | Ibrahim Khan | Per SDK release; quarterly |
| Usage/quota docs | Cole Summers | Mei Lin | Quarterly |
| Observability instrumentation | Logan Wright | Allison Grant | Quarterly |
| Models/pinning/fallback | Ethan Park / Benji Okafor | Sean Gallagher | Quarterly |

## Console deep-linking requirements for docs (contract)
To keep Console links stable:
- Each canonical page should include **explicit anchor IDs** for the sections referenced in the mapping table.
- Avoid renaming headings that are used for deep links without adding an anchor alias (if supported) or a redirect to a section.
- Add `utm_source=console&utm_medium=onboarding_checklist&utm_campaign=ttfs_ttfp` to external doc links from Console (Console implementation detail; included here so docs can filter analytics).

## Analytics requirements (docs-side)
Docs should enable measurement of:
- Page views and CTR from `/docs/onboarding/checklist` to the supporting guides
- Search queries leading to auth/429/streaming pages
- Bounce rate improvements after IA changes

Minimum tagging:
- Ensure docs analytics captures UTM parameters.
- Ensure the checklist hub page links use consistent UTM structure so we can separate Console-driven vs web-driven traffic.

## QA checklist for IA changes
Before publishing:
1. All canonical pages exist and are linked from `/docs/onboarding/checklist`.
2. All anchors referenced in the mapping table resolve (no 404/scroll failures).
3. Redirects are deployed and validated (spot-check the legacy URLs above).
4. Quickstart pages for Python/TypeScript/Go link to the canonical guides rather than duplicating.
5. Support has reviewed auth, streaming disconnect, 429, and usage lag troubleshooting sections.

## Open questions / follow-ups
1. Do we need a dedicated page for “Streaming disconnects” or is it a section under `/docs/guides/streaming`?
   - Current plan: section under streaming guide; revisit if ticket volume persists after launch.
2. Should “Budgets vs quotas vs rate limits” live on one page or split?
   - Current plan: one canonical page (`/docs/usage/quotas-budgets-alerts`) with a clear conceptual breakdown.
3. Do we standardize SDK page structure across languages?
   - MVP: only standardize the “Production readiness” section and link to shared guides.

## Changelog
- 2025-03-06: Initial IA proposal drafted (Elliot Price) with step→page mapping.
- 2025-03-18: Added usage dashboard freshness/lag anchor requirement after Support review (Dev Patel, Priya Shah).
- 2025-03-26: Added model pinning + fallback policy pages to align with “first production launch” checklist (Ethan Park, Benji Okafor).
- 2025-04-03: Finalized redirect list and ownership table; marked published for Phase 2 cohort rollout.

title: First Production Launch Checklist (Canonical Template)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the **canonical step catalog** for the “Guided onboarding + First Production Launch” checklist.

This template is the **single source of truth** for:
- Step IDs (stable identifiers used by Console + progress service + telemetry)
- Step ordering, dependencies, and completion criteria
- Deep links in the Redwood Console
- Canonical docs + quickstart/example links (with language parity expectations)

This template is used in:
- **Redwood Console** checklist UI
- **Docs** (mirrors the same steps and order)
- **Support playbooks** (triage by step state)

---

## Template versioning and change control
**Template version:** 1.0

**Rules**
1. **Step IDs are immutable.** Do not reuse an ID for a different meaning.
2. Any change that affects completion logic (criteria or event mapping) requires review from:
   - DevEx (Docs/Quickstarts): Wei Chen / Elliot Price
   - Console PM/Eng: Marissa Cole / Dylan Brooks
   - Telemetry: Logan Wright
   - Support enablement: Dev Patel
3. Backward compatibility: existing tenants’ progress must not regress due to template edits.

**Update cadence**
- Minor copy/link changes: anytime (no code changes required).
- Criteria/event changes: batched; target 1–2 releases/month.

---

## Definitions
- **TTFS (Time to First Success):** elapsed time from tenant creation → first successful (2xx) Hosted API response for a supported endpoint.
- **TTFP (Time to First Production Traffic):** elapsed time from tenant creation → first request classified as production traffic per telemetry rules.

**Checklist step states**
- Not started: no evidence yet
- In progress: partial evidence (where applicable)
- Completed: acceptance criteria met
- Blocked: step cannot complete due to detected error state (Console may show guidance)

---

## Supported environments (MVP)
This template is authored for **Hosted API** onboarding (self-serve and sales-assisted). Dedicated/Private steps will be added later.

Supported language tracks for “Quickstart/Examples” links:
- Python
- TypeScript/Node
- Go
- cURL

Parity expectation: each step should have at least one canonical docs URL and at least two language tracks with working code samples by GA.

---

## Canonical step catalog (ordered)

### Step 1 — Create an API key
**Step ID:** ONB_API_KEY_CREATED

**Goal:** Ensure the developer has a valid key and knows where to rotate/revoke it.

**Acceptance criteria (completion):**
- At least one active API key exists for the tenant in the Console.

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/settings/api-keys

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/api-keys
- Quickstart (cURL): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#curl

**Common pitfalls to address in step detail UI:**
- Using the wrong environment variable name
- Copying a key with extra whitespace
- Using an org admin key in a restricted project context

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: api_key.created (source=console)

---

### Step 2 — Configure environment (SDK + env vars)
**Step ID:** ONB_ENV_CONFIGURED

**Goal:** Ensure the key is stored safely and the SDK can authenticate reliably.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Tenant has at least one successful authenticated request (2xx) OR explicit verification event from Console “Test connection”.

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/developer/quickstart

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/authentication#environment-variables
- Quickstart (Python): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#python
- Quickstart (TypeScript): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#typescript
- Quickstart (Go): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#go

**Notes:**
- Step detail should recommend using a secrets manager for production deployments.

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: auth.success (first occurrence)
- Optional client hint: sdk.initialized (no secrets; SDK name/version only)

---

### Step 3 — Make your first successful request (non-streaming)
**Step ID:** ONB_FIRST_2XX_REQUEST

**Goal:** Achieve TTFS and validate basic request/response.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- First 2xx response from one of: /v1/chat/completions, /v1/responses, /v1/embeddings, /v1/rerank (Hosted API).

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/usage/requests (filtered to last 15 minutes)

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/first-request
- Example (TypeScript): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/first-request-ts)
- Example (Python): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/first-request-py)

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: request.2xx.first

---

### Step 4 — Try streaming (and handle disconnects)
**Step ID:** ONB_STREAMING_ESTABLISHED

**Goal:** Validate streaming works end-to-end and the client behaves correctly on disconnect.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- At least one streaming response established (server confirms stream opened and at least one token chunk delivered).

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/developer/streaming

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/streaming
- Troubleshooting: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/streaming#disconnects
- Example (prod-ready): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/streaming-chat-prod-ready)

**Implementation guidance to surface in step detail:**
- Recommend read timeouts appropriate for long generations
- Recommend reconnect pattern (resume vs retry) and how to avoid duplicate side effects

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: streaming.opened.first
- Server-side event: streaming.chunk.sent.first

---

### Step 5 — Set production-safe timeouts and retries
**Step ID:** ONB_RETRIES_TIMEOUTS_CONFIGURED

**Goal:** Prevent common early-production failures (hanging requests, spiky error rates, retry storms).

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Tenant has generated at least one request using an SDK client configured with recommended presets OR Console “Prod defaults acknowledged” action (for non-SDK use cases).

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/developer/production-defaults

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production-readiness/timeouts-retries
- Python SDK guide: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/python#retries-timeouts
- TypeScript SDK guide: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/typescript#retries-timeouts
- Go SDK guide: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/go#retries-timeouts

**Recommended defaults (for docs + SDK presets):**
- Connect timeout: 2–5s
- Overall request timeout: 30–90s (non-streaming); streaming uses read timeout/keepalive guidance
- Retry policy: exponential backoff with jitter; retry on 429/408/409/5xx where safe
- Max attempts: 3–5 (cap total retry time)

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Client hint (preferred): sdk.retry_policy.enabled (no config values, only preset name)
- Server-side fallback: request.retry_observed (inferred; less reliable)

---

### Step 6 — Review usage and latency in the Console
**Step ID:** ONB_USAGE_DASHBOARD_VIEWED

**Goal:** Ensure the user knows where to see token usage, latency, and errors; reduce “usage dashboard mismatch” tickets.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- User visits usage dashboard page OR API call to /usage summary is made.

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/usage/overview

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing/usage-dashboard
- Notes on data freshness: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing#data-freshness

**Copy requirements (Console step detail):**
- Clearly state expected ingestion delay (e.g., “Usage may take up to ~5 minutes to fully reflect in dashboards.”)

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: console.page_view (page=usage_overview)

---

### Step 7 — Configure quotas and budget alerts
**Step ID:** ONB_QUOTAS_BUDGETS_SET

**Goal:** Prevent surprise spend and reduce 429 confusion by making limits explicit.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Tenant has set at least one of:
  - Monthly budget alert threshold, OR
  - Per-minute request/token limit policy, OR
  - Project-level quota override

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/settings/quotas-budgets

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing/quotas-budgets
- Terraform snippet: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-terraform (path: examples/budget-alerts)

**Guidance to surface in step detail:**
- Explain how 429s work (quota vs rate limit vs concurrency)
- Recommend adding client-side backoff and server-side circuit-breakers

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: quotas.updated
- Server-side event: budgets.updated

---

### Step 8 — Pin a model version (avoid surprise changes)
**Step ID:** ONB_MODEL_PINNED

**Goal:** Stabilize production behavior by pinning a model and (where applicable) a revision.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Tenant has set a default model for the project and pinned to a specific version/revision OR explicitly acknowledged “floating model” risk.

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/models (then “Set default”)

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/models/model-versioning
- Quickstart section: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#model-pinning

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: model.default_set
- Server-side event: model.version_pinned

---

### Step 9 — Configure fallback policy (reliability under load)
**Step ID:** ONB_FALLBACK_POLICY_CONFIGURED

**Goal:** Ensure the application stays online during capacity constraints or model-specific incidents.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- A fallback policy exists for at least one route (same family or compatible variant), OR the tenant acknowledges “no fallback” for the project.

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/routing/fallbacks

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/reliability/fallbacks
- Quickstart section: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#fallback-policy

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: routing.fallback_policy.updated

---

### Step 10 — Add basic observability hooks (request IDs + tracing)
**Step ID:** ONB_OBSERVABILITY_ENABLED

**Goal:** Make debugging first production incidents fast (correlate customer logs ↔ Redwood request IDs, add traces/metrics).

**Acceptance criteria:**
- At least one of:
  - Request ID propagation enabled in client logs, OR
  - Tracing headers enabled and seen by gateway, OR
  - Webhook/log export integration configured (if available)

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/observability/overview

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/observability/request-ids
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/observability/tracing
- Example: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/observability-headers)

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: observability.request_id_seen
- Server-side event: observability.trace_context_seen

---

### Step 11 — Send first production traffic
**Step ID:** ONB_FIRST_PRODUCTION_TRAFFIC

**Goal:** Mark TTFP and ensure “go-live” is measurable.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- First request classified as production traffic according to telemetry rules (e.g., environment tag, header, allowlisted domain, or production API key).

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/usage/requests?filter=production

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production/go-live
- Checklist doc (canonical): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/onboarding/first-production-launch-checklist

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: request.production.first

---

## Step-to-language link map (minimum requirements)
The following table is used by Docs + Console step detail drawers. If a language link is missing, the step detail must fall back to the canonical docs page.

- ONB_ENV_CONFIGURED | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/authentication#environment-variables | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#python | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#typescript | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#go | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#curl | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-quickstarts
- ONB_FIRST_2XX_REQUEST | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/first-request | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#python | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#typescript | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#go | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#curl | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples

- ONB_RETRIES_TIMEOUTS_CONFIGURED | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production-readiness/timeouts-retries | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/python#retries-timeouts | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/typescript#retries-timeouts | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/go#retries-timeouts | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production-readiness/timeouts-retries#curl | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-quickstarts

---

## Console UX requirements (template-driven)
The Console implementation should treat this template as authoritative for:
- Step ordering
- Step titles (short) and step detail copy (long)
- Deep links

**Copy constraints (for step titles):**
- 42 characters max (including spaces)
- Verb-first (“Configure…”, “Add…”, “Review…”)

**Step detail requirements:**
- Must show: why it matters, how to complete, and troubleshooting for the top failure mode
- Must include at least one docs link

---

## Support enablement notes (what Support should see)
For each step, Support tooling should be able to display:
- Step state (not started/in progress/completed/blocked)
- Last evidence timestamp
- Evidence type (Console action vs server-side event)

Recommended macros (to be maintained in Support playbook):
- “401/403 during quickstart” → Steps 1–3
- “Streaming disconnect confusion” → Step 4 + Step 5
- “429 surprise” → Step 7 + Step 5
- “Usage dashboard mismatch” → Step 6

---

## Open questions (tracked outside this template)
- Final production traffic classification signal(s) for ONB_FIRST_PRODUCTION_TRAFFIC
- Whether ONB_RETRIES_TIMEOUTS_CONFIGURED should require SDK detection or allow an “I’ve configured this” acknowledgment
- Whether ONB_MODEL_PINNED should be mandatory for self-serve Hosted API go-live

(See PRD and ADR for current decisions and rationale.)
text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **canonical step catalog** for the “Guided onboarding + First Production Launch” checklist.

This template is the **single source of truth** for:
- Step IDs (stable identifiers used by Console + progress service + telemetry)
- Step ordering, dependencies, and completion criteria
- Deep links in the Redwood Console
- Canonical docs + quickstart/example links (with language parity expectations)

This template is used in:
- **Redwood Console** checklist UI
- **Docs** (mirrors the same steps and order)
- **Support playbooks** (triage by step state)

---

## Template versioning and change control
**Template version:** 1.0

**Rules**
1. **Step IDs are immutable.** Do not reuse an ID for a different meaning.
2. Any change that affects completion logic (criteria or event mapping) requires review from:
   - DevEx (Docs/Quickstarts): Wei Chen / Elliot Price
   - Console PM/Eng: Marissa Cole / Dylan Brooks
   - Telemetry: Logan Wright
   - Support enablement: Dev Patel
3. Backward compatibility: existing tenants’ progress must not regress due to template edits.

**Update cadence**
- Minor copy/link changes: anytime (no code changes required).
- Criteria/event changes: batched; target 1–2 releases/month.

---

## Definitions
- **TTFS (Time to First Success):** elapsed time from tenant creation → first successful (2xx) Hosted API response for a supported endpoint.
- **TTFP (Time to First Production Traffic):** elapsed time from tenant creation → first request classified as production traffic per telemetry rules.

**Checklist step states**
- Not started: no evidence yet
- In progress: partial evidence (where applicable)
- Completed: acceptance criteria met
- Blocked: step cannot complete due to detected error state (Console may show guidance)

---

## Supported environments (MVP)
This template is authored for **Hosted API** onboarding (self-serve and sales-assisted). Dedicated/Private steps will be added later.

Supported language tracks for “Quickstart/Examples” links:
- Python
- TypeScript/Node
- Go
- cURL

Parity expectation: each step should have at least one canonical docs URL and at least two language tracks with working code samples by GA.

---

## Canonical step catalog (ordered)

### Step 1 — Create an API key
**Step ID:** ONB_API_KEY_CREATED

**Goal:** Ensure the developer has a valid key and knows where to rotate/revoke it.

**Acceptance criteria (completion):**
- At least one active API key exists for the tenant in the Console.

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/settings/api-keys

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/api-keys
- Quickstart (cURL): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#curl

**Common pitfalls to address in step detail UI:**
- Using the wrong environment variable name
- Copying a key with extra whitespace
- Using an org admin key in a restricted project context

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: api_key.created (source=console)

---

### Step 2 — Configure environment (SDK + env vars)
**Step ID:** ONB_ENV_CONFIGURED

**Goal:** Ensure the key is stored safely and the SDK can authenticate reliably.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Tenant has at least one successful authenticated request (2xx) OR explicit verification event from Console “Test connection”.

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/developer/quickstart

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/authentication#environment-variables
- Quickstart (Python): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#python
- Quickstart (TypeScript): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#typescript
- Quickstart (Go): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#go

**Notes:**
- Step detail should recommend using a secrets manager for production deployments.

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: auth.success (first occurrence)
- Optional client hint: sdk.initialized (no secrets; SDK name/version only)

---

### Step 3 — Make your first successful request (non-streaming)
**Step ID:** ONB_FIRST_2XX_REQUEST

**Goal:** Achieve TTFS and validate basic request/response.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- First 2xx response from one of: /v1/chat/completions, /v1/responses, /v1/embeddings, /v1/rerank (Hosted API).

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/usage/requests (filtered to last 15 minutes)

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/first-request
- Example (TypeScript): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/first-request-ts)
- Example (Python): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/first-request-py)

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: request.2xx.first

---

### Step 4 — Try streaming (and handle disconnects)
**Step ID:** ONB_STREAMING_ESTABLISHED

**Goal:** Validate streaming works end-to-end and the client behaves correctly on disconnect.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- At least one streaming response established (server confirms stream opened and at least one token chunk delivered).

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/developer/streaming

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/streaming
- Troubleshooting: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/streaming#disconnects
- Example (prod-ready): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/streaming-chat-prod-ready)

**Implementation guidance to surface in step detail:**
- Recommend read timeouts appropriate for long generations
- Recommend reconnect pattern (resume vs retry) and how to avoid duplicate side effects

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: streaming.opened.first
- Server-side event: streaming.chunk.sent.first

---

### Step 5 — Set production-safe timeouts and retries
**Step ID:** ONB_RETRIES_TIMEOUTS_CONFIGURED

**Goal:** Prevent common early-production failures (hanging requests, spiky error rates, retry storms).

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Tenant has generated at least one request using an SDK client configured with recommended presets OR Console “Prod defaults acknowledged” action (for non-SDK use cases).

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/developer/production-defaults

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production-readiness/timeouts-retries
- Python SDK guide: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/python#retries-timeouts
- TypeScript SDK guide: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/typescript#retries-timeouts
- Go SDK guide: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/go#retries-timeouts

**Recommended defaults (for docs + SDK presets):**
- Connect timeout: 2–5s
- Overall request timeout: 30–90s (non-streaming); streaming uses read timeout/keepalive guidance
- Retry policy: exponential backoff with jitter; retry on 429/408/409/5xx where safe
- Max attempts: 3–5 (cap total retry time)

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Client hint (preferred): sdk.retry_policy.enabled (no config values, only preset name)
- Server-side fallback: request.retry_observed (inferred; less reliable)

---

### Step 6 — Review usage and latency in the Console
**Step ID:** ONB_USAGE_DASHBOARD_VIEWED

**Goal:** Ensure the user knows where to see token usage, latency, and errors; reduce “usage dashboard mismatch” tickets.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- User visits usage dashboard page OR API call to /usage summary is made.

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/usage/overview

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing/usage-dashboard
- Notes on data freshness: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing#data-freshness

**Copy requirements (Console step detail):**
- Clearly state expected ingestion delay (e.g., “Usage may take up to ~5 minutes to fully reflect in dashboards.”)

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: console.page_view (page=usage_overview)

---

### Step 7 — Configure quotas and budget alerts
**Step ID:** ONB_QUOTAS_BUDGETS_SET

**Goal:** Prevent surprise spend and reduce 429 confusion by making limits explicit.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Tenant has set at least one of:
  - Monthly budget alert threshold, OR
  - Per-minute request/token limit policy, OR
  - Project-level quota override

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/settings/quotas-budgets

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing/quotas-budgets
- Terraform snippet: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-terraform (path: examples/budget-alerts)

**Guidance to surface in step detail:**
- Explain how 429s work (quota vs rate limit vs concurrency)
- Recommend adding client-side backoff and server-side circuit-breakers

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: quotas.updated
- Server-side event: budgets.updated

---

### Step 8 — Pin a model version (avoid surprise changes)
**Step ID:** ONB_MODEL_PINNED

**Goal:** Stabilize production behavior by pinning a model and (where applicable) a revision.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Tenant has set a default model for the project and pinned to a specific version/revision OR explicitly acknowledged “floating model” risk.

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/models (then “Set default”)

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/models/model-versioning
- Quickstart section: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#model-pinning

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: model.default_set
- Server-side event: model.version_pinned

---

### Step 9 — Configure fallback policy (reliability under load)
**Step ID:** ONB_FALLBACK_POLICY_CONFIGURED

**Goal:** Ensure the application stays online during capacity constraints or model-specific incidents.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- A fallback policy exists for at least one route (same family or compatible variant), OR the tenant acknowledges “no fallback” for the project.

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/routing/fallbacks

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/reliability/fallbacks
- Quickstart section: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#fallback-policy

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: routing.fallback_policy.updated

---

### Step 10 — Add basic observability hooks (request IDs + tracing)
**Step ID:** ONB_OBSERVABILITY_ENABLED

**Goal:** Make debugging first production incidents fast (correlate customer logs ↔ Redwood request IDs, add traces/metrics).

**Acceptance criteria:**
- At least one of:
  - Request ID propagation enabled in client logs, OR
  - Tracing headers enabled and seen by gateway, OR
  - Webhook/log export integration configured (if available)

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/observability/overview

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/observability/request-ids
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/observability/tracing
- Example: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/observability-headers)

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: observability.request_id_seen
- Server-side event: observability.trace_context_seen

---

### Step 11 — Send first production traffic
**Step ID:** ONB_FIRST_PRODUCTION_TRAFFIC

**Goal:** Mark TTFP and ensure “go-live” is measurable.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- First request classified as production traffic according to telemetry rules (e.g., environment tag, header, allowlisted domain, or production API key).

**Recommended Console deep link:** /console/usage/requests?filter=production

**Docs / quickstarts:**
- Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production/go-live
- Checklist doc (canonical): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/onboarding/first-production-launch-checklist

**Telemetry / progress evidence:**
- Server-side event: request.production.first

---

## Step-to-language link map (minimum requirements)
The following table is used by Docs + Console step detail drawers. If a language link is missing, the step detail must fall back to the canonical docs page.

Table columns: Step ID | Docs | Python | TypeScript | Go | cURL | Example repo

- ONB_API_KEY_CREATED | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/api-keys | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#python | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#typescript | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#go | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#curl | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-quickstarts
- ONB_ENV_CONFIGURED | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/authentication#environment-variables | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#python | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#typescript | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#go | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#curl | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-quickstarts
- ONB_FIRST_2XX_REQUEST | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/first-request | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#python | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#typescript | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#go | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#curl | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples
- ONB_STREAMING_ESTABLISHED | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/streaming | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/streaming#python | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/streaming#typescript | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/streaming#go | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/streaming#curl | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples
- ONB_RETRIES_TIMEOUTS_CONFIGURED | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production-readiness/timeouts-retries | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/python#retries-timeouts | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/typescript#retries-timeouts | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/go#retries-timeouts | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production-readiness/timeouts-retries#curl | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-quickstarts
- ONB_USAGE_DASHBOARD_VIEWED | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing/usage-dashboard | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A
- ONB_QUOTAS_BUDGETS_SET | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing/quotas-budgets | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-terraform
- ONB_MODEL_PINNED | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/models/model-versioning | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#model-pinning | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#model-pinning | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#model-pinning | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#model-pinning | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-quickstarts
- ONB_FALLBACK_POLICY_CONFIGURED | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/reliability/fallbacks | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#fallback-policy | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#fallback-policy | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#fallback-policy | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#fallback-policy | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-quickstarts
- ONB_OBSERVABILITY_ENABLED | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/observability/request-ids | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/observability/tracing#python | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/observability/tracing#typescript | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/observability/tracing#go | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/observability/tracing#curl | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples
- ONB_FIRST_PRODUCTION_TRAFFIC | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production/go-live | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production/go-live#python | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production/go-live#typescript | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production/go-live#go | https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production/go-live#curl | https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples

---

## Console UX requirements (template-driven)
The Console implementation should treat this template as authoritative for:
- Step ordering
- Step titles (short) and step detail copy (long)
- Deep links

**Copy constraints (for step titles):**
- 42 characters max (including spaces)
- Verb-first (“Configure…”, “Add…”, “Review…”)

**Step detail requirements:**
- Must show: why it matters, how to complete, and troubleshooting for the top failure mode
- Must include at least one docs link

---

## Support enablement notes (what Support should see)
For each step, Support tooling should be able to display:
- Step state (not started/in progress/completed/blocked)
- Last evidence timestamp
- Evidence type (Console action vs server-side event)

Recommended macros (to be maintained in Support playbook):
- “401/403 during quickstart” → Steps 1–3
- “Streaming disconnect confusion” → Step 4 + Step 5
- “429 surprise” → Step 7 + Step 5
- “Usage dashboard mismatch” → Step 6

---

## Open questions (tracked outside this template)
- Final production traffic classification signal(s) for ONB_FIRST_PRODUCTION_TRAFFIC
- Whether ONB_RETRIES_TIMEOUTS_CONFIGURED should require SDK detection or allow an “I’ve configured this” acknowledgment
- Whether ONB_MODEL_PINNED should be mandatory for self-serve Hosted API go-live

(See PRD and ADR for current decisions and rationale.)

title: Evals and Regression Testing for Quickstart Templates
preview:
## Overview
This document defines how Redwood Inference Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents, Python and TypeScript) are evaluated and regression-tested. The intent is to catch:
- Functional regressions (retrieval correctness, tool calling behavior, structured output parsing)
- Reliability regressions (streaming disconnect handling, retries/backoff, timeouts)
- Breaking changes in SDKs/dependencies that impact template users
- Observability regressions (missing spans/metrics, log redaction failures)

We implement a layered strategy:
1) **Golden prompt sets** (deterministic-ish behavioral checks)
2) **Offline eval harness integration** (repeatable scoring and trending)
3) **Smoke tests** (streaming + tools + basic RAG end-to-end)
4) **CI quality gates** (release blocking where appropriate)

## Goals
- Make template updates safe to ship without requiring maintainers to manually validate every behavior.
- Detect regressions that show up most often in support tickets: 401/429 handling, streaming disconnects, tool schema errors, empty retrieval, misconfigured env.
- Keep the test suite maintainable and fast enough for every PR.
- Ensure templates remain “production-minded by default”: safe defaults are verified continuously.

## Non-goals
- Creating a full model-quality benchmarking suite across the entire model catalog.
- Guaranteeing bit-for-bit identical outputs from probabilistic generation.
- Measuring absolute quality vs other vendors/providers (templates aim to be provider-agnostic; CI verifies our integration contracts).

## Definitions
- **Template**: the scaffolded quickstart app (Python/TS) generated via repo template or CLI.
- **Golden prompt**: a prompt + expected invariants (not necessarily exact text match).
- **Eval**: repeatable checks that produce a score and/or pass/fail, recorded over time.
- **Smoke test**: short end-to-end test intended to catch integration breakage (networking, streaming, tools) rather than deep quality.

## What we test (coverage map)
### RAG baseline
- Ingestion/chunking produces non-empty index
- Retrieval returns top-k items with correct schema
- Answer includes citations or provenance fields (template-defined output)
- Handles empty retrieval (returns “I don’t know”/fallback path, not hallucinated citations)

### Agent + tool calling baseline
- Tool schema validation errors are surfaced as typed errors (not raw exceptions)
- Tool call loop respects guardrails:
  - max iterations
  - tool output size limit
  - tool timeout
- Tool failures are logged with error taxonomy and include correlation IDs

### Streaming resilience baseline
- Streaming works end-to-end with expected event framing
- Disconnect/reconnect behavior does not duplicate tokens beyond the template’s documented guarantee
- Timeout and retry defaults follow the “Safe defaults” policy

### Observability baseline
- Required spans are emitted (see Observability Hooks Standard)
- Required metrics increment at least once during the smoke test
- Logs redact secrets/PII patterns by default

## Test assets layout (repo conventions)
Each template repo must include the following structure:

- `evals/`
  - `goldens/`
    - `rag/` (RAG prompt sets)
    - `agent/` (tool calling prompt sets)
  - `datasets/` (small, non-sensitive sample documents)
  - `configs/` (eval runner configuration)
  - `README.md` (how to run evals locally)
- `tests/`
  - `smoke/` (streaming + tool + RAG e2e)
  - `unit/` (schema validation, error mapping, retriever adapters)

**Security note:** all datasets must be synthetic or publicly redistributable and must not contain customer data.

## Golden prompt sets
Golden prompts are defined as **invariants** instead of strict output matching. We require each golden case to specify:
- Input prompt (and optional conversation history)
- Required fields in the structured response
- Constraints (e.g., must include 1–3 citations, must not claim it searched the web unless a tool was called)
- Tool behavior expectations (e.g., tool must be called when needed, must not call tool for trivial queries)

### Golden prompt format
We standardize on a JSONL format for simplicity:
- One case per line
- Minimal fields
- Portable across Python and TypeScript runners

Example (`evals/goldens/rag/basic.jsonl`):
- `id`: stable identifier
- `prompt`: user prompt
- `expected_invariants`: list of invariants to validate
- `tags`: for filtering

Example invariants we support:
- `contains_regex:<pattern>`
- `json_path_exists:answer.citations`
- `json_path_len_between:answer.citations,1,3`
- `must_not_contain_regex:searched the web`
- `tool_called:retriever.search`

### Managing flakiness
Golden prompts can become flaky due to model updates or sampling randomness. We mitigate by:
- Using low temperature by default in eval runs (unless the template’s default is higher, in which case we override in eval config)
- Validating invariants, not exact phrasing
- Versioning prompt sets (see “Versioning and updates”)

## Offline eval harness integration
Templates integrate with Redwood’s internal eval harness runner to:
- Run goldens and record pass rate by template version
- Track drift and regressions over time
- Produce CI artifacts (HTML/JSON summaries)

### Required eval outputs
Every eval run must produce:
- A machine-readable summary (`evals/out/summary.json`)
- A human-readable report (`evals/out/report.md` or HTML)
- Raw results for debugging (`evals/out/results.jsonl`)

### Score and thresholds
For the v1 template release gate:
- **Golden pass rate**: >= 95% on required suites (rag/basic, agent/tools-basic)
- **Hard fails** (0 tolerance):
  - Missing required structured fields
  - Tool schema validation exceptions uncaught
  - “Citation fabrication” invariant violated (citations present but not sourced from retriever results)

Notes:
- Thresholds are intentionally conservative to avoid blocking on minor model phrasing changes.
- Any reduction in pass rate > 2% vs main branch baseline requires review by DevEx (Monica Patel or Sabrina James).

### How to run locally
We support a single entrypoint per template:
- Python: `make eval` and `python -m evals.run --suite rag/basic`
- TypeScript: `pnpm eval` and `node ./evals/run.js --suite agent/tools-basic`

Local runs should support:
- Selecting suite(s)
- Selecting provider backend (Redwood default)
- Saving artifacts to `evals/out/`

## Smoke tests (CI-first, fast)
Smoke tests validate template behaviors most likely to break due to SDK or infra changes.

### Required smoke test scenarios
1) **Streaming happy path**
   - Start template server
   - Request streaming completion
   - Assert event stream is well-formed
   - Assert final response includes request ID and correlation metadata

2) **Streaming disconnect + retry**
   - Simulate mid-stream disconnect (client-side cancel or proxy drop)
   - Verify template reconnect strategy is invoked (where supported)
   - Verify surfaced error is typed and actionable if reconnect fails

3) **Tool calling**
   - Ask a question that requires a tool call
   - Validate tool schema is enforced
   - Validate max-iteration guard prevents infinite loops

4) **RAG end-to-end**
   - Ingest a tiny sample dataset (<= 10 docs)
   - Query and validate:
     - non-empty retrieved docs
     - citations/provenance present
     - empty-retrieval fallback works on an unrelated query

5) **Observability sanity**
   - Assert at least one trace span exported (or captured) with required attributes
   - Assert logs include `redwood.request_id` and redact configured sensitive fields

### Execution constraints
- Target runtime: <= 10 minutes total for all smoke tests per PR.
- Tests must run in a clean environment (no reliance on developer machine state).
- Avoid high-cardinality tagging in assertions (do not assert on randomly generated IDs beyond presence/format).

## CI quality gates
We define three CI tiers:

### Tier 1: PR checks (required)
- Lint + typecheck
- Unit tests
- Smoke tests (streaming + tools + RAG)
- Dependency vulnerability scan (per Secure SDLC policy)

### Tier 2: PR checks (non-blocking, informational)
- Full golden suite across all categories
- Optional matrix across supported models (nightly only by default)

### Tier 3: Release checks (required)
- Tier 1 must be green
- Golden pass rate meets threshold for required suites
- Release candidate tagged with template version and changelog updated

## Release gating policy
A template release cannot be cut unless:
- Required suites meet thresholds
- No open P0/P1 issues tagged `template-release-blocker`
- Observability hooks match the current standard (span/metric names) OR the release notes clearly call out deviations

Approval required for release:
- DevEx engineering lead (Monica Patel)
- SRE reviewer sign-off for changes that modify retry/timeout/streaming behavior (Christine Wong)

## Baselines, trending, and regression triage
### Baseline strategy
- `main` branch results are the baseline.
- For releases, we snapshot baseline artifacts and attach to the release tag.

### Regression triage checklist
When a suite fails:
1) Confirm failure is reproducible (rerun suite locally + in CI)
2) Classify:
   - Test harness issue
   - Model/provider drift
   - Template code regression
   - Dependency/SDK regression
3) Determine fix path:
   - If drift: update invariants only with DevEx approval and version bump prompt set
   - If code regression: fix template, add targeted unit test
   - If SDK regression: open SDK ticket and pin/patch as needed

### Flaky test handling
- Any flaky test observed >= 3 times in 7 days must be:
  - quarantined (moved to non-blocking) OR
  - fixed with deterministic assertions
- Quarantined tests require an owner and target date to re-enable.

## Versioning and updates
### Golden prompt set versioning
Golden sets are versioned independently from template versions:
- Directory naming: `evals/goldens/rag/v1/`, `evals/goldens/rag/v2/`
- Template release notes must state which golden version was used for gating.

### Updating goldens
Allowed reasons:
- Template behavior intentionally changes (documented in changelog)
- Observed provider/model behavior changes and invariants need to be made more robust

Not allowed:
- Relaxing invariants to hide real regressions (must include rationale + reviewer sign-off)

## Data handling and privacy
- Do not log prompts/responses in CI artifacts unless explicitly scrubbed.
- Eval runner must support redaction of:
  - API keys
  - Authorization headers
  - Email addresses
  - Phone numbers
  - Tokens that match configured secret patterns
- Keep eval datasets small and non-sensitive.

## Ownership and maintenance
- Primary owner: **Sabrina James** (Examples + template test coverage)
- Backup owner: **Dana Lo** (TypeScript template + SDK integration)
- Reviewers for changes to thresholds/required suites: **Monica Patel**, **Christine Wong**

Maintenance expectations:
- Every template PR that changes agent loop, retriever, retry/timeout, streaming, or observability MUST include updated/added tests.
- Nightly runs are reviewed weekly; recurring failures become tracked issues.

## Appendix A: Example eval config (conceptual)
The eval runner config should allow overriding model settings for stability:
- temperature: 0–0.2
- max_tokens: capped to avoid runaway costs
- timeouts: aligned with safe defaults

Recommended defaults for eval runs:
- `temperature=0.1`
- `max_tokens=512`
- `request_timeout_s=30`
- `max_retries=2`

## Appendix B: Required CI artifacts
Every CI run that executes Tier 2 or Tier 3 must upload:
- `evals/out/summary.json`
- `evals/out/report.md` (or HTML)
- `evals/out/results.jsonl`
- Smoke test logs (with redaction enabled)

## Change log
- 2025-03-18: Updated gating thresholds and clarified smoke test scenarios for streaming disconnects and tool schema validation.
- 2025-02-26: Added golden prompt versioning guidance and artifact requirements.
- 2025-02-11: Initial publish.
text:
## Overview
This document defines how Redwood Inference Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents, Python and TypeScript) are evaluated and regression-tested. The intent is to catch:
- Functional regressions (retrieval correctness, tool calling behavior, structured output parsing)
- Reliability regressions (streaming disconnect handling, retries/backoff, timeouts)
- Breaking changes in SDKs/dependencies that impact template users
- Observability regressions (missing spans/metrics, log redaction failures)

We implement a layered strategy:
1) **Golden prompt sets** (deterministic-ish behavioral checks)
2) **Offline eval harness integration** (repeatable scoring and trending)
3) **Smoke tests** (streaming + tools + basic RAG end-to-end)
4) **CI quality gates** (release blocking where appropriate)

## Goals
- Make template updates safe to ship without requiring maintainers to manually validate every behavior.
- Detect regressions that show up most often in support tickets: 401/429 handling, streaming disconnects, tool schema errors, empty retrieval, misconfigured env.
- Keep the test suite maintainable and fast enough for every PR.
- Ensure templates remain “production-minded by default”: safe defaults are verified continuously.

## Non-goals
- Creating a full model-quality benchmarking suite across the entire model catalog.
- Guaranteeing bit-for-bit identical outputs from probabilistic generation.
- Measuring absolute quality vs other vendors/providers (templates aim to be provider-agnostic; CI verifies our integration contracts).

## Definitions
- **Template**: the scaffolded quickstart app (Python/TS) generated via repo template or CLI.
- **Golden prompt**: a prompt + expected invariants (not necessarily exact text match).
- **Eval**: repeatable checks that produce a score and/or pass/fail, recorded over time.
- **Smoke test**: short end-to-end test intended to catch integration breakage (networking, streaming, tools) rather than deep quality.

## What we test (coverage map)
### RAG baseline
- Ingestion/chunking produces non-empty index
- Retrieval returns top-k items with correct schema
- Answer includes citations or provenance fields (template-defined output)
- Handles empty retrieval (returns “I don’t know”/fallback path, not hallucinated citations)

### Agent + tool calling baseline
- Tool schema validation errors are surfaced as typed errors (not raw exceptions)
- Tool call loop respects guardrails:
  - max iterations
  - tool output size limit
  - tool timeout
- Tool failures are logged with error taxonomy and include correlation IDs

### Streaming resilience baseline
- Streaming works end-to-end with expected event framing
- Disconnect/reconnect behavior does not duplicate tokens beyond the template’s documented guarantee
- Timeout and retry defaults follow the “Safe defaults” policy

### Observability baseline
- Required spans are emitted (see Observability Hooks Standard)
- Required metrics increment at least once during the smoke test
- Logs redact secrets/PII patterns by default

## Test assets layout (repo conventions)
Each template repo must include the following structure:

- `evals/`
  - `goldens/`
    - `rag/` (RAG prompt sets)
    - `agent/` (tool calling prompt sets)
  - `datasets/` (small, non-sensitive sample documents)
  - `configs/` (eval runner configuration)
  - `README.md` (how to run evals locally)
- `tests/`
  - `smoke/` (streaming + tool + RAG e2e)
  - `unit/` (schema validation, error mapping, retriever adapters)

**Security note:** all datasets must be synthetic or publicly redistributable and must not contain customer data.

## Golden prompt sets
Golden prompts are defined as **invariants** instead of strict output matching. We require each golden case to specify:
- Input prompt (and optional conversation history)
- Required fields in the structured response
- Constraints (e.g., must include 1–3 citations, must not claim it searched the web unless a tool was called)
- Tool behavior expectations (e.g., tool must be called when needed, must not call tool for trivial queries)

### Golden prompt format
We standardize on a JSONL format for simplicity:
- One case per line
- Minimal fields
- Portable across Python and TypeScript runners

Example (`evals/goldens/rag/basic.jsonl`):
- `id`: stable identifier
- `prompt`: user prompt
- `expected_invariants`: list of invariants to validate
- `tags`: for filtering

Example invariants we support:
- `contains_regex:<pattern>`
- `json_path_exists:answer.citations`
- `json_path_len_between:answer.citations,1,3`
- `must_not_contain_regex:searched the web`
- `tool_called:retriever.search`

### Managing flakiness
Golden prompts can become flaky due to model updates or sampling randomness. We mitigate by:
- Using low temperature by default in eval runs (unless the template’s default is higher, in which case we override in eval config)
- Validating invariants, not exact phrasing
- Versioning prompt sets (see “Versioning and updates”)

## Offline eval harness integration
Templates integrate with Redwood’s internal eval harness runner to:
- Run goldens and record pass rate by template version
- Track drift and regressions over time
- Produce CI artifacts (HTML/JSON summaries)

### Required eval outputs
Every eval run must produce:
- A machine-readable summary (`evals/out/summary.json`)
- A human-readable report (`evals/out/report.md` or HTML)
- Raw results for debugging (`evals/out/results.jsonl`)

### Score and thresholds
For the v1 template release gate:
- **Golden pass rate**: >= 95% on required suites (rag/basic, agent/tools-basic)
- **Hard fails** (0 tolerance):
  - Missing required structured fields
  - Tool schema validation exceptions uncaught
  - “Citation fabrication” invariant violated (citations present but not sourced from retriever results)

Notes:
- Thresholds are intentionally conservative to avoid blocking on minor model phrasing changes.
- Any reduction in pass rate > 2% vs main branch baseline requires review by DevEx (Monica Patel or Sabrina James).

### How to run locally
We support a single entrypoint per template:
- Python: `make eval` and `python -m evals.run --suite rag/basic`
- TypeScript: `pnpm eval` and `node ./evals/run.js --suite agent/tools-basic`

Local runs should support:
- Selecting suite(s)
- Selecting provider backend (Redwood default)
- Saving artifacts to `evals/out/`

## Smoke tests (CI-first, fast)
Smoke tests validate template behaviors most likely to break due to SDK or infra changes.

### Required smoke test scenarios
1) **Streaming happy path**
   - Start template server
   - Request streaming completion
   - Assert event stream is well-formed
   - Assert final response includes request ID and correlation metadata

2) **Streaming disconnect + retry**
   - Simulate mid-stream disconnect (client-side cancel or proxy drop)
   - Verify template reconnect strategy is invoked (where supported)
   - Verify surfaced error is typed and actionable if reconnect fails

3) **Tool calling**
   - Ask a question that requires a tool call
   - Validate tool schema is enforced
   - Validate max-iteration guard prevents infinite loops

4) **RAG end-to-end**
   - Ingest a tiny sample dataset (<= 10 docs)
   - Query and validate:
     - non-empty retrieved docs
     - citations/provenance present
     - empty-retrieval fallback works on an unrelated query

5) **Observability sanity**
   - Assert at least one trace span exported (or captured) with required attributes
   - Assert logs include `redwood.request_id` and redact configured sensitive fields

### Execution constraints
- Target runtime: <= 10 minutes total for all smoke tests per PR.
- Tests must run in a clean environment (no reliance on developer machine state).
- Avoid high-cardinality tagging in assertions (do not assert on randomly generated IDs beyond presence/format).

## CI quality gates
We define three CI tiers:

### Tier 1: PR checks (required)
- Lint + typecheck
- Unit tests
- Smoke tests (streaming + tools + RAG)
- Dependency vulnerability scan (per Secure SDLC policy)

### Tier 2: PR checks (non-blocking, informational)
- Full golden suite across all categories
- Optional matrix across supported models (nightly only by default)

### Tier 3: Release checks (required)
- Tier 1 must be green
- Golden pass rate meets threshold for required suites
- Release candidate tagged with template version and changelog updated

## Release gating policy
A template release cannot be cut unless:
- Required suites meet thresholds
- No open P0/P1 issues tagged `template-release-blocker`
- Observability hooks match the current standard (span/metric names) OR the release notes clearly call out deviations

Approval required for release:
- DevEx engineering lead (Monica Patel)
- SRE reviewer sign-off for changes that modify retry/timeout/streaming behavior (Christine Wong)

## Baselines, trending, and regression triage
### Baseline strategy
- `main` branch results are the baseline.
- For releases, we snapshot baseline artifacts and attach to the release tag.

### Regression triage checklist
When a suite fails:
1) Confirm failure is reproducible (rerun suite locally + in CI)
2) Classify:
   - Test harness issue
   - Model/provider drift
   - Template code regression
   - Dependency/SDK regression
3) Determine fix path:
   - If drift: update invariants only with DevEx approval and version bump prompt set
   - If code regression: fix template, add targeted unit test
   - If SDK regression: open SDK ticket and pin/patch as needed

### Flaky test handling
- Any flaky test observed >= 3 times in 7 days must be:
  - quarantined (moved to non-blocking) OR
  - fixed with deterministic assertions
- Quarantined tests require an owner and target date to re-enable.

## Versioning and updates
### Golden prompt set versioning
Golden sets are versioned independently from template versions:
- Directory naming: `evals/goldens/rag/v1/`, `evals/goldens/rag/v2/`
- Template release notes must state which golden version was used for gating.

### Updating goldens
Allowed reasons:
- Template behavior intentionally changes (documented in changelog)
- Observed provider/model behavior changes and invariants need to be made more robust

Not allowed:
- Relaxing invariants to hide real regressions (must include rationale + reviewer sign-off)

## Data handling and privacy
- Do not log prompts/responses in CI artifacts unless explicitly scrubbed.
- Eval runner must support redaction of:
  - API keys
  - Authorization headers
  - Email addresses
  - Phone numbers
  - Tokens that match configured secret patterns
- Keep eval datasets small and non-sensitive.

## Ownership and maintenance
- Primary owner: **Sabrina James** (Examples + template test coverage)
- Backup owner: **Dana Lo** (TypeScript template + SDK integration)
- Reviewers for changes to thresholds/required suites: **Monica Patel**, **Christine Wong**

Maintenance expectations:
- Every template PR that changes agent loop, retriever, retry/timeout, streaming, or observability MUST include updated/added tests.
- Nightly runs are reviewed weekly; recurring failures become tracked issues.

## Appendix A: Example eval config (conceptual)
The eval runner config should allow overriding model settings for stability:
- temperature: 0–0.2
- max_tokens: capped to avoid runaway costs
- timeouts: aligned with safe defaults

Recommended defaults for eval runs:
- `temperature=0.1`
- `max_tokens=512`
- `request_timeout_s=30`
- `max_retries=2`

## Appendix B: Required CI artifacts
Every CI run that executes Tier 2 or Tier 3 must upload:
- `evals/out/summary.json`
- `evals/out/report.md` (or HTML)
- `evals/out/results.jsonl`
- Smoke test logs (with redaction enabled)

## Change log
- 2025-03-18: Updated gating thresholds and clarified smoke test scenarios for streaming disconnects and tool schema validation.
- 2025-02-26: Added golden prompt versioning guidance and artifact requirements.
- 2025-02-11: Initial publish.

title: Quickstart Templates: Release Process (Versioning, Changelog, Deprecations)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood Inference ships and maintains Quickstart Templates (RAG + Agents, etc.) across languages, including:
- Versioning policy (templates + scaffolding CLI)
- Changelog expectations
- Deprecation strategy
- Step-by-step instructions for cutting a release
- How docs link to “latest” vs “pinned” template versions

The goal is to keep templates:
- Safe by default (security + privacy)
- Stable for production use (repeatable scaffolds, predictable upgrades)
- Easy to maintain (CI gates, reproducible builds)

## Scope
**In scope:**
- Template repositories under the Redwood org (e.g., `redwood-quickstarts`)
- The scaffolding CLI used to initialize templates (e.g., `redwood-templates`)
- Docs-site integration (how we link templates and recommend versions)

**Out of scope:**
- SDK release processes (tracked in SDK repos)
- Observability Pack release process (separate repo cadence)

## Ownership and roles
- **Release owner:** Connor O'Brien (Release/versioning)
- **DevEx engineering lead:** Monica Patel (release readiness, cross-repo alignment)
- **Docs owner:** Elliot Price (docs linking, launch notes, changelog surfacing)
- **CI owner:** Sahana Iyer (release gates, smoke tests, vulnerability scanning)
- **Security reviewer:** Marcus Lin (secure defaults, supply chain policy compliance)
- **Observability reviewer:** Allison Grant (OTel conventions + cardinality review)

## Repositories and artifacts
Templates are shipped as:
1) **Git tags + GitHub Releases** in the template repo.
2) **CLI-distributed scaffolds** that stamp an app with a template version.
3) **Docs pages** that reference a specific template version (pinned) or the current recommended version (latest).

### Required version stamp in generated projects
Every generated project must contain a machine-readable version stamp so Support and DevEx can quickly identify what a user is running.

**Required file:** `.redwood/template-version`
- Value: the template tag (e.g., `quickstarts/v1.4.2`) or a semver string (e.g., `1.4.2`) depending on the template family.
- Also include build metadata in logs (see Observability Standard) but do not include secrets.

## Versioning policy
We use **SemVer (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH)** for:
- Template families (e.g., `rag-agent-python`, `rag-agent-typescript`)
- The scaffolding CLI

### What constitutes a MAJOR vs MINOR vs PATCH
**MAJOR (X.0.0)**
- Breaking config changes (required env vars renamed/removed)
- Changes that break runtime behavior or API compatibility (e.g., tool schema format changes)
- Removal of an adapter/vector store option
- Breaking observability schema changes (span/metric renames) that would break dashboards/alerts

**MINOR (0.Y.0)**
- Add new optional features (new tool, optional adapter, new deployment recipe)
- New metrics/spans/attributes that do not break existing dashboards
- New docs and troubleshooting guidance paired with small code improvements

**PATCH (0.0.Z)**
- Bug fixes (streaming stability, retry correctness)
- Security fixes (dependency patch bumps, log redaction fixes)
- Documentation corrections

### Template families and tags
We tag releases at the repo level, and the release notes must enumerate which template families changed.

**Tag format (repo-level):** `quickstarts/vX.Y.Z`
- Example: `quickstarts/v1.3.0`

**Within the release notes**, call out which templates changed:
- `rag-agent-python`
- `rag-agent-typescript`
- (future) `rag-api-only-python`, etc.

### CLI versioning
The CLI is versioned independently, but it must remain compatible with:
- The most recent MINOR of the latest MAJOR template series
- At least the previous MINOR (N-1) for upgrade safety

**Compatibility rule:** A CLI release may not require a template MAJOR bump unless explicitly planned and approved by DevEx lead + Docs lead.

## Changelog expectations
Every release must have:
1) A **GitHub Release** with human-readable notes.
2) A **CHANGELOG.md** update in the repo.
3) A **docs note** (if the release changes user-facing behavior or upgrade guidance).

### CHANGELOG format
Add entries under a section for the new version.

**Template:**
- Summary (2–4 bullets)
- Added
- Changed
- Fixed
- Security
- Deprecations
- Upgrade notes (required when breaking or behavior-changing)

### Required content in release notes
Include:
- Template families impacted
- Any breaking changes and exact migration steps
- Any new/changed env vars (with defaults)
- Any observability schema changes (new metrics/spans; confirm no renames unless major)
- Any dependency upgrades with security implications (CVEs fixed)

### “Upgrade notes” bar for user-impacting changes
If the change affects a running deployment, include an explicit section:
- “Who is affected?”
- “What will break?”
- “What to change” (copy/paste-ready)
- “How to validate” (commands + expected output)

## Deprecation strategy
We deprecate features/configs intentionally, with predictable timelines.

### Deprecation policy
- **MINOR release**: may introduce deprecations (warnings), must not remove behavior.
- **Next MAJOR**: may remove deprecated behavior.

### Deprecation windows
- Default window: **90 days** from first deprecation notice to removal eligibility.
- For enterprise-facing items (common deployment recipes, major retriever adapter): **180 days**, unless a security incident requires faster action.

### How deprecations must appear
Deprecations must be communicated via:
- CHANGELOG + GitHub Release
- A runtime warning (where applicable) that is:
  - visible in logs
  - non-spammy (rate-limited)
  - includes a link to migration docs

### Example deprecation notice (log)
Message must include:
- Deprecated item name
- Removal target version
- Migration link

Example:
`DEPRECATION: VECTOR_STORE=faiss-legacy will be removed in v2.0.0. Migrate to VECTOR_STORE=local. Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/templates/rag-agent#vector-store`

## Release cadence
- Target cadence: **biweekly** patch/minor releases while templates are in active launch iteration.
- Major releases: **as-needed**, explicitly planned (requires PM + Security + Docs alignment).

We may cut out-of-band PATCH releases for:
- security fixes
- broken onboarding paths
- severe reliability regressions (streaming disconnects, retry storms)

## Release gates (must-pass checks)
A release cannot be cut unless the following are green on the release candidate commit:

1) **Unit + integration tests** (language-specific)
2) **Streaming stability smoke tests**
3) **Tool-calling schema validation tests**
4) **Eval harness hooks**: golden prompt set runs (baseline regression check)
5) **Security checks**:
   - dependency pinning is intact
   - vulnerability scan passes or has explicitly approved exceptions
   - secrets scanning passes
6) **Observability checks**:
   - spans/metrics emitted match the Observability Standard
   - no high-cardinality attributes added (or explicitly approved)
7) **Docs check** (if required): docs PR ready/merged per below

## How to cut a release (step-by-step)
### 0) Decide release type and scope
- Patch: bug/security fix only
- Minor: new optional capabilities, new adapters/recipes, non-breaking improvements
- Major: breaking changes; requires explicit sign-off (DevEx lead + Docs lead + Security)

### 1) Create a release branch
- Branch name: `release/quickstarts-vX.Y.Z`
- Branch from `main`.

### 2) Update versions and stamps
- Update `CHANGELOG.md`
- Ensure generated project stamp logic writes `.redwood/template-version`
- If CLI is involved, ensure CLI metadata references the target template tag (or compatibility logic is updated)

### 3) Run full CI and required local verification
At minimum (release owner responsibility):
- Run template generation once per language and confirm:
  - `init` succeeds
  - `doctor` passes with a local OTel collector
  - a basic run produces traces/metrics/logs

### 4) Security and observability review checkpoints
- Security: Marcus Lin must approve if dependencies changed, logging changed, or secrets-handling changed.
- Observability: Allison Grant must approve if metrics/spans changed.

### 5) Merge docs updates (when required)
Docs are required if:
- Any env var schema changes
- Any troubleshooting changes needed
- Any user-facing behavior change
- Any deprecation introduced

Docs PR should:
- reference the template tag
- include “Pinned version” links (see below)
- update troubleshooting entries if needed

### 6) Tag the release
On the release branch tip commit:
- Create tag: `quickstarts/vX.Y.Z`
- Push tag to origin

Example:
- `git tag quickstarts/v1.4.2`
- `git push origin quickstarts/v1.4.2`

### 7) Create GitHub Release
The GitHub Release must include:
- Summary + Added/Changed/Fixed/Security/Deprecations
- Upgrade notes (if applicable)
- Link to docs
- Known issues (if any) and workarounds

### 8) Publish/verify CLI compatibility
If the CLI needs an update:
- cut CLI release after the template tag is live
- verify CLI can target:
  - `--template-version quickstarts/vX.Y.Z`
  - default “recommended” version points to a stable pinned tag (not an untagged commit)

### 9) Announce and monitor
- Post in internal #announcements and #devex with:
  - version
  - highlights
  - any known issues
  - support notes
- Monitor:
  - template-tagged traffic (Console filter)
  - error rate changes (network/auth/quota/tool)
  - streaming disconnect patterns

## Hotfix process
Use a hotfix when an already-released tag is causing widespread breakage.

Policy:
- Do not rewrite existing tags.
- Cut a new PATCH release (e.g., `v1.4.2` → `v1.4.3`).

Steps:
- Branch from the tag: `hotfix/quickstarts-vX.Y.(Z+1)`
- Minimal changes only
- Repeat release gates
- Publish new tag + Release notes clearly labeled “Hotfix”

## Docs linking: “Latest” vs “Pinned”
Docs must support two user modes:
- **Latest recommended**: most users should follow this.
- **Pinned version**: platform teams and regulated customers need repeatability.

### Definitions
- **Latest recommended:** A docs page that points to the current recommended stable tag (updated as we release).
- **Pinned:** A versioned docs reference that points to a specific template tag.

### Linking rules
1) Docs must always show the recommended pinned tag near the top of the page.
2) The default docs flow should not require users to choose a version, but must expose an explicit pin option.
3) “Copy/paste” commands must include pinned versions when feasible.

### Required docs snippet (example)
Include a short block like:
- Recommended: `quickstarts/v1.4.2`
- Pinned init command: `redwood-templates init rag-agent-python --template-version quickstarts/v1.4.2`
- Latest init command: `redwood-templates init rag-agent-python` (with a note that it will resolve to the current recommended stable version)

### When docs must be pinned vs latest
- Security/compliance guidance: always reference a pinned version (so instructions match behavior).
- Troubleshooting pages: can reference latest but must mention that symptoms may vary by version and how to check `.redwood/template-version`.

## Backward compatibility and support expectations
### Supported versions
Support and DevEx will treat the following as supported:
- The latest release (N)
- The previous MINOR (N-1) within the same MAJOR

Example: if latest is `1.4.2`, we support `1.4.x` and `1.3.x`.

### End-of-support communications
If we drop support for a MINOR train:
- announce in release notes
- add a docs note on the template page
- ensure the CLI defaults no longer resolve to an unsupported series

## Checklist (release owner)
Before tagging:
- [ ] CHANGELOG updated
- [ ] Version stamp `.redwood/template-version` verified
- [ ] CI gates green
- [ ] Security scan passed / exceptions documented
- [ ] Observability review complete
- [ ] Docs PR merged (if required)
- [ ] Release notes drafted

After tagging:
- [ ] GitHub Release published
- [ ] CLI compatibility verified
- [ ] Announcement posted
- [ ] Post-release monitoring started (first 24 hours)

## Appendix: Common pitfalls
- Cutting a release before docs land (leads to drift and support load)
- Adding new metric dimensions that explode cardinality (breaks dashboards and increases cost)
- Shipping “helpful” logs that accidentally include prompts/PII (violates safe defaults)
- Failing to stamp the template version in generated apps (blocks debugging)

## Change history
- 2025-03-18: Clarified docs “latest vs pinned” rules; added supported versions policy.
- 2025-02-21: Added release gates for streaming/tool-calling smoke tests and security scanning.
- 2025-01-29: Initial publication.
